name: Home
title: Home
description: Landing page for discovering engraving products by category, browsing featured and popular items, and starting key flows such as product search, Gift Finder, store pickup, bulk orders, and custom projects.

core_features: Display primary navigation categories retrieved from getPrimaryNavCategories and expose direct links into category listing pages.
Provide a global product search form that routes to the search results page and can optionally show live preview results using searchProducts.
Show a featured products section powered by getFeaturedProducts, with each product card linking to its product detail page.
Show a popular products section powered by getPopularProducts, with each product card linking to its product detail page.
Expose clear entry points into Gift Finder, Corporate & Bulk Orders, Store Pickup, and Custom Projects via navigational tiles/buttons.
user_workflows: Browse by category: (1) User lands on Home. (2) Page calls getPrimaryNavCategories with no parameters. (3) User sees a list of primary categories. (4) User clicks a category link (for example a Kitchen, Drinkware, or Weddings category). (5) Browser navigates to category.html with URL parameter categoryId set to the selected category’s id.

Search for products: (1) User types a query into the global search input. (2) As the user types, the component may call searchProducts with the current query and suitable paging parameters to show a small set of preview results. (3) User either clicks one of the preview products, navigating to product.html?id={product_id}, or submits the form. (4) On submit, the page navigates to search.html?query={encoded_query} without needing additional API calls on Home.

View featured products: (1) On initial load, the Home page calls getFeaturedProducts with a configured limit and optional categoryId filter (if present in URL state). (2) The featured products section renders a list/grid of cards bound to the returned data. (3) User clicks a product card. (4) Browser navigates to product.html?id={product_id}.

View popular products: (1) On initial load, the Home page calls getPopularProducts with a configured limit and optional categoryId filter (if present in URL state). (2) The popular products section renders a list/grid of cards. (3) User clicks a product card. (4) Browser navigates to product.html?id={product_id}.
Access Gift Finder: (1) User sees a Gift Finder entry tile on Home. (2) User clicks the tile. (3) Browser navigates to gift_finder.html.
Access Corporate & Bulk Orders: (1) User sees a Corporate & Bulk Orders entry tile on Home. (2) User clicks the tile. (3) Browser navigates to bulk_orders.html.
Access Store Pickup / Same-Day Pickup: (1) User sees a Store Pickup entry tile on Home. (2) User clicks the tile. (3) Browser navigates to store_pickup.html.
Access Custom Projects & Consultation: (1) User sees a Custom Projects entry tile on Home. (2) User clicks the tile. (3) Browser navigates to custom_projects.html.
interactions: Typing in the global search field updates the search input value; optional debounced calls to searchProducts(query, filters, sort_by, page, page_size) can populate a live preview dropdown.
Submitting the search form triggers a navigation to search.html with the query URL parameter set from the input value.
Clicking a category link in the primary navigation or category shortcuts triggers navigation to category.html?categoryId={category_id}.
Clicking a featured product card navigates to product.html?id={product_id} using the identifier from getFeaturedProducts.
Clicking a popular product card navigates to product.html?id={product_id} using the identifier from getPopularProducts.
Clicking the Gift Finder tile navigates to gift_finder.html.
Clicking the Corporate & Bulk Orders tile navigates to bulk_orders.html.
Clicking the Store Pickup tile navigates to store_pickup.html.
Clicking the Custom Projects tile navigates to custom_projects.html.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Home page does not require URL parameters but can interpret optional ones to control its internal state. An optional query parameter q can prefill the global search input and, if present, may trigger an initial searchProducts call to show preview results on load; submitting the search still navigates to search.html?query={q}. Optional parameters featuredCategoryId and popularCategoryId can be used to request category-specific lists from getFeaturedProducts and getPopularProducts, respectively; if these parameters are present, the page passes them as categoryId to those interfaces, otherwise categoryId is omitted. An optional section parameter can be used to control which section is scrolled into view on load (e.g., section=popular or section=featured); this affects only scroll/focus behavior and not which interfaces are called. All other state (such as which product card is hovered or which live search preview item is highlighted) is maintained in memory during the session and is not persisted to URL or localStorage.

components.functionality: Provides a global product search input, optional live search preview, and routes to the dedicated search results page on submit.
event_handlers: onInputChange: when the user types, optionally call searchProducts with the current query, default filters, sort_by, and first page to retrieve a small list of matching products for preview; update an internal previewResults state.
onPreviewResultClick: when the user clicks a preview product result, navigate to product.html?id={product_id} where product_id comes from the searchProducts response.
onSubmit: prevent default form submission, read the input value, and navigate to search.html?query={encoded_query}.

components.functionality: Fetches and displays primary navigation categories, allowing users to jump directly to a category listing page.
event_handlers: onInit: call getPrimaryNavCategories with no parameters; store the resulting categories in component state.
onCategoryClick: when a category item is clicked, navigate to category.html?categoryId={category_id} using the id from the selected Category record.

components.functionality: Uses the same category data as the primary navigation to render prominent shortcuts to key shopping areas on the Home page.
event_handlers: onInit: reuse the data loaded by primary-navigation-categories or call getPrimaryNavCategories if not yet loaded; filter or order categories in-memory based on properties such as is_primary_nav and sort_order.
onShortcutClick: when a shortcut item is clicked, navigate to category.html?categoryId={category_id} using the selected category’s id.

components.functionality: Displays a subset of featured products on the Home page, optionally filtered by category based on URL state.
event_handlers: onInit: read the optional featuredCategoryId URL parameter; call getFeaturedProducts with a configured limit and, if present, categoryId=featuredCategoryId; bind the returned products array to the grid.

onCategoryFilterChange: if the section exposes an internal category filter (e.g., via a dropdown populated by Category data), update the URL parameter featuredCategoryId and re-call getFeaturedProducts with the new categoryId.
onProductClick: when a product card is clicked, navigate to product.html?id={product_id} using the product_id from the getFeaturedProducts result.

components.functionality: Displays products with high popularity on the Home page, optionally filtered by category based on URL state.
event_handlers: onInit: read the optional popularCategoryId URL parameter; call getPopularProducts with a configured limit and, if present, categoryId=popularCategoryId; bind the returned products array to the grid.
onCategoryFilterChange: if the section exposes an internal category selector, update the URL parameter popularCategoryId and re-call getPopularProducts with the new categoryId.
onProductClick: when a product card is clicked, navigate to product.html?id={product_id} using the product_id from the getPopularProducts result.

components.functionality: Shows a small list of product results under the global search input as the user types, using searchProducts.
event_handlers: onSearchQueryChange: debounced handler invoked by global-search-form when the query changes; call searchProducts with the current query, optional filters and sort_by, and a small page_size; update component state with the returned products for display.
onPreviewItemClick: when a result in the preview list is clicked, navigate to product.html?id={product_id}.
onClearPreview: when the search input is cleared or loses focus, clear the preview results from state.

components.functionality: Exposes a clear entry point from Home into the Gift Finder flow.
Exposes a clear entry point from Home into the Corporate & Bulk Orders flow.
Exposes a clear entry point from Home into the Store Pickup / Same-Day Pickup flow.
Exposes a clear entry point from Home into the Custom Projects & Consultation flow.
Controls initial scroll position and focus on the Home page sections based on URL parameters, without additional data fetching.
event_handlers: onInit: read the optional section URL parameter; if it matches a known section identifier (e.g., 'featured' or 'popular'), programmatically scroll that section’s DOM anchor into view and set focus for accessibility.
onSectionLinkClick: when in-page navigation links are used to jump to specific sections, update the section URL parameter so that reloading the page restores the same section.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with three equal-width content columns (each roughly 4 columns wide) for the product cards. Content is centered within wide horizontal margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a top-to-bottom, centered content arrangement on a full-bleed dark background. A full-width top navigation bar contains the primary navigation categories and the global search. Directly below, a hero-like band hosts three large category shortcut cards (homepage-category-shortcuts) in a single row, mirroring the mockup’s 3-column card layout. Below the hero, a compact 2x2 grid of navigation tiles (Gift Finder, Bulk Orders, Store Pickup, Custom Projects) provides quick-entry flows. Under that, the page transitions into two product grid sections (featured then popular), each using a three-column layout and aligned to the same central max-width. The search preview results appear as an overlaying panel beneath the nav/search when active. A section anchor controller floats along the right edge of the content band as a subtle utility.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a top-to-bottom, centered content arrangement on a full-bleed dark background. A full-width top navigation bar contains the primary navigation categories and the global search. Directly below, a hero-like band hosts three large category shortcut cards (homepage-category-shortcuts) in a single row, mirroring the mockup’s 3-column card layout. Below the hero, a compact 2x2 grid of navigation tiles (Gift Finder, Bulk Orders, Store Pickup, Custom Projects) provides quick-entry flows. Under that, the page transitions into two product grid sections (featured then popular), each using a three-column layout and aligned to the same central max-width. The search preview results appear as an overlaying panel beneath the nav/search when active. A section anchor controller floats along the right edge of the content band as a subtle utility.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The navigation bar occupies a shallow, full-width horizontal strip at the top (~64px–72px tall). The hero band below it gets generous vertical space, with each hero card devoting roughly 65–70% of its height to photography and 30–35% to the overlapping white text panel, matching the mockup. Horizontally, all main content (hero cards, tiles, product grids) is centered and constrained to a max-width of about 1120–1280px, consuming roughly 70–80% of the viewport width on desktop with dark margins framing it. Vertical gaps between major sections (hero → utility tiles → featured → popular) use 48–64px spacing, while gaps between rows of cards or tiles use around 24–32px, following the established spacing scale.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density remains moderately spacious: the nav bar is compact but clearly separated items; hero cards are large and visually rich but surrounded by ample side margins and consistent gutters; product grids show three items per row but maintain comfortable padding inside each card and consistent spacing between rows. Utility tiles are smaller but still have sufficient internal padding and breathing room, avoiding a cluttered feel. The search preview is concise and layered over existing content with soft shadows and clear separation, avoiding overwhelming the page.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup’s vertical-then-horizontal pattern. The eye enters at the top-left of the nav (first category), sweeps right across the navigation to the search box and any highlighted category. From there, the gaze drops down into the hero band where attention scans left-to-right across the three large category cards. Next, the eye moves slightly downward to the utility tiles grid, then down again to the Featured Products section, then Popular Products, reading each row left-to-right. When the search is active, the search preview results intercept attention directly below the nav, before the hero content, while the section anchor controller provides a subtle right-edge reference for jumping between main sections as the user scrolls.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a nearly-black, full-bleed background with subtle texture, giving a modern workshop feel. A shallow, full-width dark navigation bar anchors the top, with the logo/primary categories aligned to the left and a prominent search bar integrated toward the right, all accented with muted gold highlights. Below, the main content is centered within the viewport, constrained to a broad but not edge-to-edge max-width, leaving generous dark side margins that frame the experience. The first band is a hero row of three tall category cards, each showing a rich product or material photograph, with a crisp white text panel overlapping the lower third of the image and carrying bold italic headings and short descriptions. Beneath this, a compact grid of four navigation tiles gives quick access to Gift Finder, Bulk Orders, Store Pickup, and Custom Projects, each appearing like smaller, refined cards on the same dark background. Scrolling down, the layout transitions into a three-column Featured Products grid, then another three-column Popular Products grid, both mirroring the hero’s card proportions but in a slightly smaller scale, maintaining consistent gutters and vertical rhythm. When the user types into the search bar, a layered panel drops down below the nav, centered over the content width, showing a three-column preview of matching products and softly dimming the hero beneath. To the far right of the content band, a slender anchor controller floats as you scroll, offering quick jumps to Featured, Popular, and other sections without intruding. Overall, the layout feels linear and intentional, with strong vertical stacking and left-to-right scanning of cards, framed by an artisan, dark-and-gold aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global search form is embedded in the top navigation bar on the right half, visually prominent but balanced with the primary navigation categories. Horizontally, it begins roughly at the center of the page and extends toward the right, spanning about 35–40% of the viewport width on desktop. Vertically, it sits centered within the nav bar’s height (around 64–72px tall), with 16px–24px of internal padding inside the input field itself. The search input forms a long pill or rounded rectangle with a subtle gold outline or accent on focus, contrasting against the dark nav background. There is 16px–24px horizontal spacing between the last navigation item and the left edge of the search form, preserving a clean separation. The search icon is positioned inside the input on the right, aligned vertically at the center. When the search is focused, the search-preview-results panel appears directly below this bar, aligned so that the preview’s left edge matches the left edge of the search form and its right edge aligns with the content container’s right edge, creating a clear vertical alignment from nav to content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed within the top navigation bar, which is sticky to the top of the viewport on scroll. When active, it triggers the search-preview-results panel to overlay the top of the main content area while keeping the nav itself in place.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The primary navigation categories form a horizontal list aligned to the left side of the top navigation bar. Starting below the logo (if present) or flush with the left content margin, the categories occupy roughly the left 40–50% of the nav bar’s width. Each category item is separated by 24px–32px of horizontal spacing and has 12px–16px horizontal padding within its hit area, following the moderately spacious pattern. The active category is distinguished with a gold underline that spans the width of the text, visually connecting to the gold accents used elsewhere in the design. Vertically, the nav items are centered within the bar, maintaining consistent top and bottom padding (around 16px total). The nav bar itself spans the full viewport width and uses a dark background that blends seamlessly into the dark page background but with a slightly more solid tone. The rightmost nav item sits 24px–32px to the left of the global-search-form, emphasizing separation between navigation and search while still aligning them on a shared baseline.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed within a sticky top navigation bar that remains locked to the top of the viewport as users scroll down through the hero, tiles, and product sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The homepage-category-shortcuts appear immediately below the navigation bar as the hero content band, structured as a single row of three tall cards centered on the page. The entire row sits within a max-width content container (approximately 1120–1280px wide) that is horizontally centered, leaving broad dark margins on both sides (about 10–15% of viewport width each on large screens). Each card takes up one third of this container, with equal widths and consistent 24px–32px gutters between them. Vertically, each card is tall—roughly a 3:4 width-to-height ratio—with about 65–70% of its height dedicated to a rich photographic image on the top. The lower 30–35% is a white or very light panel that overlaps the bottom of the image, creating the signature layered look from the mockup. This text panel contains a bold italic heading at the top and a smaller paragraph beneath, with around 16px padding on all sides. The cards are positioned so that the overlapping white panel appears to sit partly over the photo and partly over the dark background, giving a slightly elevated, crafted feel. The entire hero row is separated from the navigation above by about 48px of vertical space and from the next section (navigation tiles) below by another 48–64px, preserving a strong, airy hero presence.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main flow, forming the primary hero band of the homepage. Cards resize responsively but maintain a three-column layout on wider screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured-products-section sits below the navigation tiles band, forming the first major product grid section. It begins with a section header (e.g., “Featured Engravings”) aligned to the left of the central content container, with the header baseline aligned to the left edge of the hero cards above, preserving strong vertical alignment. Below the header, a three-column product grid spans the same max-width container used by the hero section, centered on the page. Each product card occupies roughly one third of the container width, with 24px–32px horizontal gutters between cards. The cards echo the hero card structure but at a slightly smaller scale: around 60–65% of the card’s height is occupied by the product photo, with a lighter (not necessarily pure white, but bright) text panel or content area below taking 35–40% of the height. This lower section houses the product name, short description, and key actions, with 16px internal padding. Multiple rows of three cards stack vertically, with 24px–32px vertical spacing between rows. The entire section is separated from the tiles above and the popular-products-section below by 48–64px of vertical whitespace, maintaining the moderate yet open density.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, flowing in the normal document order. It locks into the same central grid as the hero content, maintaining visual continuity of the three-column pattern.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The popular-products-section is placed immediately after the featured-products-section, creating a continuous vertical flow of product exploration. It mirrors the layout of the featured section: a left-aligned section heading within the central content container followed by a three-column product grid that spans the same width. Product cards have the same approximate proportions and structure as in the featured section—top-heavy images occupying about 60–65% of the card height with a lower text panel taking the remaining 35–40%, padded by 16px. The horizontal gutters between cards remain 24px–32px, and vertical spacing between rows of cards is consistent with the featured section. The section is separated from the featured-products-section above by roughly 48px of vertical space (so they feel connected but not visually fused) and from the page footer or any subsequent sections by 64px at the bottom, allowing the grid to breathe and maintain the mockup’s spacious, crafted feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main flow, occupying the same central three-column grid and reinforcing the page’s primary visual rhythm.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search-preview-results panel appears conditionally when the user interacts with the global search form. It is positioned directly below the sticky navigation bar, overlaying the top portion of the main content (starting over the hero category cards). Horizontally, the panel is aligned with the main content container rather than the full viewport: its left edge aligns with the left edge of the hero/product grid, and its right edge aligns with the right edge of the global-search-form or content container, effectively spanning about 70–80% of the viewport width and remaining centered. Vertically, the panel sits flush against the bottom of the nav bar with no gap, and extends downward enough to display one to two rows of product cards or compact list items (roughly 280–480px tall), depending on design decisions. Inside, the preview uses a condensed three-column grid of small product cards or tiles, each significantly shorter than the main grid cards but still using the imagery-plus-text pattern, with tight vertical spacing (16px–24px) between rows to fit more results. A subtle dark overlay (or simply a strong drop shadow and slightly darker background tint) distinguishes the preview panel from the main content beneath without fully obscuring the hero. The panel’s bottom edge leaves at least 32px–48px of visible hero content on typical desktop heights, so the user sees it as a layered surface rather than a full-page takeover.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Absolutely positioned relative to the viewport (or fixed to the top content area) immediately under the sticky nav. It overlays the main content while active and scrolls away with the page when closed, without affecting the underlying layout flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The gift-finder-entry is one of four navigation tiles that form a compact grid directly beneath the hero category cards. This tile occupies the top-left slot in a 2x2 grid centered within the same max-width content container as the hero and product grids. Each tile spans approximately half the container width (about 6 of the 12 implicit columns), with a 24px–32px gutter between the left and right tiles. Vertically, the tiles in each row share equal height, slightly shorter than the hero cards—around 40–50% of the hero card height—to keep this band lighter and more utility-oriented. The Gift Finder tile may feature a small illustrative icon or image at the top, with a light panel or card surface on the dark background, and text (title + short tagline) below, all padded by 16px–24px. There is 24px–32px vertical spacing between the hero row above and this tile grid, and another 32px–48px gap below the tiles before the featured-products-section begins, maintaining clear separation of sections while preserving visual continuity.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main flow, locked to the central container. It shares a row with the bulk-orders-entry tile and aligns with the same left margin as the hero cards and product grids.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bulk-orders-entry tile sits to the right of the gift-finder-entry within the same 2x2 navigation tile grid beneath the hero. It occupies the top-right half of the central content container, mirroring the gift finder tile’s width and height. The left edge of the bulk orders tile is separated from the gift finder tile by a consistent 24px–32px gutter. Its content arrangement mirrors that of the gift finder tile: a simple icon or small photographic element aligned toward the top, with a brief heading (e.g., “Bulk Orders”) and a short explanatory line beneath, all inside a lighter, card-like surface that stands out against the dark background. Internal padding of 16px–24px ensures readability and a crafted feel. The top of the bulk orders tile aligns horizontally with the top of the gift finder tile, and its right edge aligns with the right edge of the central content container, keeping the grid visually balanced.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and grid-aligned, forming the right tile of the top row in the 2x2 navigation tile band under the hero.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The store-pickup-entry tile is positioned in the bottom-left slot of the same 2x2 grid of navigation tiles below the hero cards. It sits directly beneath the gift-finder-entry tile, with a vertical gap of 24px–32px between them. The store pickup tile shares the same width (about half the content container) and height as its tile siblings, maintaining a cohesive band. Its internal layout uses a similar pattern: a small icon or inset photo representing pickup, with a concise title and supporting copy below, all on a lighter card surface that contrasts with the dark background. Padding of 16px–24px is used on all sides. The left edge aligns with the left edge of the gift finder tile above and the hero/product grid columns, continuing the strong vertical alignment across sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, occupying the lower-left position in the 2x2 tile layout, aligned to the same central grid as the other tiles and main content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The custom-projects-entry tile occupies the bottom-right slot of the 2x2 navigation tile grid directly beneath the hero section. It sits to the right of the store-pickup-entry and directly below the bulk-orders-entry, with consistent 24px–32px gutters between adjacent tiles both horizontally and vertically. Its width and height match the other three tiles, completing the square grid and preserving visual symmetry. The tile likely emphasizes custom work with a small visual cue (e.g., a stylized tool or custom piece) at the top, followed by a title like “Custom Projects” and a brief explanatory line. The tile’s surface uses the same lighter card treatment as the other utility tiles, with 16px–24px internal padding. The right edge aligns with the right edge of the bulk-orders-entry and the central content container, tying the band to the same grid as the surrounding sections. The entire tile grid sits approximately 48px below the hero and 48px above the featured-products-section, forming a clearly defined band of utility actions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, completing the 2x2 tile grid in the main flow and sharing the same central alignment as other major content sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section-anchor-controller appears as a slim, vertical control aligned near the right edge of the main content area, providing quick links to key sections (e.g., Top, Featured, Popular). On desktop, it is positioned so that its vertical center roughly aligns with the mid-height of the viewport when the user is in the middle of the page. Horizontally, the controller is placed just outside the main content container—about 16px–24px to the right of the content’s right edge—still on the dark background, ensuring it does not interfere with the grid of cards. It might appear as a vertical pill or a column of small circular buttons with subtle gold hover states. The controller is visible starting slightly below the hero section, once the user has begun to scroll, and remains visible as the user moves past the utility tiles and through the product sections. Its height is compact, occupying only a small portion of the right side so as not to compete with primary content. When a section is active in view, the corresponding anchor item is highlighted with a gold accent to mirror the nav bar’s active state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky or fixed relative to the viewport on desktop once the user scrolls past the hero band, staying at a consistent vertical position near the right edge of the content area. On smaller screens it can dock at the bottom or become collapsible, but it does not alter the primary content flow.

name: Category Listing
title: Category Products
description: Category listing page that shows products for a selected category with filters, sorting, pagination, and quick navigation to product detail pages.

core_features: Load and display products for the category specified by the categoryId URL parameter using getCategoryProducts.
Load available filter options for the current category using getCategoryFilterOptions and render dynamic filters (material, size, capacity, product type, style/theme, rating, price).
Apply filters and sorting based on URL parameters and re-request products when parameters change.
Provide sorting controls (price ascending, price descending, most popular, rating) mapped to the sort_by URL parameter.
Provide pagination controls using the page URL parameter and total_count/page_size from getCategoryProducts.
Display a list/grid of product cards with key information and navigate to product detail when a card is clicked.
Optionally display a small strip of popular products for the current category using getPopularProducts.
user_workflows: Browse a category from site navigation: 1) User clicks a category link in the header that navigates to category.html with categoryId set. 2) Page reads categoryId (and any other URL parameters). 3) Page calls getCategoryFilterOptions(categoryId) to fetch filter metadata. 4) Page derives the active sort_by value (URL parameter or default_sort from the filter options). 5) Page calls getCategoryProducts with categoryId, active filters derived from URL, sort_by, and page (defaulting if missing). 6) Product grid renders products and pagination using the API response.

Filter products within a category: 1) User changes a filter in the filter sidebar (e.g., material, size, rating, price range). 2) Filter component constructs a new URL with updated parameters for the changed filters, removing parameters for filters that are cleared, and setting page=1. 3) Browser navigates to category.html with the updated query string. 4) Page reloads, re-runs getCategoryFilterOptions and getCategoryProducts using the new URL parameters, and shows the updated product list.

Change sorting of products: 1) User selects a different sort option from the sort control. 2) Sort control updates the sort_by parameter in the URL and resets page=1. 3) Browser navigates to the new URL. 4) Page calls getCategoryProducts with the new sort_by value and existing filter parameters from the URL and re-renders the grid.

Paginate through results: 1) User clicks a pagination control (next, previous, or specific page number). 2) Pagination component updates the page parameter in the URL while leaving other parameters unchanged. 3) Browser navigates to category.html with the new page value. 4) Page calls getCategoryProducts with the new page and re-renders the products.

View product details: 1) User clicks on a product card in the grid or popular products strip. 2) Product card component reads the product_id from the clicked item and navigates to product.html?id=product_id. 3) Product detail page loads based on the id parameter.

Clear filters: 1) User clicks an option to clear a specific filter (e.g., material) or all filters. 2) Applied filters component removes the corresponding parameters (or all filter-related parameters) from the URL while preserving categoryId and sort_by where appropriate, and sets page=1. 3) Browser navigates to the updated URL. 4) Page reloads and calls getCategoryProducts with only the remaining parameters.
interactions: Selecting a filter value (material, size, capacity, product type, style/theme, rating): triggers URL update for the corresponding parameter and a navigation to category.html with page reset to 1.
Typing or adjusting price range (min/max): updates price_min and/or price_max in the URL when the user submits or applies the price filter and navigates to category.html with page reset to 1.
Changing sort option: updates sort_by in the URL, resets page=1, and navigates to the same page for refreshed results.
Clicking pagination controls: updates the page parameter in the URL and navigates to the corresponding results page.
Clicking an applied-filter chip or clear button: removes that filter’s URL parameter (or all filter parameters) and reloads results.
Clicking a product card: navigates to product.html?id={product_id} using the product_id from getCategoryProducts or getPopularProducts.
Loading the page with different URL parameters: automatically updates the active filters, sort, and pagination state from the query string and uses them for API calls.
page_functionality.state_logic: The authoritative state for this page is stored in the URL query parameters, not in localStorage. The categoryId parameter is required; the page only runs its main data-loading logic when categoryId is present. On initial load or navigation: 1) The page parses categoryId, material, size, capacity_oz, product_type, style_theme, rating_min, price_min, price_max, sort_by, and page from the URL. 2) It calls getCategoryFilterOptions(categoryId) to get available filters and a default_sort value. 3) If sort_by is missing from the URL, the page uses the default_sort returned by getCategoryFilterOptions for subsequent product loading; if sort_by is present, it takes precedence. 4) The active page number is taken from the page parameter; if missing or invalid, the page logic treats it as page 1 but does not hardcode any data values beyond that default. 5) A filters object is built dynamically from URL parameters that are present and non-empty; parameters that are absent or cleared are omitted from the filters object passed to getCategoryProducts. 6) The page calls getCategoryProducts(categoryId, filters, sort_by, page, page_size), where page_size is determined by page logic and not hardcoded to any particular value in the functional design. 7) Pagination controls are driven entirely by total_count, page, and page_size returned from getCategoryProducts; they update only the page parameter in the URL. 8) Filter and sort controls never update state directly in memory; instead, they construct new URLs with updated query parameters, causing a full or client-side navigation that re-runs the initialization flow. 9) If any filter options returned by getCategoryFilterOptions no longer correspond to the current products (for example, because the catalog changes), the page respects the API response and only renders filters that exist in the API output. 10) If total_count is zero for the given filters, the product grid displays an empty state message, and pagination controls are hidden or disabled; the URL parameters remain unchanged until the user modifies them.

components.functionality: Initializes the category listing page by reading URL parameters, loading filter options and products, and computing derived state (active filters, sort, page). Orchestrates calls to getCategoryFilterOptions and getCategoryProducts, and exposes the results to child components.

Displays dynamic filter controls (material, size, capacity, product type, style/theme, rating, price range) using data from getCategoryFilterOptions and current URL parameters. When the user changes filter selections, it updates the URL parameters accordingly and navigates, resetting page=1.

Summarizes currently active filters based on URL parameters and filter metadata (e.g., shows chips for selected material, size, rating, price range). Allows removing individual filters or clearing all filters, which updates the URL parameters and resets page=1.

Displays sorting options and highlights the currently active sort option based on sort_by from the URL or the default_sort value returned by getCategoryFilterOptions. When the sort option changes, it updates sort_by in the URL, resets page=1, and triggers navigation.

Renders a grid or list of product cards using the products array from getCategoryProducts. Each card shows key product data and click actions navigate to product.html?id={product_id}. Handles the empty state when total_count is zero.

Represents an individual product within the product grid or popular products strip. Displays product name, image, pricing information, rating, and any key badges (e.g., eligibility flags from the product data). Clicking the card navigates to the product detail page using the product_id.

Displays pagination controls based on total_count, page, and page_size returned by getCategoryProducts. Clicking a page number or next/previous updates only the page parameter in the URL and navigates to reload products.
Shows a horizontal list of popular products within the current category using getPopularProducts with categoryId. Allows quick navigation to product detail by clicking product cards.

Displays the current category name and an optional description based on categoryId and category information provided through getCategoryProducts (category_name) or other category metadata passed into the page. Does not perform API calls itself but consumes data initialized by the category-context-loader.
Displays a message when total_count from getCategoryProducts is zero, optionally suggesting that the user adjust or clear some filters. Appears conditionally and does not modify state directly.

Provides simple breadcrumb links that include the current category context and allow navigation back to higher-level pages or other category listings. When a breadcrumb item representing another category is clicked, it navigates to category.html with categoryId set and clears filter parameters unless explicitly preserved.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with three equal-width content columns (each roughly 4 columns wide) for the product cards. Content is centered within wide horizontal margins.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing full-width dark navigation bar, all category content sits in a centered, max-width band that echoes the mockup’s three-card hero section. The top of this band holds breadcrumb navigation and a prominent category heading, followed by a horizontal control strip containing applied filters and sort controls. Beneath this, the page splits into a two-column layout: a narrower filter sidebar on the left and a wider product grid on the right. Pagination appears below the grid, and a popular-products strip anchors the bottom of the content band as a horizontal recommendation row.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing full-width dark navigation bar, all category content sits in a centered, max-width band that echoes the mockup’s three-card hero section. The top of this band holds breadcrumb navigation and a prominent category heading, followed by a horizontal control strip containing applied filters and sort controls. Beneath this, the page splits into a two-column layout: a narrower filter sidebar on the left and a wider product grid on the right. Pagination appears below the grid, and a popular-products strip anchors the bottom of the content band as a horizontal recommendation row.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The central content band spans roughly 70–80% of the viewport width, leaving generous dark margins on both sides. Vertically, the header context (breadcrumb + heading + controls) takes about 20–25% of the above-the-fold height, the filter + product grid section takes the bulk (around 55–65%), and pagination plus recommendations use the remaining 10–20%. Within the grid, each product card occupies one of three equal-width columns (approx. 4 grid columns each), mirroring the mockup’s three-card row. The filter sidebar uses about 3 of 12 columns (roughly 25% of the content band), with the product grid taking the remaining 9 columns (75%).
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is moderately spacious: clear breathing room around each major section, consistent gutters between cards, and comfortable padding inside panels. Vertical gaps between major sections (context header, controls row, grid, pagination, recommendations) follow the 48–64px range, while spacing between UI elements within a section (filter chips, sort dropdown, cards) stays around 16–24px. Nothing feels crammed; the dark background and white/light panels create clear separation while retaining an artisan, crafted feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow starts top-left at the breadcrumb, then drops slightly to the bold category heading, establishing context first. The eye then moves horizontally across the applied filters bar and over to the sort control on the right. From there, the user scans down into the main content area: first noticing the vertical filter sidebar on the left, then naturally moving right into the three-column product grid. Within each product card, attention goes to the large product photo before settling on the overlapping white text panel. The flow ends at the pagination controls centered below the grid, and finally the popular-products strip, which acts as a visual footer for the page’s primary content.

layout.overall_description: The category listing page sits on a rich, nearly-black background with subtle texture, framed by generous left and right margins that create a centered, max-width content band. At the very top of this band, a slim breadcrumb line runs from the left, followed by a strong, italicized category heading that anchors the page. Just below, a horizontal strip combines applied filter chips and a right-aligned sort control, forming a compact but readable control row. Under this, the layout breaks into a left-hand filter sidebar and a right-hand product grid that mirrors the mockup’s three-column card row: tall product cards with dominant imagery and overlapping white text panels. The grid feels grounded and orderly, with consistent gutters and comfortable padding, while the dark background flows behind everything like a workshop wall. At the bottom, centered pagination invites users to move through more products, and a full-width popular-products strip echoes the three-card motif in a slightly compressed form, creating a cohesive, crafted experience from top to bottom.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category context loader appears as an overlay within the centered content band, starting just below the top navigation bar and covering the full width of the max-width container. Vertically, it spans from the breadcrumb area down to at least the first few rows of the product grid space, presenting skeleton placeholders that mimic the eventual layout: a thin bar where breadcrumb and heading will be, a wider bar for the controls strip, a narrow left column for filters, and three-column card skeletons on the right. The overlay sits above all other components with a slight dark translucent veil, ensuring the underlying layout is visible but inactive while data loads.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned as an absolute or fixed overlay relative to the main content container; covers interactive elements while loading and disappears once data is ready, allowing the normal layout beneath to remain in place without shifting.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter sidebar is anchored on the left side of the main content band, beginning a short distance below the controls row (applied filters + sort) and extending downward alongside the product grid. It occupies roughly 25% of the content band width (about 3 of 12 columns) and maintains a consistent left margin that aligns with the breadcrumb and heading above. The sidebar appears as a tall, vertically stacked panel with a slightly lighter dark tone than the background and subtle gold dividers between filter groups, with internal padding of about 16–24px all around. Its top edge aligns horizontally with the top of the first row of product cards, creating a clean horizontal baseline across the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Configured as a sticky column: it remains fixed relative to the viewport’s top edge once scrolled past the header, staying visible while the product grid on the right scrolls vertically.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The applied filters bar sits just below the category heading, occupying the full width of the central content band and forming the left portion of the controls row. It appears as a horizontal strip of filter chips starting from the left margin aligned with the breadcrumb and heading, extending to roughly two-thirds of the band’s width before leaving space for the sort control on the right. The bar has modest vertical height, with 12–16px padding top and bottom and 16px horizontal padding inside, and ample whitespace above and below (about 16–24px) separating it from the heading above and the main content area below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls normally with the page, remaining a part of the static header section for the category content. When empty (no filters applied), it collapses vertically or shows a minimal placeholder line to preserve alignment with the sort control.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control is positioned on the same horizontal line as the applied filters bar but aligned to the far right edge of the central content band. It occupies a compact width (about 15–20% of the content band) and appears as a single dropdown or segmented control with clear gold-accented borders on the dark background. The control aligns vertically with the center of the applied filters chips, creating a balanced control row with left-weighted filters and right-weighted sorting. There is 16–24px of vertical spacing below this row before the start of the filter sidebar and product grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls normally with the page, maintaining its place in the header control row. On narrower viewports, it can stack below the applied filters while still staying aligned to the right within its line.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product grid occupies the right-hand portion of the main content area, starting directly to the right of the filter sidebar and extending to the right margin of the content band. It spans roughly 75% of the content width (about 9 of 12 columns) and is arranged as a responsive three-column grid on desktop, mirroring the mockup’s three-card row. Each card column is equal width with consistent horizontal gutters of ~24px and vertical spacing between rows of ~32px. The top of the first row of cards aligns with the top of the filter sidebar, creating a unified horizontal line, and the grid can extend several rows downward depending on the number of products.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls vertically with the page beneath the header; it adapts responsively to available width but preserves the three-column structure in desktop view to echo the mockup’s card layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each product card is a tall, vertically oriented tile within the grid, occupying one of the three equal-width columns. The upper ~65–70% of the card height is dominated by a full-bleed product image that reaches the card’s left, right, and top edges. A white or off-white text panel overlaps the lower part of the image, occupying the bottom ~30–35% of the card; its top edge cuts across the image, echoing the mockup’s overlapping treatment. Inside this text panel, a bold, slightly italic product name sits at the top, with a smaller descriptive line and price beneath, all padded 16px from the panel edges. The card has subtle elevation or border contrast against the dark background, with consistent external margins forming the grid gutters.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the grid columns; on hover, the card can slightly elevate or the image can subtly zoom within its frame, but spatially it remains anchored in its grid cell.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls are centered horizontally below the product grid, spanning the middle portion of the content band (approximately 40–50% of its width) with equal dark space to the left and right. There is generous vertical spacing (about 32–40px) between the last row of product cards and the pagination row, giving it clear separation while still visually connected. Pagination elements—page numbers, next/prev arrows—are arranged in a single horizontal line, with subtle gold accents for the active page and hover states, standing out against the dark background.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the page and always appears immediately after the grid content; it does not stick or float but feels like the natural end cap to each product page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The popular-products strip sits below the pagination controls, acting as a final recommendation band within the same centered content width. It spans the full width of the content band and appears as a slightly distinct horizontal section, possibly with a slightly lighter dark background or a thin gold top border for separation. Inside, a compact heading on the left introduces the strip, while a row of 3–4 smaller product cards (echoing the main card style but slightly reduced in height) is arranged horizontally across the band with tight but consistent spacing. Vertical padding of about 32–48px top and bottom gives this strip breathing room and a clear sense of being a supporting, not primary, section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls normally and forms the visual footer of the category content; cards within the strip align to the same central grid as the main product grid but may allow horizontal scrolling on smaller viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category heading is positioned near the top of the content band, just below the breadcrumb navigation, aligned to the left with the central grid. It spans roughly 60–70% of the content width, leaving open space to the right that keeps the layout feeling airy. The heading uses a bold, slightly italic type treatment to mirror the mockup’s card headings and sits with 16–24px of vertical spacing above the applied filters bar. This heading serves as the primary textual anchor for the page, visually stronger than surrounding labels and controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static text component that scrolls with the page; its horizontal alignment sets the left edge for most subsequent content in the central band.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb navigation forms the first line of content inside the central band, sitting flush with the left alignment of the category heading and about 24–32px below the bottom edge of the top navigation bar. It is a slim horizontal line of text links occupying roughly the left 40–50% of the content width, with the rest of the row left open to maintain spaciousness. The breadcrumbs are relatively small and subtle, with gold-accented separators or current-page indicator, ensuring they support the design without overpowering the heading below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls normally as part of the page; stays at the top of the content section and does not stick as the user scrolls down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When no products match the selected filters, the empty state message appears in the area normally occupied by the product grid, centered both horizontally within the grid’s width and vertically within the visible portion of that section. It spans roughly 50–60% of the content band, aligned to the grid’s center column, with a white or light panel that echoes the product-card text panels but enlarged, featuring an icon or illustration above a bold title and supporting text. There is generous padding inside the panel (24–32px) and ample dark space around it, maintaining the open, crafted aesthetic while clearly communicating the lack of results.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily replaces the product cards within the grid area when no results are available; it scrolls with the page like normal content and aligns to the same grid as the product cards.

name: Search Results
title: Search Results
description: Displays products matching a search query with dynamic filters, sorting, and pagination. Users can refine results by attributes such as material, style, rating, and price, then open product detail pages from the results.

core_features: Read the current search state from URL parameters (query, material, style_theme, rating_min, price_min, price_max, sort_by, page) and keep the UI in sync with those parameters.
Fetch dynamic filter metadata for the current query using getSearchFilterOptions and render available filter options without hardcoding values.
Fetch paginated search results for the current query and filters using searchProducts, and render a grid/list of product cards.
Provide a search input on the page that allows updating the query and reloading results while resetting or adjusting filters as defined.
Allow users to refine results by material, style_theme, rating_min, and price range, with every change updating URL parameters and triggering a new searchProducts call.
Provide sorting controls bound to the sort_by URL parameter, updating search order via searchProducts without hardcoding sort labels into the spec.
Provide pagination controls bound to the page URL parameter, using searchProducts.total_count and searchProducts.page_size to compute pages.
Allow opening product details by clicking a product card, navigating to product.html with a product ID in the URL while preserving normal browser behaviors such as opening in a new tab.
user_workflows:
Initial search from another page:
1. User types a search term into the global search bar on index.html and submits.
2. The site navigates to search.html with the query URL parameter set and other parameters absent.
3. On load, the Search Results page parses URL parameters and determines the effective search state (query from URL, other parameters undefined or default).
4. The page calls getSearchFilterOptions with the query and uses the response to render available materials, style themes, rating thresholds, and a suggested price range.
5. The page calls searchProducts with the query, derived filters (none applied yet if no filter params), current sort_by (if present), page (defaulted when missing), and a computed page_size.
6. Once searchProducts returns, the product grid renders the list of products and pagination controls; the header indicates the query and number of results.

Refining results with filters:
1. User looks at the filter sidebar and chooses one or more options (for example, a material or style_theme) based solely on the options returned from getSearchFilterOptions.
2. When the user confirms the filter change (e.g., by clicking an Apply button or by an auto-apply interaction defined by the implementation), the page constructs a new URL for search.html that:
   - Preserves the existing query parameter.
   - Sets or updates the relevant filter parameters (material, style_theme, rating_min, price_min, price_max) to match the user’s selections.
   - Resets the page parameter to the first page.
   - Preserves or updates sort_by depending on whether the user changed sorting.
3. The browser navigates to the updated URL (either via full reload or client-side history manipulation), and the page re-runs getSearchFilterOptions and searchProducts with the new state.
4. The active-filters summary shows the applied filters, and the product grid shows updated results.

Adjusting sorting of results:
1. User opens a sorting control (e.g., a dropdown) that lists sort options whose underlying values map to sort_by parameter values supported by searchProducts.
2. User selects a different sort option.
3. The page constructs a new search.html URL where:
   - query and all filter parameters (material, style_theme, rating_min, price_min, price_max) are preserved.
   - sort_by is set to the selected sort key.
   - page is reset to the first page.
4. The browser navigates to the new URL, and the page calls searchProducts again with the new sort_by while keeping other filters the same.
5. The product grid re-renders in the new order.

Paginating through results:
1. User scrolls to the pagination controls beneath the product grid.
2. User clicks on a specific page number, a "next" control, or a "previous" control.
3. The page calculates the target page number and constructs a new search.html URL that:
   - Preserves query, filters (material, style_theme, rating_min, price_min, price_max), and sort_by.
   - Sets the page parameter to the chosen page number.
4. The browser navigates to the new URL and calls searchProducts with the updated page while keeping query, filters, and sort_by unchanged.
5. The product grid updates to show the products for that page.

Updating the search query from within the Search Results page:
1. The user edits the search input at the top of the Search Results page and submits a new query string.
2. The page constructs a new search.html URL that:
   - Sets query to the newly entered value.
   - Optionally clears or preserves existing filters and sort_by, according to the desired business rule; for this design, the default is to clear material, style_theme, rating_min, price_min, and price_max while preserving sort_by if present.
   - Sets page to the first page.
3. The browser navigates to this URL. The page calls getSearchFilterOptions and searchProducts with the new query.
4. Filters are recalculated based on the new query; the product grid shows results for the new term.

Removing individual filters or clearing all filters:
1. User views currently active filters in the active-filters summary component.
2. To remove one filter, the user clicks on the corresponding remove control for that filter.
3. The page constructs a new search.html URL identical to the current one except that the corresponding filter parameter (material, style_theme, rating_min, price_min, or price_max) is removed or set to an empty value; page is reset to the first page.
4. The page navigates to the new URL and re-runs searchProducts.
5. To clear all filters at once, the user clicks a "clear filters" control. The page then constructs a URL containing only query, sort_by (if kept), and page reset to the first page, removes all filter parameters, and re-runs searchProducts.
6. The filter sidebar resets its controls to match the cleared filter state.

Opening a product detail page from search results:
1. User scans the product grid and identifies a product of interest.
2. User clicks anywhere on the product card or a dedicated link within the card.
3. The click event leads to a navigation to product.html with an id URL parameter equal to the product_id from the searchProducts response for that card.
4. If the user uses a browser modifier (such as Ctrl/Cmd+click or middle-click), the product detail page opens in a new tab or window, handled by the browser.
5. The Search Results page state remains encoded in the URL, so the user can return via the back button or by switching tabs and still see the same results.
interactions:
On initial page load or whenever URL parameters change, the page:
  - Reads query, material, style_theme, rating_min, price_min, price_max, sort_by, and page from the URL.
  - Calls getSearchFilterOptions with the current query (if present).
  - Calls searchProducts with the current query, an assembled filters object derived from the filter parameters, the sort_by value (if present), the page number, and a dynamically computed page_size.
  - Updates all bound components once responses are received.

Search input submission (within the page):
  - Event: onSubmit of the search form.
  - Behavior: Prevent default form submission, read the entered query, construct a new URL with query set to the new value, clear existing filter parameters, optionally preserve sort_by, reset page to the first page, then navigate to that URL.

Filter selection and confirmation:
  - Event: onChange in filter controls for material, style_theme, rating threshold, or price range.
  - Behavior: Update internal, transient selection state for each filter control without immediately updating the URL (if the UI uses an explicit Apply button) or directly update the URL for auto-apply designs.
  - Event: onClick on an Apply Filters button (if present) or the completion of the last field in auto-apply.
  - Behavior: Read the current filter selections, construct a URL reflecting those filters, preserve query and sort_by, set page to the first page, navigate to the new URL, which then triggers a new searchProducts call.

Filter clearing interactions:
  - Event: onClick on a Clear Filters button.
  - Behavior: Build a URL that removes material, style_theme, rating_min, price_min, and price_max from the query string, preserves query and sort_by (if desired), sets page to the first page, and navigates to this URL.
  - Event: onClick on an individual filter chip in the active-filters summary.
  - Behavior: Remove only the corresponding parameter, preserve others, reset page to the first page, and navigate.

Sorting interactions:
  - Event: onChange on the sorting control.
  - Behavior: Read the selected sort option’s underlying key, construct a URL with sort_by set to that key, preserve query and filter parameters, reset page to the first page, navigate to the URL, then re-run searchProducts.

Pagination interactions:
  - Event: onClick on a page number control.
  - Behavior: Determine the target page based on what the user clicked, set page to that number in the URL, preserve query, filters, and sort_by, navigate to the new URL, and re-run searchProducts.
  - Event: onClick on Next/Previous.
  - Behavior: Increment or decrement the current page value (respecting bounds based on total_count and page_size), then behave as above.

Product card interactions:
  - Event: onClick on a product card or card link without modifiers.
  - Behavior: Navigate to product.html with id set to the product_id for that card.
  - Event: onClick with browser modifiers (Ctrl/Cmd, middle-click).
  - Behavior: Allow the browser to open the link in a new tab or window; no custom logic is required beyond having a correct href.
  - Event: onFocus / onKeyPress (e.g., Enter) on a focused card or link for keyboard navigation.
  - Behavior: Trigger the same navigation as a click event.

Loading and error states:
  - While getSearchFilterOptions or searchProducts are in progress, components that depend on their data show loading indicators instead of stale or empty content.
  - If either interface call fails, the page displays an error message and a retry control that re-invokes the failed call with the same parameters when clicked.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page uses URL parameters as the single source of truth for search state. On every load or navigation to search.html, it parses the query string to determine the current state:

- query (string): If present, this is passed directly to getSearchFilterOptions and searchProducts. If absent or empty, the page may choose to treat this as a no-query state (depending on business rules) and can still call searchProducts with an empty or null query.

- material (string): If present, it represents a selected material filter. The filter-sidebar and active-filters-summary components mark the corresponding material option as selected by comparing this value with the values returned in getSearchFilterOptions.materials. When calling searchProducts, this value is included in the filters object if it matches a returned option; if it does not match any available option, it is ignored for searching but remains reflected in the URL until user interaction updates it.

- style_theme (string): Operates like material. It reflects the selected style filter and is matched against getSearchFilterOptions.style_themes. When calling searchProducts, it is included in the filters object if valid.

- rating_min (number): If present, it indicates the minimum average rating filter. The rating filter UI uses this parameter to select the matching threshold option from getSearchFilterOptions.rating_thresholds. When calling searchProducts, rating_min is included in the filters object if it is numeric.

- price_min (number) and price_max (number): These represent the current price range filter. If either parameter is present and valid, it is bound into the filter controls (e.g., the min and max price inputs). When calling searchProducts, both are passed in the filters object where they are present. If only one is provided, only that boundary is applied. If they fall outside the range suggested by getSearchFilterOptions.price_range_suggestion, the UI still reflects the current values but may highlight that the selection is outside the typical range.

- sort_by (string): If present, it signals the current sorting mode. The sorting control selects the corresponding option by matching the sort_by value against a configuration of supported sort keys. When calling searchProducts, this value is passed directly. If sort_by is absent, the UI displays its default (e.g., a relevance label), and searchProducts relies on its backend default ordering.

- page (number): Represents the current page for pagination. If present and valid, it is passed directly to searchProducts. If absent or not a valid positive integer, the page logic treats it as the first page. Pagination controls read searchProducts.page and searchProducts.page_size to compute their own state, but the URL’s page remains the canonical value. When page is updated via user interaction, all other URL parameters are preserved.

Consistency and synchronization rules:
- Whenever user interactions change any part of the search state (query, filters, sort_by, page), the page does not store that state in localStorage or other persistent client storage. Instead, it encodes the complete current state into the URL parameters and navigates to that URL. This ensures that the browser’s back/forward navigation and sharable links fully reproduce the same search context.
- Components derive their current display state from URL parameters combined with interface responses. For example, the filter-sidebar uses URL parameters to determine which checkboxes or inputs are selected, while the actual list of possible values comes from getSearchFilterOptions.
- The filters object passed into searchProducts is assembled at call time by checking each URL parameter; only parameters that are present and valid are included, which avoids sending stale or irrelevant filters.
- If the query parameter changes, the page treats it as a new search context and, by default in this design, clears all filter parameters (material, style_theme, rating_min, price_min, price_max) while optionally preserving sort_by. The URL and UI both reflect this reset behavior.
- Any error state or empty results state does not modify the URL parameters; instead, it only affects the visual representation, allowing users to adjust filters or query without losing prior state.

components.functionality: Displays the current search query, total number of results, and a search input for modifying the query directly on the Search Results page. It also triggers new searches by updating the query URL parameter.

Renders dynamic filter controls for materials, style themes, rating thresholds, and price range based on getSearchFilterOptions. It reads current filter values from URL parameters, allows the user to change them, and applies changes by updating the URL and triggering a new search.
data_binding: URL parameters: material, style_theme, rating_min, price_min, price_max

components.functionality: Summarizes currently applied filters as removable items. Each item corresponds to one or more URL parameters. Removing an item updates the URL by clearing the relevant parameter(s) and resets pagination, leading to a refreshed search.
data_binding: URL parameters: material, style_theme, rating_min, price_min, price_max

components.functionality: Provides user-selectable sorting options for the search results. It binds to the sort_by URL parameter and updates it when the user chooses a different sort order, then triggers a new search via searchProducts.

Displays the list of products returned by searchProducts for the current query, filters, sort, and page. Each product card includes key information such as name, image, price range, rating, and flags like same-day pickup eligibility, and provides navigation to the product detail page.
data_binding: searchProducts: products array (product_id, name, slug, image_url, base_price, min_price, max_price, category_id, category_name, product_type, material, size_label, style_theme, rating_average, rating_count, is_same_day_pickup_eligible)

components.functionality: Controls navigation between pages of results. It uses searchProducts.page, searchProducts.page_size, and searchProducts.total_count to determine how many pages exist and which page is current. Clicking a page or next/previous updates the page URL parameter and triggers a new search.

Coordinates loading, error, and empty-state behavior for search results and filters. It tracks the status of getSearchFilterOptions and searchProducts requests and determines whether to show loading indicators, error messages, empty-results messages, or the actual content.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with three equal-width content columns (each roughly 4 columns wide) for the product cards. Content is centered within wide horizontal margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s vertical-first, then horizontal pattern: a full-width dark background with a constrained, centered content band. Below the existing global nav, the search-query-header sits at the top of this band. Directly beneath it, the active-filters-summary forms a slim horizontal strip. Below that, the main results zone is a two-column layout: a left filter-sidebar column and a right results column containing, top to bottom, sort-controls, results-state-handler (when needed), product-grid, and finally pagination-controls. The product-grid uses a three-column card layout that mirrors the mockup’s 3-card hero row, repeated vertically.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the mockup’s vertical-first, then horizontal pattern: a full-width dark background with a constrained, centered content band. Below the existing global nav, the search-query-header sits at the top of this band. Directly beneath it, the active-filters-summary forms a slim horizontal strip. Below that, the main results zone is a two-column layout: a left filter-sidebar column and a right results column containing, top to bottom, sort-controls, results-state-handler (when needed), product-grid, and finally pagination-controls. The product-grid uses a three-column card layout that mirrors the mockup’s 3-card hero row, repeated vertically.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The central content band spans roughly 70–80% of the viewport width (max-width around 1120–1200px), with generous dark margins on the left and right. Vertically, the header region (search-query-header + active-filters-summary) is relatively compact compared to the main results zone. Within the results zone, the left filter-sidebar occupies about 25–30% of the content band width (around 3 of 12 grid columns), while the product area on the right uses the remaining 70–75% (around 9 of 12 columns). Product cards maintain the mockup’s proportion: each card’s image takes roughly 65–70% of card height, with the white text panel overlaying the lower 30–35%. Major vertical separations between these bands use 48–64px spacing; gutters between columns and rows use 24–32px.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density remains moderately spacious, echoing the mockup. The search header and filter summary are compact but not cramped, with 16–24px internal padding. The filter-sidebar groups are well spaced (24–32px between groups), avoiding visual overload despite multiple controls. The product-grid, though denser than the original single row, still feels open thanks to consistent 24–32px gutters, clear card separation, and ample outer margins. Pagination is compact and visually light so it doesn’t compete with the cards.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves from the top of the results content band downward, then sweeps horizontally. First, the user reads the search-query-header at the top-left of the content band, then glances across to any right-aligned elements in that header. Next, attention drops slightly to the active-filters-summary row, scanning left-to-right across active filter chips. From there, the gaze moves down into the main results zone: it first catches the left filter-sidebar (a strong vertical column of controls), then shifts right to the sort-controls at the top of the product area. Finally, attention flows down into the product-grid, reading the three-column cards left-to-right and then row-by-row. Pagination anchors the bottom of this flow. In empty/error states, the results-state-handler becomes the visual focal point in the product area, temporarily overriding the grid in that right-hand column.

layout.overall_description: The search results page sits on a nearly-black, full-bleed background, with all interactive content contained inside a centered, max-width content band framed by generous dark margins on both sides. Below the site’s full-width navigation, the search query headline anchors the top of this band, left-aligned and given enough breathing room to feel like a clear section start. Immediately beneath it, a slim row of active filter chips stretches horizontally, visually tying the query to the currently applied refinements. Dropping down, the layout divides into two columns: on the left, a tall, structured filter sidebar that runs most of the visible results height; on the right, a wider results area. At the top of the right column, sorting controls align horizontally with the top of the filter sidebar, establishing a clear baseline. Below, the three-column product grid echoes the mockup’s card style, with tall, richly lit product photos and overlapping white text panels creating a strong contrast against the dark background. Rows of these cards step downward with consistent vertical rhythm. At the bottom, centered within the results column, compact pagination controls provide navigation without drawing attention away from the products. Overall, the page feels like a refined, artisan workshop shelf: filters as the sturdy shelving on the left, and the crafted items displayed in orderly, glowing rows on the right.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search-query-header sits immediately below the existing global navigation bar, within the same centered content band used throughout the site. It is left-aligned inside this band and spans nearly the full content width (about 90–100% of the content band, which itself is roughly 70–80% of the viewport). There is a generous top margin from the nav (48–64px) and a slightly smaller margin below it (24–32px) before the active-filters-summary. The header may comprise a large, bold title such as “Results for ‘Walnut Cutting Board’” on the left, with an optional secondary line showing the result count below or to the right of the main title. The overall height is modest—about 80–120px depending on text wrapping—but the surrounding whitespace lets it clearly mark the start of the search results context, echoing the hero-like presence of the mockup’s card row without being visually heavy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in the normal document flow, always anchored at the top of the results content band. On narrow viewports it spans the full available width and may stack any secondary text below the main heading.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter-sidebar begins just below the active-filters-summary and aligns to the left edge of the centered content band. It occupies roughly 25–30% of the content band width (about 3 of 12 grid columns) and runs vertically alongside the product area, extending downwards for most of the results zone. The sidebar appears as a subtly elevated dark panel slightly lighter than the page background, with 24px internal padding on all sides. Filter groups (e.g., Material, Style, Rating, Price) are stacked vertically, with section titles left-aligned and 24–32px of vertical spacing between groups. Individual filter controls (checkboxes, sliders, etc.) use 8–16px vertical spacing for clean scannability. Horizontally, there is a 24–32px gutter between the right edge of the sidebar and the start of the product-grid column, maintaining the mockup’s comfortable separation between major blocks. On larger screens, the top of the filter-sidebar aligns horizontally with the top of the sort-controls to create a stable horizontal baseline across the results zone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop and larger tablets, the sidebar is vertically scroll-sticky: once the user scrolls past its initial position, it remains fixed relative to the viewport’s top (just below the nav and search header) until its bottom reaches the bottom of the content band. On smaller screens, it collapses into a slide-out or accordion panel above the product-grid, switching from a left column to a full-width, toggleable block.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The active-filters-summary forms a thin horizontal band situated between the search-query-header and the main results zone. It is aligned with the same centered content band, spanning roughly 90–100% of the band’s width. Vertically, there is about 16–24px of space above and below this strip, making it feel light and unobtrusive.
