Active filters appear as pill-shaped chips starting from the left edge, wrapping to additional lines as needed. When there are only a few filters, the strip is about 40–56px tall; when many filters are active, it can expand to two or three rows but never dominates the page due to consistent, modest chip sizing and 8–12px vertical spacing between rows. The right side of this band can optionally hold a “Clear all” action, aligned to the right edge. Visually, this component creates a bridge between the query header and the more functional filter-sidebar and sort-controls below, subtly echoing the mockup’s organized but open feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the vertical flow. When there are no active filters, the entire element either collapses to a minimal, one-line hint or disappears, allowing the search-query-header to sit closer to the results zone and conserve vertical space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort-controls sit at the top of the right-hand results column, directly to the right of the filter-sidebar and aligned with its top edge. They occupy the full width of the product area (about 70–75% of the content band, or 9 of 12 grid columns). Within this horizontal band, content is arranged so that the primary sort dropdown and label are right-aligned, emphasizing their control-like nature, while any secondary info (e.g., “123 items”) can appear left-aligned at the start of the row. The band has a height of about 48–56px, with 16–24px padding above and below to keep it breathable. A subtle bottom divider or shadow may separate it visually from the product-grid beneath, similar in weight to the nav’s separation in the mockup, but kept delicate to avoid competing with the cards. Below the sort-controls, there is a 16–24px gap before the first row of product cards to maintain clear hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the normal flow of the right column. On narrower viewports, controls may stack vertically (sort label on one line, dropdown below) but remain fixed above the product-grid. On very small screens, sort-controls can be paired with the filter toggle into a single bar that sits above the grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-grid fills the right-hand results column beneath the sort-controls, extending downwards and forming the main visual body of the page. It occupies about 70–75% of the content band width (roughly 9 of 12 grid columns), starting immediately to the right of the filter-sidebar with a 24–32px horizontal gutter between them. Within this area, products are arranged in a three-column grid on desktop, directly echoing the mockup’s three-card row. Each card is a tall rectangle: the upper 65–70% is a full-bleed product image, while the lower 30–35% is a white or very light panel that overlaps the bottom portion of the image, containing a bold, slightly italic product name and a smaller description or key info beneath it. Cards are vertically spaced with 24–32px gaps between rows and 24–32px gutters between columns, letting the dark background show through and frame each card. The grid can extend for multiple rows, with the vertical rhythm matching the mockup’s spacious feeling. On tablet widths, the grid may collapse to two columns; on mobile, to one column, but the card’s image/text proportion remains consistent to preserve the design language.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static scrolling grid within the page flow. It expands vertically as more products are loaded, pushing the pagination-controls downward. In loading or empty states, the grid area yields visual priority to the results-state-handler, either dimming or hiding its cards depending on implementation, but its spatial footprint (the right column) remains the same.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination-controls sit at the bottom of the right-hand results column, directly beneath the last row of the product-grid. There is a 32–48px vertical gap between the bottom of the final card row and the top of the pagination bar, allowing the product grid to visually conclude before navigation options appear. The controls themselves are horizontally centered within the product column, not the full viewport, aligning with the card columns above. Pagination elements (previous/next arrows and page numbers) are laid out in a single row, with the current page highlighted using the gold accent color and perhaps a subtle border or fill. The overall height is compact (around 40–48px), and the horizontal footprint is modest—usually occupying the central 40–60% of the product column—so it reads as a functional footer to the results rather than a dominant element.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the bottom of the results list, pushed downward as more content is added above. On long pages, it may appear just above the fold or below, but does not stick or float. On small screens, pagination controls may stretch closer to full width for tap targets while remaining within the content band.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results-state-handler occupies the same right-hand results column as the product-grid, sharing its horizontal boundaries. In loading, empty, or error states, it becomes the primary content within that area. It is vertically centered within the visible portion of the results column when possible, with generous breathing room above and below (at least 64px padding from the preceding header/controls and from the bottom of the viewport). Its content—such as an icon or illustration, a message like “No products match your filters,” and a call-to-action button to adjust filters—stacks vertically and is center-aligned within this column. The filter-sidebar remains visible on the left, emphasizing that the user can adjust filters to resolve the empty state. When the state handler is active, the product-grid appears hidden or visually de-emphasized behind it, and pagination controls are concealed to avoid confusion.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contextual and conditional. It appears in place of, or layered above, the product-grid within the right column. In loading states, it can overlay a skeleton version of the grid but remains centered and prominent. Once results are successfully loaded, it collapses entirely from the layout, returning visual priority to the product-grid and pagination-controls.

name: Product Detail
title: Product Detail
description: Page for viewing a single product, configuring variants and personalization, selecting fulfillment and shipping options, and adding the configured product to the cart or wishlist, with an option to request a bulk quote when available.

core_features: Read the product ID from the URL (id) and load product details using getProductDetails.
Load all available shipping and pickup options for the product using getProductShippingOptions.
Display full product information, including images, description, price or price range, size/capacity, material, and rating information.
Provide a configuration form with variant options (color, wood finish, engraving style, font) based on the product’s available option arrays.
Provide personalization inputs that are conditionally shown based on product flags (main engraving text, multiple names for sets, event date, personalization notes).
Allow the user to set quantity and fulfillment type (shipping or store pickup when eligible) and select a shipping or pickup option.
Highlight the lowest-priced shipping option within a configurable delivery window (e.g., within 10 business days) when such options exist.
Allow adding the configured product to the cart using addConfiguredProductToCart, including selected shipping/pickup option and personalization configuration.
Allow saving the configured product to the wishlist using addConfiguredProductToWishlist with the same configuration and desired quantity.
Expose a Request Bulk Quote button for bulk-eligible products that navigates to bulk_quote.html with the current product ID and entered quantity.
user_workflows:
View and understand product details:
1. User navigates to product.html with an id URL parameter.
2. The page reads id and calls getProductDetails(productId=id).
3. In parallel or after, the page calls getProductShippingOptions(productId=id).
4. When responses return, the product header shows name, category, rating, description, material, size/capacity, and pricing information.
5. A shipping summary banner shows the most relevant shipping/pickup information derived from the shipping options.

Configure product variants and personalization:
1. After product data loads, the configuration form initializes with default or empty values.
2. If the product has color_options, the user selects a color; similarly for wood_finish_options, engraving_style_options, and font_options.
3. If has_engraving_text is true, a main engraving text field is shown; user enters names/message.
4. If has_multiple_name_fields is true, a list of name inputs appears; user can add/edit/remove names, which are stored as an array.
5. If has_event_date_field is true, the user chooses or enters an event date, stored as both a display text and a structured date value.
6. If supports_gift_wrap is true, the user selects a gift wrap option (none, free gift wrap if has_free_gift_wrap is true, or paid gift wrap if implemented by the backend).
7. The user can add optional personalization notes (e.g., special instructions).
8. A live preview panel updates as the user changes these options (purely front-end state).

Select fulfillment and shipping/pickup and add to cart:
1. After shipping options load via getProductShippingOptions, the fulfillment selector identifies which options are standard shipping and which are pickup (is_pickup_option).
2. If the product is_store_pickup_eligible is true and any pickup options exist, the user can choose store pickup; otherwise only shipping is available.
3. The component preselects a default shipping option (based on is_default, or another rule if none are marked default) and highlights the lowest-priced eligible option within the target delivery window.
4. The user adjusts quantity as needed.
5. On clicking Add to Cart, the page validates required fields (e.g., engraving text if applicable, selected shipping/pickup option, quantity > 0).
6. If valid, the page constructs a configuration object from current selections and calls addConfiguredProductToCart(productId, quantity, fulfillment_type, shippingOptionId, configuration).
7. On success, a success banner shows a brief summary (using the returned cart object) and offers a button to go to cart.html and an option to continue on the product page.
8. On failure, an error message is shown and the user can correct issues and retry.

Save configuration to wishlist:
1. The user configures the product (variants, personalization, and quantity) as desired.
2. The user clicks Add to Wishlist.
3. The page validates required fields similar to the add-to-cart flow (excluding fulfillment and shipping, which are not part of the wishlist interface).
4. On valid input, the page calls addConfiguredProductToWishlist(productId, desired_quantity, configuration).
5. On success, the page displays a success message including wishlist item count and provides a View Wishlist button that navigates to wishlist.html.
6. On failure, the page displays an error message and keeps the configuration intact for correction and retry.

Request a bulk quote from this product:
1. The user sets the quantity field to the approximate number of items they want for a bulk order.
2. If product.is_bulk_order_available is true, the Request Bulk Quote button is visible and enabled when quantity is at least a minimum application-defined value (handled in front-end validation if needed).
3. When the user clicks Request Bulk Quote, the page reads the current product id and quantity.
4. The page navigates to bulk_quote.html with URL parameters productId (from id) and quantityRequested (from entered quantity), without making additional API calls for the quote itself.
5. The bulk_quote.html page then uses those parameters in its own flow (outside the scope of this page).
interactions:
URL-driven loading:
- On page load or when the id URL parameter changes, trigger calls to getProductDetails and getProductShippingOptions.
- If id is missing or invalid, show an error state and do not attempt further actions.

Variant selection:
- onChange for color, wood finish, engraving style, and font dropdowns or selectors updates configuration state.
- Disabled or hidden if the product does not provide corresponding options arrays.

Personalization input:
- onChange for the main engraving text field updates configuration.engraving_text.
- onChange for per-item name fields updates configuration.engraving_names as an ordered array.
- onChange for event date chooser updates configuration.event_date and configuration.event_date_text.
- onChange for gift wrap selection updates configuration.gift_wrap_selection, respecting whether free gift wrap is available.
- onChange for personalization notes updates configuration.personalization_notes.

Quantity and fulfillment selection:
- onChange for quantity updates the internal quantity state.
- onChange for fulfillment type (shipping vs pickup) toggles which set of shipping options are shown and what fulfillment_type will be passed to addConfiguredProductToCart.
- onChange for shipping/pickup option selection updates the selected shippingOptionId and updates the displayed delivery estimate.

Add to Cart:
- onSubmitAddToCart validates configuration and quantity.
- On passing validation, call addConfiguredProductToCart with the current productId, quantity, fulfillment_type, shippingOptionId, and configuration.
- onAddToCartSuccess updates a success banner with data from the returned cart object and enables navigation to cart.html.
- onAddToCartError displays the error message from the API response, if present, or a generic error.

Add to Wishlist:
- onClickAddToWishlist validates required personalization fields.
- On passing validation, call addConfiguredProductToWishlist with productId, desired_quantity, and configuration.
- onAddToWishlistSuccess shows a success banner and provides a link to wishlist.html.
- onAddToWishlistError displays the error message and keeps the current configuration in place.

Bulk quote navigation:
- onClickRequestBulkQuote reads the current quantity and productId.
- If quantity is valid, the browser navigates to bulk_quote.html with productId and quantityRequested URL parameters.
- If quantity is invalid, a validation error is shown and navigation is blocked.

Feedback and validation:
- Validation errors (missing engraving text, zero quantity, missing required selections, etc.) show inline messages before any API call.
- API success and error responses update a centralized feedback banner component that can be dismissed by the user.

Navigation to other pages:
- After a successful add to cart, clicking View Cart navigates to cart.html.
- After a successful add to wishlist, clicking View Wishlist navigates to wishlist.html.
- The product page does not manage cart or wishlist state beyond what is returned from the addConfiguredProductToCart/addConfiguredProductToWishlist responses.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary persistent state for this page is derived from URL parameters and backend responses. The required URL parameter is id, which is treated as the productId. On initial page load: (1) The page reads id from the query string (e.g., product.html?id=...). (2) If id exists, the page calls getProductDetails(productId=id) and getProductShippingOptions(productId=id). (3) If id is missing or blank, the page enters an error state and does not call the interfaces. The product and shipping option data returned from these calls populate in-memory state used by components. Configuration selections (quantity, variant selections, engraving text, names array, event date, gift wrap choice, notes) live only in client memory for the lifetime of the page; they are not stored in URL parameters or localStorage. When the user clicks Request Bulk Quote, the page constructs a navigation URL to bulk_quote.html that includes productId (copied directly from the current id) and quantityRequested (taken from the current quantity state). Navigation to cart.html and wishlist.html after successful add actions does not require extra URL parameters from this page. All state related to the current cart and wishlist is managed by the backend and is surfaced only via the responses from addConfiguredProductToCart and addConfiguredProductToWishlist. No localStorage or other client-side persistence is used; reloading the product page discards unsaved configuration and re-derives all data from the id URL parameter and the interfaces.

components.functionality: Reads and validates the product ID from the id URL parameter, and triggers data loading or an error state.
Given a valid productId from the URL, calls getProductDetails and getProductShippingOptions, stores the results in page state, and exposes loading, success, and error states to child components.
Displays core product details: name, category, description, main image, material, size/capacity, base or range price, and rating (average and count), using data from getProductDetails.

Uses the shipping options from getProductShippingOptions to show a concise summary (e.g., the lowest-priced option within a configured delivery window and its delivery_window_text), updating automatically when options change.

Hosts all product configuration controls: quantity, variant selectors (color, wood finish, engraving style, font), personalization inputs (engraving text, multiple names, event date), gift wrap selection, and personalization notes. Builds a configuration object compatible with addConfiguredProductToCart and addConfiguredProductToWishlist.

Allows the user to choose fulfillment_type (shipping or store_pickup when eligible) and select an associated shipping or pickup option from getProductShippingOptions. Exposes the selected fulfillment_type and shippingOptionId to the add-to-cart flow.

Displays a dynamic textual preview of how the engraving and selected options will appear, using current configuration values (engraving text, engraving_names, style, font, event date), purely as a front-end representation.

Takes the current productId, quantity, fulfillment_type, shippingOptionId, and configuration object and calls addConfiguredProductToCart. Handles loading state, displays any warnings/messages from the API, and exposes a View Cart navigation action on success.

Uses the current productId, desired_quantity (from quantity), and configuration object to call addConfiguredProductToWishlist. Shows success and error messages and offers a View Wishlist navigation option on success.

Checks product.is_bulk_order_available and, if true, shows a Request Bulk Quote button that navigates to bulk_quote.html with productId and quantityRequested URL parameters based on the current product ID and quantity state.
Centralizes display of validation errors, API errors, and success messages from configuration, add-to-cart, and wishlist actions. Messages can be dismissed without affecting underlying state.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with three equal-width content columns (each roughly 4 columns wide) for the product cards. Content is centered within wide horizontal margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom flow with a full-width (but constrained) content band centered on a dark, full-bleed background. Immediately under the global nav sit thin horizontal banners (status + shipping) spanning the central content width, followed by a single, dominant three-column row that acts as the hero/product area. That row carries the same 3-card rhythm as the mockup: left column as the visual/brand card (preview + summary), middle column as the configuration card, right column as the fulfillment + purchase card stack. All subsequent interactive elements (wishlist, bulk quote) live inside or just below the right column stack to preserve the 3-column structure rather than adding extra columns or breaking the band.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom flow with a full-width (but constrained) content band centered on a dark, full-bleed background. Immediately under the global nav sit thin horizontal banners (status + shipping) spanning the central content width, followed by a single, dominant three-column row that acts as the hero/product area. That row carries the same 3-card rhythm as the mockup: left column as the visual/brand card (preview + summary), middle column as the configuration card, right column as the fulfillment + purchase card stack. All subsequent interactive elements (wishlist, bulk quote) live inside or just below the right column stack to preserve the 3-column structure rather than adding extra columns or breaking the band.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The central content container occupies roughly 70–80% of the viewport width, aligned center, leaving generous dark margins on both sides. Vertically, the three main columns above the fold are tall, with the left column’s image/preview taking ~65–70% of that column height and the overlapping product summary panel using the lower ~30–35%, directly echoing the mockup’s card proportion. The configuration and fulfillment columns match that apparent height so the three columns visually align. Banners at the top are relatively shallow (about 10–15% of the hero zone height) and spaced apart by 16–24px. Major vertical gaps between the banner stack and the three-column hero are 48–64px, and gaps between stacked cards in the right column are 24–32px.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is moderately spacious. Each card (preview, configuration, shipping/fulfillment, actions) has 16–24px internal padding. The three columns are separated by 24–32px gutters, ensuring the band feels open despite being information-heavy. Form fields in the configuration column are grouped with 16px vertical spacing and 24px between field groups. The right column’s stacked cards have enough breathing room (24–32px) to read as distinct modules rather than one dense slab. Overall, no component feels crammed edge-to-edge; the dark margins and consistent whitespace echo the relaxed, crafted feel of the mockup.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye follows the same vertical-then-horizontal Z-like pattern: start at the top-left nav/logo, move across to any highlighted nav item and cart icon, then drop down to the status and shipping banners. From there, the gaze naturally falls into the hero band’s left column with the large engraving preview image and overlapping white product summary panel. Once the product is understood, the flow moves horizontally: from the left column to the central configuration form (how to customize), then to the right-hand column where fulfillment, shipping choices, and primary purchase actions live. Secondary actions (wishlist, bulk quote) sit below or alongside the add-to-cart area in the same right column, encountered after the primary call-to-action in the natural scroll/order.

layout.overall_description: The product detail page sits on a nearly-black, full-bleed background with subtle woodgrain texture, framed by generous left and right margins. A slim, full-width top navigation bar (already defined elsewhere in the system) anchors the page, with a soft gold accent on the active nav item and cart icon. Directly below the nav, centered within a max-width content container, a shallow status banner and a shipping/availability banner stack vertically, spanning about 70–80% of the viewport width. A comfortable 48–64px gap separates these banners from the main hero band: a single, strong three-column row that echoes the original mockup’s trio of tall image cards. In the left column, a tall personalization preview image dominates, with a clean white panel overlapping its lower third, presenting the product name and key details in bold, slightly italic typography. The middle column hosts a well-organized configuration form, styled as a light-on-dark card with ample paddings and grouped options, matching the left column’s visual weight. The right column stacks fulfillment and shipping options, followed by a compact but visually strong purchase actions card (add to cart, price, quantity), with wishlist and bulk quote links subtly grouped beneath. All three columns align at the top, share equal width, and sit within consistent horizontal gutters, giving the impression of three cohesive cards resting in a dark workshop environment. As the user scrolls, the left preview column tends to remain visible (via sticky behavior on taller viewports), keeping the crafted product in view while the user fine-tunes configuration and shipping details in the center and right columns.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non-visual, infrastructural component that runs before any visible content. It does not occupy any visual space on the page. Conceptually, it sits at the very top of the page logic, resolving the product ID from the URL, but from a layout perspective it has no height, width, or visual representation and does not affect spacing between other components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual infrastructure component; occupies no space and does not impact layout flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is also non-visual. It executes immediately after the URL param reader, fetching product data and providing it to the visual components. It does not render any visible UI elements and thus does not take up physical space. It conceptually underpins all subsequent visual components in the three-column band and banners but is invisible in the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual infrastructure component; occupies no space and does not impact layout flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product main info appears within the left column of the three-column hero band, occupying the lower 30–35% of that column’s height. It is rendered as a white (or very light) text panel that overlaps the bottom of the personalization preview image, echoing the mockup’s card design. Horizontally, it spans nearly the full width of the left column but leaves a small 8–16px inset from the card’s left and right edges for breathing room. Vertically, its top edge cuts into the bottom of the preview image, overlapping by roughly 25–30% of its own height, so the impression is that the text panel is sitting on top of the photo. Internally, the product title is a bold, slightly italic heading at the top of this panel, with a short descriptive paragraph beneath, both left-aligned. The panel’s bottom edge aligns roughly with the bottoms of the middle and right columns’ primary cards, so all three columns visually finish along a common baseline. There is 16–24px padding inside the panel on all sides, ensuring a crafted, spacious feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned relative to the personalization preview card in the same column; visually overlapping via negative margin or absolute positioning on larger screens. On smaller screens, it can stack directly below the preview image without overlap to preserve readability.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shipping summary banner sits near the top of the content band, directly below any global status feedback banner and above the three-column hero band. It is a full-width (within the central max-width container) horizontal strip, spanning approximately 70–80% of the viewport width and centered on the page. Its height is modest—roughly 10–15% of the hero band’s height, similar to a compact alert bar. The banner uses a dark base slightly lighter than the page background, with a subtle border or top accent line in gold to differentiate it. Text is horizontally centered or left-aligned depending on content, with 16–24px vertical padding and 24–32px horizontal padding. There is 16–24px vertical spacing above and below this banner: above, it sits just under the nav and any status feedback; below, a larger 48–64px gap separates it from the top edges of the three main columns, keeping it clearly distinct as a contextual information strip rather than part of the card grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrolls with the page content. Always spans the content container width and remains at the top of the main content area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product configuration form occupies the entire middle column of the three-column hero band. It is styled as a vertically oriented card with a dark base and lighter inner sections to echo the layered feel of the mockup’s cards. Horizontally, it takes roughly one-third of the content band width (about 4 columns in a 12-column grid), with 24–32px gutters separating it from the left and right columns. Vertically, it aligns with the top of the personalization preview in the left column and aims to match or slightly exceed the preview’s height, giving the three columns a balanced skyline. Inside the card, there is 20–24px padding on all sides. The top of the card might feature a concise heading (e.g., “Customize Your Engraving”) aligned left, followed by field groups stacked vertically. Each field group has 16px spacing between internal elements and 24px spacing between groups. On taller viewports, if the form height exceeds the viewport, the card simply scrolls with the page; on shorter ones, the top remains aligned with the preview, and the bottom can extend lower than the other columns, but the initial above-the-fold view still shows the key fields and maintains the three-column alignment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the central column; scrolls with the page. On wide screens, its width is fixed by the 3-column grid; on narrow screens, it collapses into a full-width block stacked beneath the main preview and info.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The fulfillment and shipping selector sits at the top of the right-hand column of the three-column hero band, directly opposite the top of the personalization preview and configuration form. It occupies roughly the upper 45–55% of the right column’s height, styled as a card similar in width and padding to the configuration form—full width of the column with 20–24px padding inside. This card presents options such as pickup vs shipping, estimated dates, and carrier choices. Option groups are stacked vertically with 16px spacing, and an accent—like a thin gold rule or small icon—can mark the currently selected option. The bottom of this card leaves 24–32px of vertical space before the next card (add-to-cart actions), creating a clear separation and echoing the spacing between cards in the original mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column and scrolls with the page. It anchors the top of the right column’s stack so the three primary columns align at the top edge.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The personalization preview is the dominant visual element in the left column of the three-column hero band. It appears as a tall image card, occupying roughly the top 65–70% of the left column’s height. Horizontally, it spans the full width of the left column, with a slightly inset image or canvas (8–12px) from the card’s edges to suggest a framed product photo or live engraving preview. The background is the dark site color, but the image itself is rich and contrasty, featuring the product against a wood or workshop-inspired surface. The white product info panel (product-main-info) overlaps the bottom portion of this preview, so the bottom 25–30% of the image area is visually covered by that panel. There is 48–64px vertical separation between the bottom of this combined card (image + overlapping text) and any content that might follow below it in the same column on longer pages, ensuring the left column still feels like a singular hero card rather than multiple stacked cards.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop and larger tablets, this card is sticky within the left column, staying pinned a short distance (e.g., 80–96px) below the nav as the user scrolls through longer forms in the middle and right columns, so the product remains visible. On smaller screens, the sticky behavior is disabled and the preview simply scrolls with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-cart actions card sits in the right column directly beneath the fulfillment-and-shipping-selector, separated by 24–32px of vertical space. It spans the full width of the right column and occupies roughly 25–30% of its column height, though its exact height is driven by content (price, quantity selector, primary CTA button, possibly a short note on production time). The card uses a slightly lighter background than the shipping selector to emphasize its importance, and the primary ‘Add to Cart’ button uses a rich gold fill contrasting against the dark surroundings. Inside the card, content is arranged in a vertical stack: price and key meta at the top, quantity and variant confirmation in the middle, and the CTA button at the bottom, all left-aligned with 16px vertical spacing. There is 20–24px padding inside. The bottom edge of this card aligns visually with or slightly above the bottom of the left column’s product info panel, reinforcing the sense that all three main columns are part of one cohesive hero band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column; scrolls with the page. The button area can have a slight elevation or shadow to suggest clickability, but the card itself does not float or stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist actions component appears in the right column, immediately below the add-to-cart actions card, separated by a modest 16–24px vertical gap so it reads as related but secondary. It takes the full width of the right column but is relatively short in height, functioning more like an inline control or slim bar than a full card. The background can be either transparent (on the dark page background) or a slightly raised dark panel. The primary element is a text link or small button (e.g., ‘Save to wishlist’) aligned left, with a subtle heart icon in gold or outline style. Internal padding is about 16px left and right, 8–12px top and bottom. The visual styling is intentionally quieter than the add-to-cart card so it does not compete with the main CTA.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column, part of the stacked card sequence; scrolls with the page. On narrow screens, it appears just beneath the main purchase actions when the layout collapses to a single column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bulk quote entry component is placed directly below the wishlist actions in the right column, with a small 8–16px vertical gap to keep them visually related but distinct. It spans the right column width but is compact, akin to a tertiary utility within the purchasing stack. The layout can be a single line: an icon and label like ‘Request a bulk quote’ on the left, with a subtle arrow or chevron on the right, all within a lightly bordered or underlined area. Padding is around 12–16px horizontally and 8–12px vertically. The typography is smaller and less bold than the add-to-cart text, but the gold accent may be used sparingly (icon or underline) to make it discoverable. This placement means users encounter it after considering the standard purchase path, matching its secondary nature.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column; scrolls with the page. On smaller screens, it appears as a small full-width row beneath wishlist actions in the vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The status feedback banner appears at the very top of the main content area, directly under the global navigation bar and above the shipping summary banner. It spans the same max-width content container—centered and occupying about 70–80% of the viewport width—with generous dark margins on the sides. Its height is modest, similar to the shipping summary banner, with 12–16px vertical padding and 24px horizontal padding. It uses color to signal status: for success, a deep desaturated green overlay on the dark background; for errors, a muted dark red; with text in high-contrast off-white. There is 8–16px vertical space between this banner and the shipping summary banner beneath it, preserving the feeling of layered, distinct strips rather than a single bulky block. When not active, this area simply collapses, and the shipping banner shifts up into its place.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width within the content container, scrolling with the page. It appears and disappears dynamically based on state, pushing the content below it down when shown.

name: Shopping Cart
title: Shopping Cart
description: Page where users review, adjust, and manage items in their cart before proceeding to checkout, including personalization summaries and fulfillment details.

core_features: Load and display the current cart with all line items using getCart, including product details, personalization summaries, and fulfillment information.
Allow users to update the quantity of each cart item and reflect updated totals using updateCartItemQuantity.
Allow users to remove items from the cart and reflect updated totals using removeCartItem.
Display a price breakdown including per-line subtotals, items subtotal, shipping total, and order total based on getCart data.
Provide navigation from cart items to individual product detail pages (product.html) for viewing or editing configurations.
Provide navigation to continue shopping (index.html) and to proceed to checkout (checkout page URL) without performing checkout logic here.
Handle and display feedback messages (success/error) from cart update and removal operations.
Support highlighting or focusing a recently added cart item based on URL parameters.
user_workflows: View cart contents: 1) User navigates to cart.html from the site header or after adding an item. 2) On page load, the cart-page-controller component calls getCart. 3) The cart-items-list component renders each item with product info, personalization summary, and fulfillment details. 4) The order-summary-panel shows totals from the getCart response.

Update item quantity: 1) User adjusts the quantity control for a specific line item. 2) cart-items-list validates the new quantity and triggers updateCartItemQuantity with cartItemId and quantity. 3) After a successful response, cart-page-controller refreshes totals using the returned cart summary or triggers a full getCart refresh if needed. 4) The UI updates the line subtotal and order summary, and cart-feedback-banner displays a confirmation or error message.

Remove an item: 1) User clicks the remove button for a cart line item. 2) cart-items-list triggers removeCartItem with the associated cartItemId. 3) On success, cart-page-controller updates the in-memory cart state using the returned cart summary or by refetching via getCart. 4) The removed item disappears from cart-items-list, order-summary-panel shows updated totals, and cart-feedback-banner shows a confirmation. 5) If the cart becomes empty, cart-empty-state is displayed.

Inspect or edit item details: 1) User clicks a product image or name in a cart line. 2) cart-items-list navigates to product.html?id=<product_id> using the product_id from getCart. 3) The product page (out of scope) handles configuration. 4) After editing and potentially re-adding, the user can return to cart.html; if product.html redirects with parameters (e.g., addedItemId), cart-recent-item-highlighter will highlight the relevant row.

Continue shopping: 1) User clicks the 'Continue Shopping' action in order-summary-panel or cart-empty-state. 2) The page navigates to index.html with no additional parameters or with optional context parameters (e.g., returnTo=cart).

Proceed to checkout: 1) User clicks the 'Proceed to Checkout' button in order-summary-panel. 2) If the cart has at least one item, the page navigates to a checkout URL (e.g., checkout.html) without calling additional APIs on this page. 3) Any necessary cart validations specific to checkout occur on the checkout page (out of scope).

Highlight recently added item: 1) Another page (e.g., product.html) redirects the user to cart.html with a URL parameter such as addedItemId=<cart_item_id>. 2) cart-page-controller reads the parameter and, after loading cart data via getCart, passes it to cart-recent-item-highlighter. 3) cart-recent-item-highlighter identifies the matching item row and applies a temporary visual highlight or scrolls it into view without altering the cart data.
interactions: On page load, cart-page-controller calls getCart and initializes cart state; if the cart is empty, cart-empty-state is shown instead of cart-items-list.
Clicking a product image or name in cart-items-list triggers navigation to product.html with the id URL parameter set to the product_id from the corresponding item.

Editing the quantity input or using increment/decrement controls in a line item triggers onQuantityChange, which calls updateCartItemQuantity and then updates the visible line subtotal and order summary.
Clicking the remove item button in a line item triggers onRemoveClick, which calls removeCartItem; on success, the item is removed from the displayed list, and the order summary is updated.
Clicking 'Continue Shopping' triggers navigation to index.html, optionally preserving a returnTo or source parameter if present in the current URL.
Clicking 'Proceed to Checkout' in order-summary-panel triggers navigation to the checkout URL only if at least one line item exists in the current cart state.
If updateCartItemQuantity or removeCartItem returns an error, cart-feedback-banner displays the message from the API response; user can then adjust inputs or retry the action.
If the URL contains addedItemId or similar contextual parameters, cart-recent-item-highlighter uses this information to highlight the corresponding line item after the cart data has been loaded.
page_functionality.state_logic: The cart page state is primarily server-driven via getCart, with URL parameters used to control view context and non-persistent UI behavior. The base URL is cart.html with optional query parameters. Example parameters include: 1) addedItemId: a cart item identifier used to highlight a specific line after getCart has loaded; cart-page-controller reads this and passes it to cart-recent-item-highlighter, which searches the getCart.items array for a matching cart_item_id. 2) messageType and messageCode: optional keys that other pages can pass to indicate contextual messages (for example, indicating that an item was just added); cart-feedback-banner interprets these values and displays a non-conflicting, API-independent informational banner. 3) returnTo: used to preserve navigation context (for example, indicating that the user came from index.html or a category page); order-summary-panel and cart-empty-state can include this when linking back to index.html or other pages. The page does not rely on localStorage for any core functionality; all authoritative cart data is retrieved via getCart on load and after relevant mutations. When updateCartItemQuantity or removeCartItem is called, cart-page-controller uses the returned cart summary to update client-side state and, if necessary (for example, if item-level details might have changed), may trigger a subsequent getCart call to sync line-level data. URL parameters are read once on load (or on navigation), and any visual effects (such as highlighting a row) are applied without affecting the stored cart data.

components.functionality: Coordinates loading and updating of the cart, manages in-memory cart state, interprets URL parameters, and passes data and callbacks to child components.

Displays all cart items returned by getCart, including product image and name, configuration summary, fulfillment details, quantity controls, line subtotals, and remove actions. Delegates quantity updates and removals to the controller.
Summarizes personalization and configuration per cart item, such as engraving text, engraving names, event date, gift wrap selection, and notes, in a compact text format under each line item.
Displays fulfillment information per cart item, including whether it is shipped or store pickup and the associated shipping option details or store location information.
Renders an editable quantity input (e.g., numeric field with increment/decrement controls) for each cart item and validates values before triggering quantity updates via the controller.
Provides a remove action for each cart item that calls removeCartItem through the controller and updates the list upon success.
Shows aggregated pricing information (items subtotal, shipping total, order total) from getCart and provides primary actions to proceed to checkout or continue shopping.
Displays when the cart has no items, explaining that there are no products in the cart and providing a call-to-action to continue shopping.
Displays informational, success, or error messages triggered by updateCartItemQuantity, removeCartItem, or contextual URL parameters (such as messageType and messageCode).
Reads the addedItemId (or similar) URL parameter via the controller and visually highlights or scrolls to the corresponding cart item row after getCart data has been loaded.
Encapsulates calls to getCart, updateCartItemQuantity, and removeCartItem, normalizing responses for use by cart-page-controller and ensuring consistent error handling.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with three equal-width content columns (each roughly 4 columns wide) for the product cards. Content is centered within wide horizontal margins.
layout.layout_pattern: Follow the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom structure: a full-width dark navigation bar (existing global element), then a centered content band for the cart. Inside this band, create a primary two-column layout that still respects the three-card DNA: the left 2/3 (≈8 of 12 columns) is a vertical stack of image-first cart item cards; the right 1/3 (≈4 of 12 columns) is a tall order summary card. Above these, a compact cart-page controller and an optional feedback banner span the same content width. All content is centered with wide dark margins on left and right.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Follow the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom structure: a full-width dark navigation bar (existing global element), then a centered content band for the cart. Inside this band, create a primary two-column layout that still respects the three-card DNA: the left 2/3 (≈8 of 12 columns) is a vertical stack of image-first cart item cards; the right 1/3 (≈4 of 12 columns) is a tall order summary card. Above these, a compact cart-page controller and an optional feedback banner span the same content width. All content is centered with wide dark margins on left and right.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Maintain a full-bleed dark background. Constrain the main cart content to a max-width center band (~70–80% of viewport width), leaving generous side margins as in the mockup. Allocate most vertical space to the cart item area: each cart line card mirrors the mockup cards with ≈65–70% height for the image area and ≈30–35% for a white information panel. The order summary panel is a single tall card on the right, visually comparable in height to the stacked items area, with internal padding of 16–24px. Vertical separation between major page bands (controller, banner, main row) uses 48–64px; spacing between cards and list elements stays around 24–32px.
layout_strategies.content_density: Use a moderately spacious layout: cards have clear breathing room, with 16–24px internal padding and 24–32px gutters between cards and columns. The cart items list feels rich due to product imagery and multiple details (personalization, fulfillment), but each line is grouped into a single coherent card to avoid visual clutter. Controls like quantity and remove are visually compact but never cramped, separated by 8–16px.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow remains vertical-first then horizontal. The eye moves from the global nav down to the cart-page controller (page title / main cart controls), then to any feedback banner, then drops into the main content row where attention goes left to the cart items list first (primary decision area), then sweeps right to the order summary panel. Within each cart item card, the eye starts on the image, then naturally moves down into the overlapping white panel where headings, personalization, fulfillment, and controls are arranged from left-to-right and top-to-bottom. Any recent-item highlighting and banners subtly support this flow without overpowering core actions.

layout.overall_description: The cart page sits on a nearly-black, full-bleed background that evokes a workshop-like atmosphere, with the main content framed in a centered band. At the very top, the existing site navigation stretches edge-to-edge, slim but confident, with gold accents that echo the brand’s crafted feel. Below it, the cart-page controller appears as a horizontal strip centered on the page, spanning about 70–80% of the viewport width, leaving generous dark margins on both sides. Just beneath, a subtle feedback banner, when present, runs across the same width, visually separated by comfortable vertical spacing. The primary cart region is a single main row: on the left, an 8-column-wide stack of tall cart item cards, each with a dominant product image on top and a white, slightly overlapping text panel at the bottom; on the right, a 4-column-wide order summary panel stands like a single, solid card. The cards align neatly on an implicit 12-column grid, with consistent gutters between the left and right sections. Each line item card feels like a crafted object: rich imagery above, clean white content below, with bold italic headings and compact typography describing personalization and fulfillment. Quantity and remove controls sit neatly within this lower band, while subtle highlighting can draw the eye to the most recently added item. When the cart is empty, a centered empty state takes over the main content band, still honoring the generous side margins. Overall, the page feels organized, intentional, and handcrafted, with a strong vertical-to-horizontal reading pattern that mirrors the original three-card mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-page controller is positioned directly below the full-width navigation bar, centered within the main content band. It spans roughly 70–80% of the viewport width (aligned to the 12-column grid) and is horizontally centered, leaving wide dark margins on the left and right. Vertically, it sits with about 48px of space below the nav and about 24–32px above the feedback banner or main content. Visually, it presents as a low-height horizontal strip: on the left side (≈6–8 columns) sit the page title and any secondary cart-level details; on the right side (≈4–6 columns) are global cart actions (e.g., ‘Continue Shopping’, ‘Clear Cart’), right-aligned. Internal padding is about 16–24px on all sides, matching the card styling. The background of this controller can either be slightly lighter than the page background (a very dark gray) or appear as a clean, transparent band with text and subtle bottom border, so it feels integrated but distinct.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, participates in normal document flow immediately below the nav; horizontally centered with a max-width constraint matching the main content band.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-items-list occupies the left portion of the main content row, underneath the cart-page controller and any feedback banner. It spans approximately 8 of 12 columns (about 65–70% of the content band width) and runs from the top of the main row downwards, stacking multiple cart item cards vertically. Left and right edges align with the left boundary of the content band and the internal gutter to the order summary panel (≈24–32px gutter). Each cart item within this list is a tall card that mimics the mockup’s product card: a large image area takes up the upper 65–70% of the card’s height, while a white text panel overlaps the lower part of the image and forms the bottom 30–35%. Cards are separated vertically by 24–32px of space. The entire list scrolls with the page; if it becomes taller than the viewport, the order summary panel remains aligned to the top of the list so both feel like a unified row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content flow; aligned to a 12-column grid, occupying roughly 8 columns on the left of the main cart row.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within each cart item card, the personalization summary lives in the white text panel that overlaps the bottom of the product image. This panel spans the full card width and roughly the lower 30–35% of the card’s height. The personalization summary is positioned in the left half of this panel, just beneath the bold italic product name/heading. There is about 16px of padding from the left edge and 8–12px below the heading. The text is set in smaller, readable typography, forming a compact multi-line block that describes engraving options, custom text, or selected styles. The block typically occupies the upper-left quadrant of the white panel, leaving visual space to its right for price and controls. Enough vertical spacing (8–12px) separates it from the fulfillment summary below or beside it, keeping the information legible but cohesive within the card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contained entirely within each cart line card’s white text panel; follows the card as it stacks within the cart-items-list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The fulfillment summary is placed close to the personalization summary within the same white text panel at the bottom of each cart item card, but visually separated to clarify its purpose. It typically sits either directly beneath the personalization summary (stacked) or slightly to the right and below, depending on available width. In a wide layout, it can align to the left at the same x-position but appear one line lower, occupying the mid-left to mid-center area of the panel. Vertical spacing of about 8–12px separates it from the personalization text. It focuses on estimated delivery dates, pickup vs. shipping, and other logistics details, using a slightly lighter or subtler text tone. This section stays within the left 2/3 of the white panel, leaving the right side free for quantity and remove controls and pricing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contained within each cart line card’s white text panel; stacked beneath or near the personalization summary, in normal flow inside the card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quantity control is positioned in the lower-right area of the white text panel on each cart item card, visually balancing the descriptive text on the left. It aligns horizontally to the right padding edge (≈16px from the right edge of the card) and vertically near the bottom of the white panel, leaving about 12–16px of bottom padding. It may sit on the same horizontal line as the item price, with the price slightly left and the quantity control to the right, or directly beneath the price if vertical space is preferred. The control itself is compact but clear (e.g., minus button, numeric value, plus button), with 4–8px spacing between elements; the entire control area occupies about 1/3 of the panel width at most. This placement gives a clear action zone on the right side of each card, mirroring the mockup’s organized yet spacious composition.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the card’s white text panel; right-aligned and anchored to the bottom padding area, moving with the card in normal scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The remove item button is placed adjacent to the quantity control to form a cohesive action cluster. It sits either immediately below the quantity control with about 8px vertical spacing, or to its left with 8–12px horizontal spacing, depending on available width. In both cases, it remains in the lower-right quadrant of the white text panel, sharing the same right alignment and bottom padding (≈12–16px). The button is visually lighter than primary CTA buttons—possibly a text button with a subtle icon or a ghost-style button—so it doesn’t overpower the quantity control but remains easy to find. It stays within the rightmost 1/3 of the panel, balanced against the dense information block on the left.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline with the quantity control in the card’s action zone; right-aligned within the card and scrolling with the cart-items-list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order summary panel forms the right column of the main cart row, mirroring the proportions of a single tall card from the mockup. It occupies approximately 4 of 12 columns (about 30–35% of the content band width), aligned to the right edge of the centered content band. A consistent horizontal gutter of 24–32px separates it from the cart-items-list on the left. The panel’s top aligns with the top of the first cart item card, giving the impression of a single, structured row across the page. The panel has a distinct, slightly lighter card background (charcoal or deep gray) with 16–24px of internal padding. Inside, line items (subtotal, tax, shipping, total, checkout button) are vertically stacked with 8–16px spacing. A bold, high-contrast checkout button sits at the bottom of the panel, spanning nearly the full width. The panel’s height extends to accommodate content but generally remains visually comparable to the overall stacked height of several cart items, anchoring the page visually on the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and aligned to the right within the main content row; participates in normal scroll, with a fixed width relative to the content band (4-column grid span).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state appears when there are no items in the cart and replaces the cart-items-list and order summary panel within the main content band. It is centered both horizontally and vertically within the available viewport area below the cart-page controller, while still respecting top spacing (≈64px from controller) and bottom breathing room. The content (icon, heading, body text, and link/button back to shopping) is stacked vertically and aligned to the center of the page, occupying roughly the middle 40–50% of the content band’s width, leaving wide dark margins on both sides. The visual treatment echoes the mockup’s hero-like feel: a subtle illustration or icon centered at the top, with a bold message beneath, followed by supportive text and a primary action button. The surrounding space is intentionally open, leveraging the dark background to make the empty state feel calm and deliberate rather than barren.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered instead of the main cart row; centered within the content band, scrollable if necessary but typically fits within a single viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The feedback banner sits directly below the cart-page controller and above the main cart content row. It spans the same max-width content band (≈70–80% of viewport), centered with equal dark margins on both sides. Its height is relatively compact, roughly 48–64px, with 12–16px of vertical padding. The banner has a slightly lighter background color or a tinted stripe (e.g., muted gold or subtle red) depending on the type of feedback (success, warning, error), maintaining the rustic aesthetic. Text is left-aligned with an optional icon on the far left, while any dismiss or secondary action sits on the far right, forming a left-to-right layout consistent with the nav and controller. There is about 16–24px of vertical spacing between the bottom of the banner and the top of the main cart row so the banner feels clearly separated but clearly associated with the cart.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the document flow; appears as a transient band between the controller and main content when active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The recent-item highlighter is a subtle visual treatment applied to the most recently added cart item card within the cart-items-list. It does not occupy its own separate space; instead, it modifies the appearance of the selected card—e.g., adding a thin gold outline around the card, a soft gold glow at the top edge of the image area, or a delicate gold accent bar along the left edge of the card. This effect stays within the card’s existing bounds and respects the 24–32px gutter between cards. The highlight is strongest near the top of the card, aligning with how the eye first hits the image before moving downward, reinforcing the mockup’s vertical-first card reading. Over time or after interaction, the highlight might fade or reduce in intensity, but always without changing layout dimensions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Decorative overlay and style treatment bound to a specific cart item card; no independent spatial footprint, moves with the card during scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-api-adapter is a non-visual service component with no direct on-screen layout. It operates entirely behind the scenes, coordinating data between the UI components (cart-items-list, quantity controls, order summary, etc.) and the backend. It influences how and when visible components update but does not occupy or alter any physical space in the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: No visual or spatial behavior on the page; exists purely as a logical/functional layer.

name: Wishlist
title: Wishlist
description: View and manage saved products and their personalization details, remove items, and move configured items into the shopping cart.

core_features: Load the current wishlist using getWishlist and display all saved items with their product details and personalization configuration.
Show key personalization details for each wishlist item (engraving text, names, color/finish selections, event date, gift wrap, notes) based on the configuration returned by getWishlist.
Allow users to remove individual items from the wishlist by calling removeWishlistItem with the selected wishlist item ID.
Allow users to move a wishlist item into the shopping cart for purchase by calling moveWishlistItemToCart with the wishlist item ID and a chosen fulfillment type (shipping or store_pickup).
After successfully moving an item to the cart, present a clear option to navigate to cart.html to review the cart contents.
Allow users to click an item’s product image or title to navigate to product.html with the corresponding product ID for further customization or review.
Handle empty and error states for the wishlist (no items returned, or API errors) with functional messaging and actions such as navigating back to product detail pages or other site navigation.
user_workflows:
View saved wishlist items:
1) User navigates to wishlist.html (via header 'Wishlist' link or a 'View Wishlist' call-to-action from another page).
2) On page initialization, the page controller parses URL parameters for any contextual state (for example, highlightWishlistItemId or addedProductId).
3) The page calls getWishlist with no parameters.
4) When getWishlist returns, the wishlist-page-controller stores wishlist_id and the items array in page state.
5) wishlist-list renders each item row with product image, name, price snapshot, desired quantity, and personalization summary from the configuration object.
6) If URL parameters indicate a specific item (highlightWishlistItemId or addedProductId), the matching row is flagged in state so wishlist-item-row can visually highlight it.

Remove an item from the wishlist:
1) In the wishlist-list, the user clicks the 'Remove' action on a specific wishlist-item-row.
2) wishlist-item-row emits an event to wishlist-remove-confirmation-dialog to open a confirmation dialog bound to that wishlistItemId.
3) If the user confirms, wishlist-remove-confirmation-dialog calls removeWishlistItem with the selected wishlistItemId.
4) On success, the dialog notifies wishlist-page-controller via onRemoveSuccess, including the affected wishlist_id and updated item_count from the API response.
5) wishlist-page-controller removes the item from its local items list (or triggers a fresh getWishlist call) and updates any count display.
6) wishlist-feedback-banner shows a success message; optionally, the page updates the URL with a transient messageCode and lastRemovedItemId if the application wants state to persist on refresh.
7) If the last item is removed (item_count becomes 0), wishlist-list switches to its empty state view.

Move a wishlist item into the cart for purchase:
1) In the wishlist-list, the user clicks the primary 'Move to Cart' action on a wishlist-item-row.
2) wishlist-item-row emits an onClickMoveToCart event with the wishlist_item_id (and product metadata if needed for messaging) to wishlist-move-to-cart-handler.
3) wishlist-move-to-cart-handler determines the fulfillment_type to use (for example, 'shipping' by default, or 'store_pickup' if a dedicated control on the row was selected). shippingOptionId is passed as undefined or an empty value if no explicit shipping option is selected on this page, allowing the backend to apply a default option.
4) wishlist-move-to-cart-handler calls moveWishlistItemToCart with wishlistItemId, fulfillment_type, and shippingOptionId.
5) On success, the handler receives cart_id and cart_item_id and emits onMoveToCartSuccess to wishlist-page-controller.
6) Depending on backend behavior (move vs copy), the wishlist may already be updated; wishlist-page-controller either removes the item from local state or triggers getWishlist again to sync.
7) wishlist-feedback-banner displays a success message along with a 'View Cart' action. Clicking 'View Cart' navigates to cart.html (no additional parameters required).
8) If moveWishlistItemToCart fails, wishlist-feedback-banner displays an error message and the item remains in the wishlist.

Revisit and adjust a product from the wishlist:
1) User identifies an item they want to adjust and clicks the product image or product name link in a wishlist-item-row.
2) wishlist-item-row triggers navigation to product.html with the URL parameter id set to the product_id from getWishlist.
3) product.html loads where the user may adjust configuration and, if the product page supports it, re-save to wishlist or add to cart.
4) If product.html redirects back to wishlist.html (for example via a 'Back to Wishlist' or 'View Wishlist' link), it can optionally append URL parameters such as addedProductId to indicate which product was just affected.
5) On returning to wishlist.html, wishlist-page-controller reads addedProductId, finds the corresponding item in the items list after calling getWishlist, and highlights it in wishlist-list for contextual continuity.

Access wishlist after adding an item from another page:
1) On product.html or another page, the user saves an item to the wishlist.
2) The other page’s logic redirects or links the user to wishlist.html, optionally including a URL parameter such as addedProductId (the product_id of the newly saved item).
3) wishlist-page-controller parses addedProductId from the URL and then calls getWishlist.
4) After getWishlist resolves, wishlist-page-controller locates the first item whose product_id matches addedProductId and marks it as highlightedItem in state.
5) wishlist-list receives highlightedItem info and displays the corresponding row in a highlighted state so the user can immediately see the item they just saved.
interactions: Clicking on a wishlist item’s product image or title navigates the browser to product.html?id={product_id} using the product_id field from getWishlist.

Clicking the 'Remove' action on a wishlist item opens a confirmation dialog; confirming triggers removeWishlistItem with the selected wishlistItemId, and on success the item is removed from the in-memory list and the visible wishlist.

Clicking the primary 'Move to Cart' action on a wishlist item calls moveWishlistItemToCart with the item’s wishlist_item_id, a chosen fulfillment_type value (such as 'shipping' or 'store_pickup'), and a shippingOptionId if available, then displays a success or error message in the feedback banner.
Clicking the 'View Cart' call-to-action in the feedback banner after a successful move operation navigates directly to cart.html.

If the wishlist is empty, the list component shows an empty-state notice with a functional link or button (for example, to trigger standard site navigation back to product browsing) without making any additional API calls from this page.

Reloading the page preserves any contextual highlighting controlled by URL parameters (such as highlightWishlistItemId or addedProductId) because wishlist-page-controller re-reads the URL and reapplies highlighting after each getWishlist call.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Wishlist page relies on getWishlist for primary data and uses URL parameters (and in-memory state) to manage contextual behavior. No localStorage or other client-side persistent storage is used.

Recognized URL parameters:
- highlightWishlistItemId: Optional. If present, after getWishlist returns, the controller searches the items array for a wishlist item whose wishlist_item_id matches this value. If found, it marks that item as highlightedItemId in state so wishlist-item-row can render it as highlighted. This parameter is purely for UI context and does not affect API calls.
- addedProductId: Optional. If present (for example, when coming from a 'View Wishlist' link after saving an item on product.html), the controller searches the items list for the first item with a matching product_id. The matching row is marked as highlightedItemId in state. If both highlightWishlistItemId and addedProductId are present, highlightWishlistItemId takes precedence.
- messageCode: Optional. Used to encode a simple status message key (for example, indicating a prior successful remove or move that occurred through a redirect-based flow). When present, wishlist-feedback-banner reads this code and maps it to a UI message. Once rendered, the controller may choose to update the URL (for example, via history.replaceState) to remove messageCode so messages do not persist indefinitely.

Page state (in-memory only) includes: loading flags for getWishlist, removeWishlistItem, and moveWishlistItemToCart; the current wishlist_id and items array; highlightedItemId; any transient error or success messages; and information about the last cart_id and cart_item_id returned from moveWishlistItemToCart. All operations that modify wishlist contents depend solely on the assigned interfaces and the latest API responses, not on any hardcoded or cached data.

Navigation is strictly parameter-based: product navigation uses product.html?id={product_id}, and cart navigation uses cart.html with no required additional parameters. Any cross-page workflow that needs to carry context back to Wishlist must do so via URL parameters as described above.

components.functionality: Initializes the Wishlist page, coordinates API calls, parses URL parameters, manages in-memory state (loading, errors, items, highlighting), and passes data and callbacks to child components.
event_handlers: onInit: Triggered on page load; parses URL parameters (highlightWishlistItemId, addedProductId, messageCode) and calls getWishlist.

onWishlistLoaded: Handles the successful response from getWishlist by storing wishlist_id and items, computing any derived data (such as total item count), and determining highlightedItemId based on URL parameters.
onWishlistLoadError: Stores and exposes error state if getWishlist fails so wishlist-feedback-banner and wishlist-list can display error information and retry options.

onItemRemoved: Invoked after wishlist-remove-confirmation-dialog reports a successful removeWishlistItem call; updates the local items list or triggers getWishlist again, and may set messageCode in the URL or in local state.

onItemMovedToCart: Invoked after wishlist-move-to-cart-handler reports a successful moveWishlistItemToCart call; updates the local items list (if the item was removed server-side) via direct mutation or a fresh getWishlist call, stores the returned cart_id and cart_item_id, and sets a success message for wishlist-feedback-banner.
onUrlParamsChange: Reacts to changes in URL parameters (for example, when the application updates messageCode via history APIs) to refresh highlighting or messages without reloading the page.

components.functionality: Displays high-level wishlist information such as total number of items and any active contextual messages, and provides a one-click navigation action to view the shopping cart.
event_handlers: onViewCartClick: Navigates directly to cart.html when the user clicks the 'View Cart' action.
onRetryLoadClick: Optionally exposed if getWishlist previously failed; calls back into wishlist-page-controller.onInit or a dedicated reload method to re-trigger getWishlist.

components.functionality: Renders the list of wishlist items returned by getWishlist, including empty and error states, and delegates item-specific actions to wishlist-item-row components.
event_handlers: onItemClick: When a user interacts with an item row (excluding action buttons), relays events such as requests to navigate to product.html?id={product_id} to the router or navigation subsystem.

onEmptyStatePrimaryActionClick: When the wishlist has no items, the component can expose a primary action (for example, to navigate to a generic browsing or home page); this emits an event to the page controller to perform the actual navigation.
onItemRemoveClick: Forwards a remove action from a specific item row to wishlist-remove-confirmation-dialog, passing the relevant wishlist_item_id.
onItemMoveToCartClick: Forwards a move-to-cart action from a specific item row to wishlist-move-to-cart-handler with the appropriate wishlist_item_id and the selected fulfillment context (if any).

components.functionality: Represents a single wishlist item. Shows product image and name, unit_price_snapshot, desired_quantity, category name, and a concise summary of personalization fields (such as engraving_text, engraving_names, color_selection, wood_finish_selection, ink_color_selection, size_label_snapshot, capacity_oz_snapshot, event_date_text, gift_wrap_selection, and personalization_notes). Provides actions to navigate to the product details, move the item to cart, and remove the item from the wishlist.
event_handlers: onClickProductLink: Navigates to product.html?id={product_id} when the image or product name is clicked, using the product_id from the item data.

onClickMoveToCart: Emits an event (with wishlist_item_id and any local fulfillment selection, if present) to wishlist-list or directly to wishlist-move-to-cart-handler to initiate moveWishlistItemToCart.
onClickRemove: Emits an event (with wishlist_item_id) to wishlist-list or directly to wishlist-remove-confirmation-dialog to start the removeWishlistItem flow.
onHighlightStateChange: Responds to a highlighted flag from the page controller (for example, when wishlist_item_id matches highlightedItemId) to adjust its rendered state accordingly.

components.functionality: Asks the user to confirm removal of a selected wishlist item and, if confirmed, executes the removeWishlistItem API call. Handles loading, success, and error states for the removal operation.
event_handlers: onOpen: Triggered when an item’s 'Remove' action is selected; binds the dialog to the chosen wishlistItemId and displays confirmation content.
onConfirm: When the user confirms, calls removeWishlistItem with the bound wishlistItemId, sets a local loading state, and awaits the API response.
onCancel: Closes the dialog without calling removeWishlistItem if the user cancels.
onRemoveSuccess: On successful API response, emits an event to wishlist-page-controller.onItemRemoved, passing the wishlist_id and item_count from the API so the parent can update state.
onRemoveError: Handles and surfaces any error from removeWishlistItem, allowing the dialog or wishlist-feedback-banner to show an error message and leaving the item unchanged in the visible list.

components.functionality: Encapsulates the logic to move a wishlist item into the shopping cart using moveWishlistItemToCart. It prepares the API parameters (wishlistItemId, fulfillment_type, and shippingOptionId), manages loading and error states, and notifies the page controller upon success so the UI and wishlist list can be updated.
event_handlers: onExecuteMoveToCart: Invoked when a user triggers 'Move to Cart' from a wishlist-item-row; receives wishlist_item_id and any selected fulfillment context, then calls moveWishlistItemToCart with wishlistItemId, a fulfillment_type value (such as 'shipping' or 'store_pickup'), and a shippingOptionId when available or an empty/undefined value when not.

onMoveToCartSuccess: After a successful API response, emits an event to wishlist-page-controller.onItemMovedToCart including cart_id, cart_item_id, and wishlist_id; it may also clear any local loading and error states.

onMoveToCartError: Captures errors from moveWishlistItemToCart, stores error details, and signals wishlist-feedback-banner or the invoking row so that an appropriate error message can be shown without altering the wishlist contents.

onFulfillmentTypeChange: If the UI allows toggling between shipping and store_pickup at the item level, this handler updates the selected fulfillment_type in local state before the user confirms the move.

components.functionality: Displays transient success or error messages related to wishlist operations such as loading, item removal, and moving items to the cart. It can also expose a 'View Cart' call-to-action after a successful move to cart.
event_handlers: onShowMessage: Triggered by wishlist-page-controller when there is a status message to display, including messages derived from messageCode URL parameters or from API responses.
onDismiss: Allows the user to dismiss the banner, clearing its message from in-memory state. The controller may also remove any corresponding messageCode URL parameter when this occurs.
onViewCartClick: If the current message relates to a successful moveWishlistItemToCart call, this handler navigates directly to cart.html when the user clicks 'View Cart'.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with three equal-width content columns (each roughly 4 columns wide) for the product cards. Content is centered within wide horizontal margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a vertical, top-to-bottom structure: global navigation (outside this scope) at the very top, followed immediately by an optional feedback banner, then a compact wishlist toolbar acting as the page's 'hero control band,' and finally a large central content band where wishlist items are displayed as a 3-column grid of product cards. Each wishlist item card mirrors the mockup’s pattern: a tall product image on top with a white text panel overlapping the lower portion of the image.
