layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a vertical, top-to-bottom structure: global navigation (outside this scope) at the very top, followed immediately by an optional feedback banner, then a compact wishlist toolbar acting as the page's 'hero control band,' and finally a large central content band where wishlist items are displayed as a 3-column grid of product cards. Each wishlist item card mirrors the mockup’s pattern: a tall product image on top with a white text panel overlapping the lower portion of the image.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The background is full-bleed dark, but all wishlist content is constrained to a centered max-width band (roughly 70–80% of the viewport, aligned to an implicit 12-column grid). The toolbar and list share this central band. Vertically, the global nav occupies a shallow slice, the feedback banner and toolbar remain compact, and the main vertical space is dedicated to the wishlist card grid. Within each card, roughly 65–70% of the height is allocated to the product image, and 30–35% to the overlapping white text panel. Generous side margins of dark background frame the content.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is moderate. The toolbar is compact but well-spaced, with clear separation between title and controls. The wishlist cards are visually rich due to large photos but are arranged in a single grid with only three columns, maintaining openness. Card gutters are around 24–32px, section separations are 32–48px, and internal padding in panels is 16–24px, consistent with the spacing system.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves from the global nav down to any active feedback banner (a gold accent band that briefly steals attention), then to the wishlist toolbar title on the left and its controls on the right, and finally drops into the wishlist grid. Within the grid, attention flows left-to-right across the three large cards in the first row, then continues downward row by row. Inside each card, the vertical flow goes from the product image to the overlapping white text panel, then horizontally across the primary action (Move to Cart) and secondary actions (like remove). When open, the confirmation dialog interrupts this flow as a centered focal point.

layout.overall_description: The Wishlist page sits on a nearly-black, full-bleed background with subtle artisan/woodshop character. At the very top is the site’s global navigation bar (not defined here), and directly beneath it, within the same dark backdrop, a slim gold-toned feedback banner may appear, centered within the content width. Below that, the wishlist toolbar forms a compact, horizontally aligned strip that anchors the page: the 'Wishlist' title and item count sit on the left, with filters, view toggles, and bulk actions aligned to the right, all within a max-width central band that leaves generous dark margins to either side. Under the toolbar, a comfortable vertical gap opens into the main wishlist grid: rows of three tall product cards, each centered, evenly spaced, and aligned to an implicit 12-column grid. Each card presents a large product photo occupying the upper two-thirds, with a white text panel overlapping the lower third, housing personalization details and call-to-action buttons. This structure creates a strong central gallery framed by negative space. When the user triggers a remove action, the screen subtly darkens and a white confirmation dialog appears centered over the grid, temporarily becoming the primary focus. Move-to-cart operations show inline loading states directly on the relevant card, while success or error messages surface in the feedback banner above, maintaining the vertical-first, then left-to-right visual rhythm of the mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist-page-controller acts as the structural wrapper for all wishlist-specific content, sitting directly below the full-width global navigation bar. It spans the entire viewport width but constrains its inner content to a centered max-width band (approximately 1200–1280px, aligned to an implicit 12-column grid). The controller establishes a full-bleed, nearly-black background that runs edge-to-edge, while the core content (feedback banner, toolbar, and wishlist list) sits in the middle 70–80% of the viewport, flanked by generous dark side margins. There is 48–64px of vertical padding from the bottom of the global nav to the top of the main content band (including space for the optional feedback banner), and similar padding at the bottom before the page footer, maintaining the spacious vertical rhythm seen in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level container that fills the viewport width, providing the layout grid and background for all child components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist-toolbar is positioned at the top of the content band managed by the page controller, directly beneath the feedback banner when present (or just below the nav padding when no banner is shown). It stretches horizontally across the centered max-width content area, occupying the full width of that band but leaving dark margins on the extreme left and right of the viewport. Vertically, the toolbar is relatively shallow (about 56–64px tall), with 16–24px of padding top and bottom. Inside, content is organized into two main zones on a single row: on the left, the page title (e.g., 'Wishlist') and a subtle item count, aligned flush with the left grid edge; on the right, controls such as sort dropdown, view toggle (if any), and bulk actions (e.g., 'Move selected to cart', 'Remove selected') are right-aligned to the grid. A subtle bottom divider line or tonal change separates the toolbar from the wishlist list below. There is a 24–32px vertical gap between the bottom of the toolbar and the first row of wishlist cards, preserving the open feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, non-sticky horizontal bar anchored to the top of the centered content band; it scrolls away with the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist-list occupies the main content area below the toolbar and above the bottom padding of the page controller. It is a vertically stacked collection of rows, each row containing up to three wishlist-item-row cards arranged in a 3-column grid. The list is centered within the same max-width content band, aligning to the 12-column grid such that each card spans roughly 4 columns, with 24–32px gutters between cards and at least 48–64px of vertical spacing between card rows. The grid’s left and right edges align visually with the toolbar’s content edges, maintaining consistent vertical columns. The dark background of the page shows between cards and around the grid, framing the white text panels and bright product imagery. When there are fewer than three items in the last row, the remaining cards are aligned left, preserving the grid alignment while leaving open space to the right, which contributes to the spacious, gallery-like feeling.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical grid container; it grows downward as more wishlist-item-row components are added, pushing the page height accordingly.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each wishlist-item-row is rendered as a tall product card within the 3-column grid of the wishlist-list. On desktop, a single card occupies approximately 1/3 of the content band width (about 4 out of 12 grid columns), with a fixed aspect ratio leaning toward vertical (e.g., 3:4), making it visually similar in height to the cards in the mockup. The top 65–70% of the card is a full-bleed product photograph with slightly rounded corners, anchored to the top edge of the card. Overlapping the bottom part of the image is a white text panel that occupies the lower 30–35% of the card, slightly inset from the card’s sides (e.g., 8–12px inset), creating a layered, floating effect. This panel has 16–24px padding on all sides. Inside the panel, the product name appears at the top in a bold italic style, taking one or two lines. Below it, a smaller, regular-weight block shows personalization details and configuration summaries, stacked vertically with 8–12px spacing. At the bottom or right side of the panel, primary actions are arranged horizontally: a gold-accented 'Move to Cart' button is visually dominant, with a secondary text or icon button for 'Remove' (or a trash icon) aligned to the far right. If a remove icon is overlaid instead, it can sit in the top-right corner of the card image area, offset inward by 8–12px. The card as a whole has a subtle elevation and shadow against the dark background, increasing slightly on hover, with a thin gold border or highlight to reinforce interactivity. Horizontal spacing between adjacent cards in a row is consistent (24–32px), and there is 32–48px vertical spacing between this card and the card directly below it in the next row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card element within the grid; it responds to user hover/focus with subtle visual elevation but maintains its fixed position within the row.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist-remove-confirmation-dialog appears as a modal overlay centered over the entire wishlist page when a user attempts to remove an item. A semi-transparent, nearly-black backdrop covers the full viewport, dimming the underlying nav, toolbar, and card grid while keeping their silhouettes faintly visible. In the center of the viewport, slightly above the exact vertical midpoint to feel more intentional, a white dialog card appears. On desktop, this dialog occupies roughly 30–40% of the viewport width, never exceeding the width of a single wishlist card row, and is sized to fit its content with 24–32px padding on all sides. The dialog’s width aligns visually with the content band center, leaving equal dark space on left and right. Inside, a concise heading (e.g., 'Remove this item?') rests at the top, followed by a brief descriptive message explaining the action. Below the text, primary and secondary buttons are right-aligned: a solid, gold-accented confirmation button (e.g., 'Remove') and a lighter, outlined or ghost 'Cancel' button to its left. The vertical spacing between heading, body text, and buttons follows the 8–16px scale. The dialog has slightly rounded corners and a shadow that lifts it clearly above the backdrop. It remains fixed in place even as a user scrolls, reinforcing its priority over the page content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed, centered modal overlay with a full-screen dimmed backdrop that disables interaction with underlying components until dismissed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist-move-to-cart-handler manifests primarily as inline state changes within the relevant wishlist-item-row rather than as a separate visible block. When a user initiates 'Move to Cart' on a specific card, the handler temporarily overlays the white text panel area of that card with a subtle, semi-transparent veil (either a faint dark or light tint) while keeping the card’s overall structure in place. Centered within this panel area, a small circular loading indicator and short status text (e.g., 'Moving to cart…') appear, aligned horizontally and centered both left-to-right and top-to-bottom within the panel’s bounds. The original button label is replaced by this loading state, and the buttons become visually disabled, but the card’s size, position, and its relation to neighboring cards remain unchanged. No global repositioning occurs; all activity stays localized to the affected card. Once the action completes, the overlay disappears, the feedback banner (if used) may update at the top of the page, and the card either transitions out of the wishlist grid or updates its state accordingly.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Local, inline overlay within individual wishlist-item-row components; it does not affect overall layout flow and remains fixed relative to its parent card while active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist-feedback-banner appears directly beneath the global navigation bar and above the wishlist-toolbar, nested within the same centered content band. Horizontally, it spans the full width of that band (aligned with the toolbar and wishlist grid), leaving the same dark margins on both sides of the viewport. The banner’s background uses a rich gold or warm accent tone, creating a strong visual contrast against the nearly-black page backdrop and drawing immediate attention without occupying excessive vertical space. Its height is modest—approximately 40–56px—with 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding. Inside, content is laid out in a single row: on the left, an icon (e.g., checkmark for success or warning for errors) aligns flush with the grid; to its right, the feedback message text runs toward the center, wrapping over one or two lines if needed. On the far right, a small close (×) icon or 'Dismiss' action is aligned to the right grid edge. A 16–24px vertical gap separates the bottom of the banner from the top of the wishlist-toolbar, preserving breathing room and maintaining the vertical rhythm. When the banner is not present, the toolbar moves up to occupy this space so that the rest of the layout remains structurally consistent.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block that appears and disappears at the top of the content band; when visible, it pushes the toolbar and rest of the content downward but does not overlay them.

name: Corporate & Bulk Orders
title: Corporate & Bulk Orders
description: Page for business and bulk buyers to browse bulk-eligible product categories, view products suitable for large orders, and navigate to product detail or bulk quote request flows.

core_features: Load and display all bulk-order categories using getBulkOrderCategories.
Allow users to select a bulk category, updating the URL bulkCategoryId parameter and the currently active category state.
Load and display bulk-orderable products for the selected category using getBulkOrderProducts.
Provide navigation from each bulk product to its product detail page (product.html?id=...).
Provide navigation from each bulk product directly to a bulk quote request page (bulk_quote.html) with productId and an optional quantityRequested derived from user input.
Handle loading, empty, and error states for both category and product data without relying on localStorage.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Browse bulk-eligible categories
1. User opens bulk_orders.html, optionally with a bulkCategoryId URL parameter.
2. Page calls getBulkOrderCategories to retrieve all bulk-order categories.
3. The category list is displayed. If bulkCategoryId matches a returned category_id, that category is marked as selected. If not, no category is selected initially.
4. If a valid category is selected (either from the URL or by default logic), the page calls getBulkOrderProducts with that categoryId.
5. The user sees a list of bulk-eligible products for the selected category.

Workflow: Change category from within the page
1. User clicks on a category in the category selector.
2. The clicked category’s category_id becomes the selectedBulkCategoryId in page state.
3. The page updates the browser URL to bulk_orders.html?bulkCategoryId={selectedBulkCategoryId} (using navigation or history APIs).
4. The page calls getBulkOrderProducts with categoryId = selectedBulkCategoryId.
5. The current category’s products list is updated to show products for the new category.

Workflow: Navigate to a product detail page from bulk listing
1. User reviews the list of bulk-eligible products for the current category.
2. User clicks the product name or image in a product tile.
3. The page navigates to product.html?id={productId} using the product_id from the getBulkOrderProducts response.
4. On product.html, the user can configure engraving and review more detailed information.

Workflow: Request a bulk quote starting from the bulk orders page
1. In the bulk product list, user locates a product that is marked as bulk-order-available (is_bulk_order_available true).
2. User enters a desired quantity in a quantity input field associated with that product (optional, may be left blank depending on implementation rules).
3. User activates the 'Request Bulk Quote' control for that product.
4. The page reads the productId from the product tile and the quantity from the associated quantity input.
5. The page navigates to bulk_quote.html?productId={productId}&quantityRequested={quantityFromInput} (quantityRequested included only if a value is available according to implementation rules).
6. On bulk_quote.html, the user can complete and submit their bulk quote request.

Workflow: Handle empty or error states
1. If getBulkOrderCategories fails, the page displays an error message component with a retry action that re-calls getBulkOrderCategories.
2. If getBulkOrderCategories succeeds but no categories have has_bulk_products true, the page shows an informational empty state for categories and does not call getBulkOrderProducts.
3. If getBulkOrderProducts is called for a selected category and returns total_count = 0 or an empty products array, the page shows an empty products state for that category.
4. If getBulkOrderProducts fails, the page shows an error message for the products area, with a retry action that re-calls getBulkOrderProducts using the current selected category.
interactions: Category selection: Clicking on a category item sets that category as selected, updates the URL bulkCategoryId parameter, and triggers a new getBulkOrderProducts call.
Category re-selection via URL: Manually changing the bulkCategoryId parameter in the URL and reloading the page causes the initializer to select the matching category and fetch products for it.
Product tile click: Clicking the product title or image in a bulk product tile navigates to product.html with the corresponding product_id mapped to the id query parameter.

Bulk quote CTA click: In each product tile, submitting the bulk quote control reads the associated product_id and user-entered quantity, then navigates to bulk_quote.html with productId and quantityRequested parameters.
Retry actions: Clicking retry controls in error components re-invokes the appropriate interface (getBulkOrderCategories or getBulkOrderProducts) using the current state (e.g., selected category).
Category description view: Selecting a category updates a category description panel with the description text from getBulkOrderCategories for the currently active category.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary URL-driven state is the bulkCategoryId query parameter. On page load, the page parses bulkCategoryId from the URL. After calling getBulkOrderCategories, the page attempts to find a category whose category_id matches bulkCategoryId. If found, that category becomes the active selection and getBulkOrderProducts is called with categoryId set to that category_id. If no matching category_id is found, the page can either leave the category unselected (requiring user input) or apply a default selection based on the returned list; any default selection should be reflected by updating the URL to include the chosen bulkCategoryId. When a user selects a different category from the UI, the page updates the URL bulkCategoryId parameter to the new category_id and refreshes the products list by calling getBulkOrderProducts with that categoryId. The selected category in UI, the current products list, and the user-entered quantity in each bulk quote CTA are treated as in-memory UI state and are not persisted in localStorage. Navigation to product.html and bulk_quote.html is performed using query parameters derived exclusively from the data returned by getBulkOrderProducts and user inputs on this page.

components.functionality: On page load, parse URL parameters, fetch bulk categories, determine the initially selected category based on bulkCategoryId, and initiate the first products fetch if a valid category is selected.
Display all bulk-order categories and allow the user to select a category for viewing its bulk-eligible products.
Show the name and description of the currently selected bulk category, helping users understand the types of items suited for bulk orders in that category.
Call getBulkOrderProducts whenever the active category changes and store the resulting product list and total count in page state.
Render the list of bulk-eligible products for the selected category, including product image, name, pricing information, and bulk-availability indicator.
For a single product, display its key details (name, image, base price, pricing range if available, and category_name) and provide actions to view the product detail or start a bulk quote.
Capture an optional quantity for a selected product and navigate to the bulk quote page with productId and quantityRequested parameters when submitted.
Display a loading indicator while getBulkOrderCategories is in progress.
Display a loading indicator while getBulkOrderProducts is in progress for the selected category.
Show an error message and a retry control when loading categories fails.
Show an error message and a retry control when loading products for the selected category fails.
Inform the user when no bulk-eligible products are returned for the currently selected category and optionally suggest trying another category.
Inform the user when there are no categories with bulk products available, based on the results from getBulkOrderCategories.
Keep the selected category in sync with the bulkCategoryId URL parameter by reading it on load and updating it when the user changes categories.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with three equal-width content columns (each roughly 4 columns wide) for the product cards. Content is centered within wide horizontal margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom structure: global full-width navigation on top (outside this spec), then a centered content band. Inside that band, a short hero-like introductory strip (category summary + inline bulk quote CTA) sits first, followed by the main browsing region. The main region is a two-column layout: a left filter sidebar for bulk categories and a right 3-column product grid. The product grid uses the same tall card pattern as the mockup: photo-dominant cards with overlapping white text panels, arranged in rows of three within a constrained central width.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom structure: global full-width navigation on top (outside this spec), then a centered content band. Inside that band, a short hero-like introductory strip (category summary + inline bulk quote CTA) sits first, followed by the main browsing region. The main region is a two-column layout: a left filter sidebar for bulk categories and a right 3-column product grid. The product grid uses the same tall card pattern as the mockup: photo-dominant cards with overlapping white text panels, arranged in rows of three within a constrained central width.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Horizontally, the main content band is centered and spans roughly 70–80% of the viewport width with generous dark margins on both sides. Within this band, a 12-column grid is used: the category filter sidebar occupies the left 3/12 columns (~25% of the content width), and the product grid occupies the right 9/12 columns (~75%), which holds three equal-width product cards per row. Vertically, the global nav occupies a shallow slice at the top; the hero/summary strip takes up a modest band just below it (~15% of the initial viewport height), while the product grid and sidebar together consume most of the remaining vertical space. Each product card reserves ~65–70% of its height for the image and ~30–35% for the overlapping white text panel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is moderately spacious. The hero/summary strip has ample breathing room above and below (around 48–64px total vertical separation from nav and the grid). The sidebar content stacks with 16–24px gaps between filter groups, and the product grid has consistent gutters of about 24–32px between cards horizontally and vertically. Card interiors use 16–24px padding in the white text panels. Overall, content feels rich but not crowded, with the dark background and wide side margins ensuring the layout remains airy.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye begins at the top-left with the global logo and nav, then flows along the nav to the right where any gold-highlighted cart or call-to-action resides. From there, it drops down into the centered hero/summary strip, starting at the bold heading on the left and moving toward the inline bulk quote CTA on the right. Then the user’s gaze flows down to the left filter sidebar (where they orient by category) and sweeps horizontally across the three-column product grid on the right. Within each product card, the eye first catches the large photo, then settles on the overlapping white text panel at the bottom, reading the bold italic heading followed by the descriptive copy.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s full-width dark navigation bar, the Corporate & Bulk Orders page opens into a wide, centered content band framed by generous nearly-black margins on both sides, reinforcing the modern craftsman aesthetic. At the top of this band sits a concise hero strip: a dark, slightly textured panel that stretches across the content width, with a bold, left-aligned heading and supporting copy introducing bulk and corporate orders, and a prominent, gold-accented bulk quote CTA aligned toward the right. Beneath this, the layout resolves into a clear two-column structure anchored on a 12-column grid: on the left, a vertically scrolling, dark-toned filter sidebar that lists bulk-eligible categories and options; on the right, a spacious product gallery. The gallery is a three-column grid of tall product cards, each dominated by large, richly lit photography on top and capped with a crisp white text panel that overlaps the lower edge of the image. The images take up about two-thirds of each card’s height, while the white panel, with its bold italic title and smaller description, occupies the bottom third, creating a strong visual rhythm across each row. Rows are separated by generous vertical gaps, and the entire grid remains comfortably inset from the page edges, so the content feels curated and premium rather than crowded. As users interact with filters, loading and error states occupy the same structural areas, preserving the overall layout and visual flow while clearly signaling data changes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This controller component is non-visual and sits logically at the top of the page structure, just below the global navigation in the DOM order but without taking up any visible space. It initializes data and state for the bulk ordering experience before the visible hero strip, sidebar, and product grid render. Visually, the layout remains unchanged whether this component is present or not; it simply orchestrates which of the other components appear in their designated regions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: No visual footprint; behaves as an invisible wrapper/controller that runs on page load and on URL changes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bulk category selector appears as a vertical filter sidebar anchored to the left side of the main content band, starting just below the hero/summary strip. It occupies approximately 3 of the 12 grid columns (about 25% of the inner content width) and runs vertically down the page alongside the product grid. Horizontally, it aligns with the left edge of the centered content area, leaving a sizeable dark margin between it and the browser edge to maintain the framed look. The sidebar has a subtly lighter dark background than the page (e.g., a soft charcoal) with 24px internal padding, and its filter groups stack top-to-bottom with 16–24px spacing between them. At full height, the sidebar visually forms a solid, stable left spine, with its top edge aligned slightly below the bottom of the hero strip, and its bottom edge extending at least as far as the product grid’s bottom, or until the viewport ends, whichever is shorter. On wide screens, it may be slightly shorter than the full viewport, but its structure clearly reads as a dedicated filtering column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop, the sidebar is vertically scrollable with the page but uses a sticky header zone inside it: its title and primary category options remain pinned to the top of the sidebar column once the user scrolls past the hero, with a top offset (e.g., 80px) to clear the global nav. On narrower viewports, the sidebar collapses into a horizontally scrollable filter bar or an accordion above the product grid, but still visually occupies a separate band above the cards.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category summary panel functions as a compact hero/intro section positioned directly below the global navigation and centered within the main content band. It spans the full width of the inner content container (about 70–80% of the viewport width), leaving equal dark margins on both sides. Vertically, it is a low, wide strip—roughly 120–200px tall—giving enough room for a headline, brief explanation, and associated controls without feeling bulky. The panel aligns to the same 12-column grid: the left 7–8 columns are reserved for a bold, left-aligned heading (e.g., “Corporate & Bulk Orders”), a brief descriptive paragraph beneath it, and any small category-specific notes. The right 4–5 columns hold the bulk-quote inline CTA, aligned to the right edge of the panel, creating a visual pull from left text to right action. There is 32–48px of vertical space between the bottom of this panel and the top of the sidebar/product grid below, echoing the generous separation seen in the mockup between nav and cards.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the document flow; it scrolls off-screen as users move down into the product grid. On long pages, it does not remain sticky, maintaining focus on the products once users start browsing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This data-fetching component is non-visual and lives conceptually around the product grid area, determining when the product list or its loading/error/empty states appear. It does not occupy its own visible region; instead, it controls what fills the right-hand 9-column product region beneath the hero and to the right of the sidebar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible and inline; it toggles the visibility of the product grid versus its loading/error/empty counterparts entirely within the defined product area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bulk products list forms the main product gallery to the right of the filter sidebar. It occupies the right 9 columns of the 12-column grid within the centered content band, starting on the same horizontal baseline as the top of the sidebar. Horizontally, it extends from the inner left boundary of column 4 to the inner right boundary of column 12, creating a broad content field that still leaves outer dark margins on each side of the page. Within this area, products are arranged in a 3-column grid, each product tile taking up an equal width (3 of the 9 columns, minus gutters). Between columns, there is consistent horizontal spacing of roughly 24–32px, and each row of cards is separated by 32px of vertical space. The first visible row of three cards visually echoes the mockup’s hero row: three tall, photo-forward cards aligned across the center of the page. As users scroll, additional rows continue downward, maintaining alignment and spacing, so the grid feels like a natural extension of that first hero-like row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The grid scrolls vertically with the page. It does not fix in place, but its top aligns just below the category summary panel with a comfortable gap. On narrower viewports, the grid responsively collapses from three columns to two, then to a single column, while preserving the tall card proportions and the photo-over-text pattern.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each bulk product tile is a tall, card-like unit within the 3-column product grid. In each row, three tiles sit side-by-side across the width of the product area, all equal in width and height, and consistently spaced. Within each tile, the upper ~65–70% is dominated by a large product photograph that stretches to the left and right edges of the tile. Around the lower quarter of this image, a crisp white text panel overlaps the image, extending downward to form the bottom ~30–35% of the tile’s height. This panel is slightly inset from the tile edges (e.g., 8–12px margin) to reinforce a card-within-card feel and avoid a harsh edge against the dark background. The white panel contains a bold, slightly italicized product name as a heading at the top, with 16px padding in all directions, and a smaller, regular-weight description line or two beneath. Additional bulk-specific details (like “MOQ 50 units” or pricing ranges) sit beneath the description in a slightly muted text color. The overall composition closely mirrors the mockup cards, with the white text block visually anchored at the bottom and photos pulling the eye first. The card’s background shadow and subtle border are minimal but help the cards float slightly above the dark background.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Each tile is a fixed-width, flexibly tall block that maintains its image-to-text height ratio. The overlapping white text panel is positioned relative to the tile so that it always partially covers the lower portion of the image as the card resizes, preserving the mockup’s distinctive layered look.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The inline bulk quote CTA sits within the category summary panel, aligning it with the hero content rather than burying it in the grid. It is positioned in the right portion of the panel, roughly occupying the rightmost 4–5 grid columns. Visually it appears as a compact, horizontally oriented element: either a gold-accented button or a short inline form (e.g., a small email or company-name input with a gold “Request Quote” button) aligned to the right edge of the summary panel. It sits vertically centered relative to the summary text on the left, or slightly below the heading but above the panel’s bottom edge. There is at least 16–24px of padding around it to keep it from feeling cramped, and enough empty space around to make it pop against the dark backdrop. This positioning creates a strong left-to-right flow: read the summary on the left, then act on the CTA on the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the summary panel. On smaller screens, it stacks below the summary text, centered horizontally, while still occupying the top section of the page above the filter and grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When bulk categories are loading, this state occupies the exact region reserved for the bulk-category-selector on the left side of the main content band. It appears as a skeleton or shimmer effect shaped like the filter sidebar: a tall, narrow column with several stacked placeholder bars that roughly match where category names and filter chips will appear. It spans the same 3 columns in width and starts at the same top position just beneath the hero/summary panel. The right-hand product grid area remains either in its own loading state or hidden until categories resolve, maintaining the main two-column structure but visually indicating that the left column is still preparing content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Confined to the sidebar column. It scrolls normally with the page and disappears completely once categories have loaded, handing off the space to the actual category selector.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The products loading state fills the product grid area to the right of the sidebar, using the same spatial footprint as the bulk-products-list. It presents three-column skeleton cards arranged in one or more rows, each mirroring the proportions of a real product tile: tall rectangles with a larger grey block on top suggesting the image and a shorter stacked block at the bottom indicating the eventual overlapping white text panel. These skeleton tiles are centered within the right 9-column space and respect the same horizontal and vertical gutters, preserving the visual rhythm of the layout while signaling data is incoming.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies and controls the right-hand product area beneath the summary panel. It scrolls with the page if the skeleton extends beyond the fold, and is entirely replaced by the real product grid once loading completes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When there is an error loading categories, this state replaces the content inside the left sidebar column, occupying the same 3-column-wide area beneath the hero. It appears as a compact error panel or message block aligned to the top of the sidebar region, padded by about 16–24px. The panel might have a slightly warmer or desaturated red accent line or icon at the left, with the error message text aligned to the right, all on a dark background that still matches the artisan aesthetic. Below this message, the rest of the sidebar column remains mostly empty dark space, making the error immediately noticeable without creating visual clutter.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the sidebar region. It scrolls normally with the page and is replaced by the actual category selector once the error is resolved or a retry succeeds.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The products error state occupies the same spatial region as the product grid: it is centered within the right 9-column area and appears just beneath the hero/summary and level with the top of the sidebar. Instead of tiles, a prominent error panel stretches across most of the product area (roughly 70–80% of its width), centered horizontally. The panel has a dark base with a subtle red accent line or icon, and error messaging text aligned left within it, with 16–24px internal padding. Any retry button or link sits on the right side of the panel, mirroring the left-to-right call-to-action emphasis seen in the hero and cards. Below the panel, the product area remains largely empty so the error panel dominates the space and is easily noticed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the product grid region. It scrolls with the page and is fully replaced by the regular product tiles once the error is resolved and data loads successfully.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When there are no products matching the current filters, this empty state takes over the right-hand product area, preserving the overall two-column layout. Instead of a full grid, a centered info panel appears within the 9-column space, starting below the hero/summary and aligned vertically near the top of the product region (with about 64px of top margin for breathing room). The panel is medium width—around 50–60% of the product area—centered horizontally, with a subtle white or light outline and 24px internal padding. Inside, a concise message explains that no bulk-eligible products were found, with optional guidance on adjusting filters. The surrounding space remains dark and unobstructed, echoing the mockup’s open feel and drawing attention to the panel as a calm, informative focal point.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the product grid region, scrolling normally with the page. It disappears entirely when matching products exist, yielding the area back to the tile grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: If there are no available bulk categories, this empty state fills the left sidebar column where filters would normally appear. It is positioned near the top of the sidebar area, below the hero panel, with 24px of top padding inside the column. The panel is narrower than the full sidebar width, leaving a small dark margin on either side, and features a light outline or subtle white surface with 16–24px interior padding. The message is concise, explaining that no bulk categories are currently available or eligible, and may suggest alternative options or a link to contact for help. The rest of the sidebar region remains minimally populated, preserving the clean, open aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the sidebar column; scrolls with the page. When categories become available, this panel is removed and replaced with the full category selector.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL sync component is non-visual and operates behind the scenes, tying the state of filters, selected categories, and pagination to the browser URL. It has no visible footprint on the page and does not alter the spatial layout of the hero, sidebar, or product grid. Its presence ensures that when users navigate or share links, the same combination of sidebar filters and product grid results is restored within the established layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible, with no spatial behavior; it simply listens to and updates URL/query parameters in response to interactions within the existing layout regions.

name: Bulk Quote Request
title: Bulk Quote Request
description: Page for requesting a bulk quote tied to a specific product and quantity, loading product context from URL parameters and submitting a detailed bulk quote form.

core_features: Read productId and quantityRequested from the URL and load product context using getBulkQuoteRequestContext.
Display a product summary section (name, image, price range) for the product being quoted.
Pre-fill and allow editing of the requested quantity for the bulk quote.
Collect company information, including a required company name field.
Capture budget information (optional numeric budget field, with currency implicitly handled by backend) and link it to the requested quantity.
Provide a selectable timeline option field wired to the timeline_option enum expected by submitBulkQuoteRequest.
Provide a large project details text area for describing engraving and project requirements.
Collect contact information (name and email) needed for the quote follow-up.
Perform client-side validation before calling submitBulkQuoteRequest and prevent submission when required fields are missing or invalid.
Submit the bulk quote request using submitBulkQuoteRequest and display success or error messages based on the response.
Maintain product and quantity context across validation errors and page reloads using URL parameters.
Offer optional navigation back to the home page from the confirmation state after successful submission.
user_workflows: Workflow 1: Accessing the bulk quote form from a product or bulk orders page: (1) User clicks a 'Request Bulk Quote' action on product.html or bulk_orders.html. (2) The site navigates to bulk_quote.html with productId and quantityRequested set in the URL. (3) On load, bulk_quote.html calls getBulkQuoteRequestContext with productId and quantityRequested from the URL. (4) When context is successfully received, the page displays the product summary and pre-fills the requested quantity field with either the URL quantityRequested or the default_quantity from the interface. (5) The bulk quote form becomes available for input.

Workflow 2: Filling out and submitting the bulk quote request: (1) User reviews the product summary and adjusts the requested quantity field if needed. (2) User enters a company name (required) and optionally a budget amount. (3) User selects a timeline option from a dropdown bound to the timeline_option enum values expected by the backend. (4) User enters detailed project and engraving requirements into the project details text area (required). (5) User enters contact name and contact email (as required by the business rules for submitBulkQuoteRequest usage on this page). (6) User clicks the submit button. (7) The page runs client-side validation; if any required fields are missing or invalid, inline error messages are shown, the page remains on bulk_quote.html with the same productId and quantityRequested context, and submitBulkQuoteRequest is not called. (8) If validation passes, the page calls submitBulkQuoteRequest with the current form values, including productId and the latest quantityRequested from the form state. (9) While waiting for the response, a submission-in-progress state is shown and the form is temporarily disabled. (10) When a successful response is returned (success = true), the page shows a confirmation panel that includes the backend message and may display the quote_request_id and status.

Workflow 3: Handling errors and re-entering data: (1) If submitBulkQuoteRequest returns success = false or a network error occurs, the page displays an error panel with the response message or a generic error message. (2) The form fields remain populated with the user’s input, and the user can correct any issues and attempt submission again. (3) If the user navigates away and comes back to bulk_quote.html with the same productId and quantityRequested URL parameters, getBulkQuoteRequestContext is called again and the form is reinitialized based on that context (no state is loaded from localStorage).

Workflow 4: Post-submission navigation: (1) After a successful submission, the confirmation panel provides a 'Back to Home' or similar CTA. (2) When the user clicks this CTA, the page navigates to index.html (without additional required parameters). (3) Optionally, the confirmation panel may offer a 'Request another quote' action that reloads bulk_quote.html with the same productId and a reset form state, preserving the product context via URL parameters while clearing form fields.
interactions: On page load, the page extracts productId and quantityRequested from the URL query string and triggers a call to getBulkQuoteRequestContext.
If productId is missing or getBulkQuoteRequestContext fails to return valid product_summary data, the page shows a context error state and disables the form submission button.
Users can change the requested quantity in the form; this updates the in-page quantityRequested state that will be sent to submitBulkQuoteRequest.
Typing into the company name, budget, project details, contact name, and contact email fields updates corresponding form state in real time.
Selecting a timeline option in the dropdown updates a timeline_option state property that is used when submitting the quote.

Clicking the submit button triggers client-side validation; if validation fails, error messages are displayed next to the relevant fields and the page remains at bulk_quote.html using the same URL parameters.

When validation passes, clicking the submit button initiates submitBulkQuoteRequest with the current productId, quantityRequested, companyName, budgetAmount, timeline_option, project_details, contact_name, and contact_email.
While submitBulkQuoteRequest is pending, the submit button and interactive fields are disabled or show a loading state to prevent duplicate submissions.
On a successful submitBulkQuoteRequest response (success = true), the page displays a confirmation panel using the response message and hides or locks the main form.
On a failed submitBulkQuoteRequest response (success = false) or network error, the page displays an error panel, keeps the form inputs intact, and re-enables the submit button so the user can retry.
Clicking the 'Back to Home' CTA in the confirmation panel navigates the user to index.html.

If the user manually edits the URL quantityRequested parameter and reloads the page, getBulkQuoteRequestContext is called with the new value, and the requested quantity field is pre-populated with that updated quantity.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state for bulk_quote.html is derived from URL parameters and API responses, not from localStorage. On initialization, the page reads productId and quantityRequested from the URL query string. If quantityRequested is absent, the page still calls getBulkQuoteRequestContext and uses the returned default_quantity from the interface to pre-populate the requested quantity field. The productId from the URL is always passed to getBulkQuoteRequestContext and submitBulkQuoteRequest. The in-page state includes: loading state for context (loading, loaded, error), product context (product_summary and default_quantity from getBulkQuoteRequestContext), form state (companyName, budgetAmount, quantityRequested, timeline_option, project_details, contact_name, contact_email), validation state (per-field error flags/messages), and submission state (idle, submitting, success, failure). Validation errors do not change the URL; the page remains on bulk_quote.html with the same productId and quantityRequested values. For flows that conceptually 'reload' the page due to errors, navigation simply keeps the user on bulk_quote.html and ensures the same productId and quantityRequested are present in the URL, so getBulkQuoteRequestContext would return identical context if the page is reconstructed. After successful submission, the page moves into a 'submitted' state but stays on bulk_quote.html; the confirmation panel is shown based on the submitBulkQuoteRequest result (success boolean, status, message, and quote_request_id). Optional navigation to index.html is triggered only by explicit user actions (e.g., clicking 'Back to Home') and not automatically by the submission. Any re-entry to bulk_quote.html must re-derive context via getBulkQuoteRequestContext using productId and quantityRequested from the URL.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by reading productId and quantityRequested from the URL query string and fetching product context via getBulkQuoteRequestContext. Manages loading, success, and error states, and exposes product_summary and default_quantity to child components.

Displays a concise summary of the product being quoted using product_summary from getBulkQuoteRequestContext, including product name, image, and price range if available. Shows the current requested quantity and allows the user to edit it; updates the in-page quantityRequested state that will be submitted.

Main form for collecting bulk quote details. Fields include companyName (required), budgetAmount (optional numeric), quantityRequested (bound to bulk-quote-product-summary state), timeline_option (required select), project_details (required multiline text), contact_name, and contact_email. Performs client-side validation and, on successful validation, calls submitBulkQuoteRequest with the current form state and product context.

Encapsulates the timeline selection logic for the bulk quote form. Renders a dropdown whose options map to the timeline_option enum values defined for BulkQuoteRequest (for example, values corresponding to general, flexible, specific date, and rush scenarios). Updates the form's timeline_option state when the selection changes.

Large text area for entering project details, including engraving requirements and any side-specific instructions. Updates the project_details field in the form state and participates in validation as a required field.

Displays feedback to the user after an attempt to submit the bulk quote. When submitBulkQuoteRequest returns success = true, shows a confirmation message using the response message, status, and quote_request_id. When success = false or a network error occurs, shows an error message. In success state, provides CTAs such as navigating back to the home page.

Controls the actual submission action for the bulk quote form. It is enabled only when required fields pass client-side validation and the context loader is in a loaded state. On click, triggers the bulk-quote-form's onSubmit handler, which calls submitBulkQuoteRequest. Shows a loading or disabled state while submission is in progress.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with three equal-width content columns (each roughly 4 columns wide) for the product cards. Content is centered within wide horizontal margins.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the mockup’s pattern of a full-width dark background with a centered content band. At the top of the content band, place a single hero row divided into three equal-width vertical cards (product context, timeline, status). Directly below that row, place one wide card that spans the full width of the content band for detailed project input and the primary submit action. All form-related pieces sit inside one overarching form container that follows this structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the mockup’s pattern of a full-width dark background with a centered content band. At the top of the content band, place a single hero row divided into three equal-width vertical cards (product context, timeline, status). Directly below that row, place one wide card that spans the full width of the content band for detailed project input and the primary submit action. All form-related pieces sit inside one overarching form container that follows this structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Keep the global nav as a shallow full-width bar at the very top. Below it, center the content band so it spans about 70–80% of the viewport width (max-width around 1120–1200px), with generous dark side margins. Vertically, the hero row of three cards occupies the upper half of the content band; each card is tall, with roughly 65–70% of its height dedicated to imagery or dark visual surface and 30–35% to a white overlay panel for text/controls. The lower full-width card for project details takes a similar or slightly greater height than the hero row, with comfortable padding and whitespace for typing.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a moderately spacious feel: 48–64px margin between the nav and the top of the hero row, 24–32px horizontal gutters between the three cards, and 32px vertical separation between the hero row and the lower details card. Within cards, use 16–24px internal padding and clear vertical rhythm between labels, controls, and helper text so the form feels substantial but not cramped.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Preserve the mockup’s vertical-first, then horizontal flow. The eye moves from the top nav down into the centered hero row, first reading the product summary on the left, then scanning across to the timeline select in the center, and then to the status panel on the right. From there, the eye naturally drops down into the wide project-details card, where the textarea draws attention first and then finishes on the strong, gold-accented submit button aligned toward the lower right.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a nearly-black, full-bleed background with subtle texture, framed by generous left and right margins that keep the content in a centered band. A slim, full-width top navigation bar anchors the page but doesn’t dominate; the main focus is the bulk quote request content directly beneath it. The primary content begins with a single hero row of three tall, evenly spaced cards aligned in the center: on the left, a product summary card with a rich product photo and overlapping white detail panel; in the middle, a timeline card that visually echoes the same photo-plus-panel structure but houses the delivery timeframe selector; on the right, a status card that shows the current state of the quote request within a similar card treatment. All three cards share equal width, consistent height, and identical gutters between them, reinforcing the 3-column grid. Below this row, separated by a comfortable band of negative space, a wider card stretches across the full width of the content band. This card contains the detailed project description textarea and the main submit button, with a clean white panel floating over the dark background and generous padding around the text field. The overall effect is a calm, workshop-like interface where the product and key quote parameters are immediately visible at the top, and deeper project details and submission happen in a clearly defined, prominent region just below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader sits logically at the very top of the central content band, just below the site’s navigation but above all visible cards. It spans the full width of the content band (matching the max-width used by the cards) but is visually minimal or invisible—either occupying zero height or showing only subtle skeleton states inside the areas where the product summary and form controls will appear. Its role is to gate the appearance of the three-card hero row and the lower form card so that those components render only once product and query parameters are loaded.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual structural component; effectively full-width but with no fixed height. It behaves statically in the layout, occupying the topmost position within the main content flow without overlapping other elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product summary appears as the leftmost card in the top hero row, aligned to the top edge of the main content band. It occupies roughly one-third of the content band’s width, with 24–32px horizontal gutter to the middle card and matching top and bottom alignment with the other two cards. Vertically, the card is tall, with a large product photo filling the upper 65–70% of the card. A crisp white panel overlays the lower portion of this image, extending across the full card width and covering the bottom 30–35% of the card height. This panel has 16–24px internal padding and contains the product name in a bold, slightly italic heading at the top, with smaller descriptive text (e.g., selected variant and quantity from the URL) beneath it. The card’s edges are slightly rounded, and it sits on the dark background with a soft shadow or subtle edge highlight to reinforce its card identity.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card within a 3-column grid, left-aligned in the hero row. On narrower screens, it should stack above the other hero cards while retaining the photo-plus-overlay proportion.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bulk quote form acts as an invisible structural wrapper for the interactive elements and cards rather than a separate visual block. It spans the entire central content band from just below the navigation bar down through the hero card row and the lower project-details card. Its left and right edges align with the outer edges of the cards, maintaining the centered max-width (about 70–80% of viewport width) and consistent side margins. Within this form boundary, the first visual section is the hero row of three equal-width cards (product summary, timeline select, submission status), followed by a 32px vertical gap and then a single, wide card containing the project details textarea and submit button. This structure ensures that all controls live within one coherent form, even though they are visually divided into multiple card segments.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, block-level container that defines a single form region encompassing all other interactive components; it scrolls naturally with the page and does not fix or float relative to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The timeline selector occupies the center card in the top hero row, directly to the right of the product summary card and to the left of the status card, with equal 24–32px gutters on each side. It is the same width and height as the neighboring cards, reinforcing the 3-column rhythm. The upper 60–70% of this card is a dark, textured or subtly illustrated area (e.g., a blurred workshop or clock motif) that visually ties into the site’s craft aesthetic. Overlapping the lower portion of this area is a white panel that spans the full width and roughly the bottom 30–40% of the card height. Inside this panel, the label (e.g., “Timeline” or “When do you need this by?”) appears at the top, with the select control directly beneath it. The control itself runs almost the full width of the panel with comfortable 16–24px padding from the edges, and there is a small amount of breathing room below for helper text if needed. The card’s baseline aligns precisely with the product summary and status cards, creating a clean, unified row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card in the center column of the 3-column grid. On smaller screens, it stacks under the product summary card but maintains its internal top-image / bottom-panel proportions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project details textarea is housed in a large, wide card directly beneath the hero row. This card is centered within the same content band as the row above and spans the full usable width (all three conceptual columns), with its top edge sitting about 32px below the bottom edges of the three hero cards. The card’s background is primarily white or very light, creating strong contrast against the nearly-black page background, and it may lightly overlap a faint dark or woodgrain strip at its top to echo the overlay motif from the hero cards. Inside, there is 24px padding on all sides. The label for the textarea appears near the top-left of the panel with comfortable spacing, followed by the textarea itself which consumes the majority of the card’s vertical space and stretches almost the full width, leaving consistent side padding. The textarea is tall enough to invite multi-line input (roughly 40–60% of the card height), with extra space below reserved for the submit button and any inline guidance or character hints. Overall, this card feels like the main writing surface of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width card placed directly in the vertical flow beneath the hero row. On mobile, it remains full-width (edge-to-edge within the content band) and stacks naturally after the hero cards.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submission status panel forms the rightmost card in the top hero row, mirroring the size and general structure of the product summary and timeline cards. Positioned to the right of the center timeline card, it shares the same top alignment and bottom baseline and is separated by a 24–32px gutter. The upper 60–70% of this card is a dark area with subtle texture or an iconographic illustration (e.g., a checkmark, progress indicator, or document symbol) that reinforces the notion of quote state. Overlapping the lower portion is a white panel that covers the bottom 30–40% of the card, with 16–24px internal padding. This panel displays a concise status message (e.g., “Draft”, “Submitted”, “In Review”) and may include a short explanatory line beneath. Because it sits at the far right of the row, it visually caps the hero section and provides a clear endpoint to the initial left-to-right scan.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card at the right edge of the 3-column hero row. On smaller screens, it stacks below the timeline card while preserving its image-top, panel-bottom layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submit button lives inside the same wide card as the project details textarea, anchored toward the lower-right corner of the white panel. It sits below the textarea with approximately 16–24px of vertical space separating its top edge from the textarea’s bottom edge. Horizontally, the button is right-aligned with the textarea’s right edge and set in from the card’s right padding by the same 24px margin, creating a clean visual column. The button itself is medium to large in size, with enough padding to feel substantial, and uses the site’s gold accent as the primary fill color against either a dark label or reversed white text, making it the most visually striking interactive element in the lower section. There is a small buffer of whitespace beneath the button (around 16–24px) before the bottom edge of the card, keeping it from feeling cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the project-details card; it remains docked to the lower-right of that card and scrolls with the page. On smaller screens, it can expand to near-full width within the card while keeping its vertical position below the textarea.

name: Gift Finder
title: Gift Finder
description: Page for finding giftable products based on occasion, budget, rating, and gift wrap options, and then navigating to product detail pages to personalize and purchase.

core_features: Load available gift occasions from the getGiftOccasionOptions interface and expose them as selectable filters.
Parse URL parameters (occasion, price_max, rating_min, free_gift_wrap_only, sort_by, page) to initialize the Gift Finder state.
Call searchGifts with the selected occasion, filters (price_max, rating_min, free_gift_wrap_only), sort_by, and page to retrieve paginated giftable products.
Render a filter form for budget, minimum rating, and free gift wrap availability, tied directly to URL parameters.
Render sorting controls bound to the sort_by URL parameter, allowing users to change the order of results.
Display a paginated grid/list of gift results from searchGifts with basic product information and gift wrap indicators.
Provide pagination controls that update the page URL parameter and request the corresponding result page via searchGifts.
Allow navigation to product.html with the selected product id so users can configure personalization, shipping, and gift wrapping on the Product Detail page.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Start a gift search by occasion
1. User navigates to gift_finder.html (from header/footer navigation or any link).
2. Page controller reads existing URL parameters (if any).
3. Page controller calls getGiftOccasionOptions and populates the occasion selector.
4. User selects an occasion from the occasion selector.
5. The occasion selector updates the occasion parameter in the URL (and resets page to the initial page) via navigation to gift_finder.html with the new query string.
6. On reload, the page controller parses the updated URL, builds filters from price_max, rating_min, free_gift_wrap_only (if present), and calls searchGifts with occasion, filters, sort_by, page, and page_size.
7. The results grid displays giftable products that match the chosen occasion.

Workflow 2: Refine gifts by budget, rating, and free gift wrap
1. After initial load, user sees the filter form with current values derived from URL parameters.
2. User enters or edits a maximum price in the price_max input field.
3. User selects a minimum rating in the rating_min control.
4. User checks or unchecks the free gift wrap only checkbox.
5. User clicks an Apply or similar control on the filter form.
6. The filter form constructs a URL query with occasion (current selection), price_max, rating_min, free_gift_wrap_only, sort_by (current selection), and page set to the initial page.
7. Browser navigates to gift_finder.html with the new query string.
8. On reload, the page controller parses the new URL and calls searchGifts with the corresponding filters; the results grid updates to show only products matching the refined constraints.

Workflow 3: Change sorting of gift results
1. With results already loaded, user opens the sorting control.
2. User chooses a sort option (for example, a higher-priority rating ordering or another enabled sort mode).
3. Sorting control updates the sort_by parameter in the URL (and resets page to the initial page) while preserving occasion and filter parameters.
4. Browser navigates to the updated gift_finder.html URL.
5. On reload, page controller calls searchGifts with the same occasion, filters, and the updated sort_by value; the results grid renders in the new order.

Workflow 4: Navigate through paginated results
1. User scrolls to the bottom of the results and sees pagination controls.
2. User clicks on a page number or a next/previous control.
3. Pagination component updates the page parameter in the URL while preserving occasion, filters, and sort_by.
4. Browser navigates to gift_finder.html with the updated page query parameter.
5. On reload, page controller calls searchGifts with the same occasion, filters, sort_by, and the new page; a new subset of products is rendered.

Workflow 5: Choose a gift and proceed to personalization
1. User reviews the product cards in the results grid, which show key information such as name, price range, rating, and whether gift wrap is supported or free.
2. User clicks on a product card that looks suitable.
3. Product card click handler navigates to product.html with the selected product id as a URL parameter.
4. On product.html, the user completes personalization, gift wrap configuration, and purchase steps (handled by the Product Detail page, outside the Gift Finder).
interactions: Selecting an occasion in the occasion selector updates the occasion URL parameter and triggers a new search via navigation.
Typing or editing a maximum price value updates local filter state; clicking the filter form's apply control writes price_max (or removes it if empty) into the URL and triggers navigation.
Selecting a minimum rating value updates local filter state; clicking apply writes rating_min (or removes it if unset) into the URL and triggers navigation.
Toggling the free gift wrap only checkbox updates local filter state; clicking apply writes free_gift_wrap_only as a boolean-like query value into the URL and triggers navigation.
Choosing a sort option updates the sort_by URL parameter immediately (or on an explicit apply action, depending on implementation) and navigates to the same page with page reset.
Clicking a pagination control updates the page URL parameter and navigates to the same page with the new page value.
Clicking a product card navigates to product.html with the product id as the id URL parameter, leaving all Gift Finder state in the gift_finder.html URL.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Gift Finder page uses URL parameters as the single source of truth for its state. On every load of gift_finder.html, the page controller parses the current URL search parameters:
- occasion: Treated as the currently selected occasion. If present and matching one of the values returned by getGiftOccasionOptions, the corresponding option is marked as selected in the occasion selector. If absent or not matching any available option, the occasion selector shows an unselected or default state, and searchGifts may be called with an undefined or neutral occasion value according to implementation rules.
- price_max: Parsed as a number if present and valid. If successfully parsed, it is used to populate the maximum price input field and included in the filters object passed to searchGifts. If missing or invalid, the input field is shown empty or with no constraint, and the corresponding filter key is omitted from the filters object.
- rating_min: Parsed as a number if present and valid. If successfully parsed, it is used for the minimum rating control and included in the filters object. If missing or invalid, the rating filter is treated as not applied and omitted from filters.
- free_gift_wrap_only: Parsed as a boolean-like value. If present and equivalent to a true value as defined by the implementation (for example a specific string representation), the free gift wrap only checkbox is rendered as checked and the filters object includes this flag as true. Otherwise, the checkbox is rendered as unchecked and the filters object either omits the flag or sets it to false.
- sort_by: Used as a sorting key. If present, it is bound to the selected value of the sorting control and passed through to searchGifts. If missing, the sorting control uses an implementation-defined default, and searchGifts is called with that default or without sort_by.
- page: Parsed as a number representing the current page index (1-based or as defined by the implementation). If missing, non-numeric, or out of range, the page value defaults to an initial page (such as 1). The page value is passed to searchGifts and also used in the pagination control.

After parsing URL parameters, the page controller calls getGiftOccasionOptions to populate the occasion selector and searchGifts with the following parameters: occasion (from URL or neutral), filters (object composed from price_max, rating_min, free_gift_wrap_only), sort_by (from URL or implementation default), page, and page_size (a constant or configuration value not controlled by the URL). The response from searchGifts (total_count, page, page_size, products) drives the rendering of the results summary, product grid, and pagination. Any user interaction that changes filters, occasion, sorting, or pagination results in an updated URL query string (via full navigation or history manipulation), which in turn re-runs this initialization and fetch logic. No persistent state is stored outside the URL and the in-memory state of the current load.

components.functionality: Coordinates URL parsing, data fetching, and distribution of state to all subcomponents on gift_finder.html. On initialization, it parses URL parameters (occasion, price_max, rating_min, free_gift_wrap_only, sort_by, page), calls getGiftOccasionOptions, validates the occasion against the returned options, then calls searchGifts with the derived filters, sort_by, and page. It exposes the fetched occasions list, current selection, filters, sorting, and results to child components via internal state or props. It also provides handler functions to child components that build updated URLs and trigger navigation when the user changes filters, sort, occasion, or page.

Displays a selectable list of gift occasions populated from getGiftOccasionOptions and allows the user to choose a single occasion. Reflects the current occasion state derived from the URL. When the user selects or clears an occasion, it delegates to the page controller to update the occasion URL parameter, reset the page parameter to the initial page, and navigate to the updated gift_finder.html URL, which triggers a fresh searchGifts call.

Presents filter inputs for maximum price, minimum rating, and a free gift wrap only toggle. Initial values are populated from the parsed URL parameters (price_max, rating_min, free_gift_wrap_only). The component maintains temporary local state while the user edits fields. When the user submits the form, it constructs a new set of filter values and calls the controller's apply handler, which updates the URL query (including occasion, price_max, rating_min, free_gift_wrap_only, existing sort_by, and page reset to the initial page) and navigates to trigger a new searchGifts call.

Allows the user to choose how gift results are ordered (for example by rating, price, or popularity as defined by the implementation). The selected value is synchronized with the sort_by URL parameter. When the user selects a different sort option, the component calls the controller handler to update sort_by in the URL, preserve occasion and existing filters, reset page to the initial page, and navigate so that searchGifts is called again with the new ordering.

Displays a summary of the current search results, including total_count from searchGifts, the current page information, and optionally a description of active filters and occasion. It updates whenever searchGifts returns new data or the URL parameters change. It does not initiate any data calls itself, but consumes the controller's derived state.

Renders the list or grid of giftable products returned by searchGifts. For each product result, shows fields made available by the interface (such as name, image_url, price information, category_name, rating_average, rating_count, supports_gift_wrap, has_free_gift_wrap, free_gift_wrap_label). Each item is clickable; clicking calls the controller's product selection handler, which navigates to product.html with the product id as a URL parameter. The grid updates whenever new searchGifts results are provided by the controller.

Uses total_count, page, and page_size from the searchGifts response to calculate available pages and render pagination controls (page numbers and/or next/previous). It highlights the current page and disables controls as appropriate when at bounds. When a user selects a different page, it calls the controller's page change handler, which updates the page URL parameter while preserving occasion, filters, and sort_by, and navigates to gift_finder.html so that searchGifts is called with the new page.

Centralizes reading and writing of URL query parameters for the Gift Finder. On initialization, it parses occasion, price_max, rating_min, free_gift_wrap_only, sort_by, and page from window.location, converts them into typed values, and exposes them to the page controller. It also provides helper methods for building updated URLs when other components request state changes. No state is stored outside the current URL; any update is performed by constructing a new query string and triggering navigation or history updates, which then reinitializes the page and reloads data via the controller.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with three equal-width content columns (each roughly 4 columns wide) for the product cards. Content is centered within wide horizontal margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s top-to-bottom structure: a full-width dark navigation bar (outside this component list), followed immediately by a centered hero-like band with a single row of three tall cards used for the occasion selector. Beneath this hero band, the page transitions into a two-column results area within the same centered content band: a narrower left filter sidebar and a wider right results column containing the sort control, results summary, product grid, and pagination. All content sits inside a max-width container centered on a full-bleed nearly-black background.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s top-to-bottom structure: a full-width dark navigation bar (outside this component list), followed immediately by a centered hero-like band with a single row of three tall cards used for the occasion selector. Beneath this hero band, the page transitions into a two-column results area within the same centered content band: a narrower left filter sidebar and a wider right results column containing the sort control, results summary, product grid, and pagination. All content sits inside a max-width container centered on a full-bleed nearly-black background.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The navigation remains shallow in height. The occasion selector band beneath it is visually tall and prominent, with each occasion card echoing the original product cards: about 65–70% image, 30–35% white text panel. Horizontally, the hero row of three occasion cards consumes roughly 70–80% of the viewport width, keeping generous dark margins at the sides. Below this, the results section shares that same max-width: the left filter sidebar occupies roughly 3 of 12 columns (~25–30%), while the right results column takes about 9 of 12 columns (~70–75%). Vertical spacing between major bands (nav → occasions → filters/results → pagination) follows the 48–64px rhythm; internal element spacing uses 16–24px paddings.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout stays moderately spacious: the hero occasion cards are large and breathing, with consistent gutters between the three cards and comfortable padding inside their white text panels.
