The filter sidebar groups controls with 16–24px vertical spacing and clear section breaks, avoiding a cramped toolbox feel. The product grid repeats the three-column card rhythm from the hero row with consistent gaps, ensuring a rich but not crowded presentation. Top and bottom margins around each major block maintain the airy, modern-but-rustic aesthetic of the mockup.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: User attention flows in a strong vertical-then-horizontal sequence mirroring the mockup. The eye starts at the top-left of the nav, sweeps right across nav items to any highlighted action (e.g., cart), then drops directly down to the hero occasion row where it scans left-to-right across the three tall cards. From there, the gaze moves downward into the results band: first hitting the results summary on the left and sort control on the right, then flowing down into the three-column product grid and finally to the centered pagination controls. The persistent dark background and repeated white card panels guide the eye through content bands with a clear hierarchy.

layout.overall_description: The Gift Finder page sits on a full-bleed, almost-black background that frames a centered content band. A full-width dark navigation bar caps the top, and directly beneath it, a hero-like strip presents three tall occasion cards in a single row, each with a rich photo on top and a white overlapping text panel at the bottom, echoing the mockup’s primary card pattern. This row spans roughly three-quarters of the viewport width and is centered, leaving generous dark margins at left and right that enhance the workshop-like atmosphere. Below a comfortable gap, the layout shifts into a two-column results area within the same central band: a slim, vertically oriented white filter panel on the left and a wider right column that houses the results summary and sort control in a shared top row, followed by a three-column grid of product cards that visually rhyme with the hero cards. At the bottom of this grid, centered pagination controls sit with ample breathing room. Throughout, cards and control panels use white or very light surfaces with subtle shadows and gold accents on interactive elements, maintaining a modern, artisan feel grounded in the mockup’s dark, craft-oriented design.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller acts as the structural container for all Gift Finder content below the global navigation. It defines a centered max-width content band (approximately 1200–1320px wide on large screens) sitting on a full-bleed nearly-black background. Horizontally, it centers all child components, ensuring that the occasion cards, filter sidebar, results grid, and pagination line up on the same vertical axis. Vertically, it sequences sections in a linear flow: first the occasion selector band immediately under the nav, then a generous 48–64px gap, followed by the filter/results area, and finally the pagination controls with similar bottom spacing. The controller enforces the underlying 12-column grid: three equal columns for hero cards across the full width of the content band, then a left column of roughly 3 grid units for filters and a right column of 9 units for results. It also applies consistent top and bottom padding (around 48px) inside the content band so that no component touches the viewport edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width structural wrapper that centers and constrains inner content; it does not scroll independently but defines the grid and max-width for all child components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The occasion selector is positioned as the hero band at the top of the page content, directly beneath the navigation bar and within the page controller’s centered max-width container. It appears as a single horizontal row of three tall cards that collectively span about 70–80% of the viewport width, with equal card widths and consistent 24–32px gutters between them. Each card is vertically oriented, with the upper ~65–70% of the card displaying a full-bleed photo (e.g., an engraved gift in context) and the lower ~30–35% occupied by a white text panel that overlaps the bottom of the image slightly, echoing the original mockup’s product card structure. The text panel contains a bold, slightly italic occasion name at the top and one to two short lines of supporting copy beneath, with 16–24px internal padding. The entire row is centered within the dark background, leaving broad dark margins on both sides that emphasize the cards. A comfortable 32–48px margin separates this band from the content below, helping it read as a distinct, visually primary selector area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main scroll; acts as a hero-like section. On smaller screens, the three cards can stack vertically or become a horizontally scrollable row while maintaining central alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The gift filter form sits to the left within the main results area, beginning just below the occasion selector and its separating vertical gap. Within the 12-column grid, it occupies approximately 3 columns (about 25–30% of the content band width). The filter form is presented as a tall, white (or very light) vertical panel with subtle shadow, clearly separated from the dark background behind it. It aligns flush to the left edge of the centered content band, with its top edge horizontally aligned to the top of the results summary and sort controls on the right. Inside the panel, filter groups (occasion refinement, budget range, rating, gift wrap options, etc.) are stacked top-to-bottom with 16–24px vertical spacing between groups and 8–16px spacing between controls within a group. The panel has 16–24px padding on all sides so no control touches its edges. Its height extends down roughly to match or slightly exceed the height of the visible product grid, giving a balanced left column. A small 24–32px horizontal gutter separates the filter panel from the results column to the right, keeping the layout airy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky relative to the viewport on larger screens: once the user scrolls past the hero occasion row, the filter sidebar stays pinned near the top of the viewport while its content scrolls with the page, up until the footer. On small screens, it becomes a collapsible panel that precedes the results grid in the vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The gift sort control is placed in the top-right area of the results column, directly to the right of the filter sidebar and horizontally aligned with the top of the filter panel. Within the right 9 columns of the grid, it sits in the same horizontal row as the results summary but aligned to the far right, creating a balanced bar across the top of the grid. This bar spans nearly the full width of the results column, with the summary text on the left and the sort dropdown or buttons anchored on the right. Vertical spacing above this bar is around 16–24px from the bottom of the occasion selector band, and a 16–24px gap separates this bar from the product grid below. The sort control itself uses a compact but uncrowded design: a dropdown or segmented control with 12–16px horizontal padding and clear gold-accented focus/active states, sitting on a dark or subtly toned micro-surface that does not compete visually with the larger white cards.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the results column; it scrolls with the content. On narrow screens, it stacks above or below the results summary, typically full-width and centered or left-aligned.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results summary appears at the top of the right-hand results column, immediately under the occasion selector band and aligned horizontally with the gift sort control. It is placed on the left side of this shared top row, occupying roughly the left half to two-thirds of the row’s width, with the sort control occupying the remaining right-aligned space. The summary is presented as text on the dark background (or a very subtle low-contrast strip) rather than a full white card, to keep visual attention on the product cards below. It might read something like “24 gifts for Anniversaries under $100” using a single line or two lines of text, with 8–16px vertical padding around it to avoid feeling cramped. A 16–24px vertical gap separates this summary row from the top edge of the product grid, maintaining the moderate, open spacing characteristic of the mockup. The summary’s left edge aligns visually with the left edges of the product cards below, reinforcing the grid structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static text element within the results column, scrolling with the grid. On smaller screens, it centers or left-aligns above the sort control, maintaining clear separation with modest vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The gift results grid occupies the majority of the right-hand column beneath the summary/sort row. It spans roughly 9 grid columns, starting just to the right of the filter sidebar, and mirrors the mockup’s three-column card rhythm: each row contains three product cards of equal width, separated by consistent horizontal gutters of about 24–32px. Each product card visually echoes the hero occasion cards: the top ~65–70% of the card height is a large product image, while the bottom ~30–35% is a white text panel overlapping the lower portion of the image. Within the text panel, a bold (potentially italic), medium-sized product name sits on top, with a smaller descriptive line, price, rating, and key tags (e.g., “Gift wrap available”) stacked below with 8–12px spacing. Cards include 16–24px internal padding in the text area. Vertically, rows of cards are separated by 32–40px of space, keeping the grid rich but not dense. The entire grid is centered within the results column, aligning its left edge with the results summary and its right edge with the sort control, and it extends downward until the available products for the current page are displayed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static grid within the scrollable page. On medium screens, the grid may collapse to two columns while maintaining equal card width and consistent gutters; on small screens, it becomes a single-column stack of cards that still preserve the image-over-text overlap pattern.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls are located directly beneath the gift results grid, within the same right-hand results column and aligned with the grid’s width. A generous 32–40px vertical gap separates the last row of product cards from the pagination bar to prevent visual clutter. The controls themselves (page numbers, previous/next arrows) are horizontally centered within the results column, forming a compact strip that does not visually overpower the grid. They sit on the dark background with gold accents highlighting the current page and interactive elements, maintaining consistency with the nav’s accent color. The top and bottom padding around the pagination elements is about 12–16px, and additional 40–48px spacing beneath the pagination separates it from any footer content, preserving the open, breathable feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element at the bottom of the results area that scrolls into view as users reach the end of the grid. On small screens, controls remain centered and may become larger touch targets with slightly increased vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state synchronizer is a non-visual behavioral layer associated with the page controller and interactive controls (occasion selector, filter form, sort control, pagination). It does not occupy any visible space on the canvas and does not alter the visual layout; instead, it operates behind the scenes, ensuring that filter selections, chosen occasion, sort order, and current page are reflected in the URL and restored on load or navigation. Conceptually, it is attached at the page level, affecting how the visible components respond but not where they are placed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: No visual footprint; logically positioned at the page-controller level. Its behavior is global and persistent during navigation, but it does not influence spatial layout or scrolling.

name: Store Pickup
title: Store Pickup
description: Location-based page where users select a store by ZIP code and browse products that are eligible for pickup at that store, with filters for category, price, rating, and same-day pickup availability.

core_features: Load and display the current store pickup context (selected ZIP and store) using getStorePickupContext.
Allow users to search for nearby store locations that support pickup by entering a ZIP code, using searchStoreLocationsByZip.
Allow users to select a store from the returned list and save it as the active pickup store using selectStorePickupLocation.
Retrieve available filter options (categories, rating thresholds, suggested price range) for pickup products using getStorePickupFilterOptions.
List pickup-eligible products for the selected store, applying URL-driven filters (category, max price, minimum rating, same-day only) and sorting using getStorePickupProducts.
Support pagination of pickup-eligible products using getStorePickupProducts with page and page_size.
Highlight products that are eligible for same-day pickup, based on is_same_day_pickup_eligible in getStorePickupProducts results.
Enable navigation from a pickup-eligible product in the list to the Product Detail page for configuration via product.html?id={product_id}.
user_workflows: Workflow 1: First-time visitor selects a store and views pickup products: 1) User navigates to store_pickup.html (no URL parameters). 2) Page calls getStorePickupContext to check if a ZIP and store are already selected. 3) If no store is selected, the page shows an empty state with a ZIP input form. 4) User enters a ZIP code in the ZIP search form and submits. 5) The page reloads or updates URL to store_pickup.html?zip={enteredZip}. 6) The page calls searchStoreLocationsByZip with the ZIP from the URL and shows matching store locations. 7) User clicks a 'Select store for pickup' action on one of the locations. 8) The page calls selectStorePickupLocation with the chosen storeLocationId and the current ZIP. 9) On success, getStorePickupContext is refreshed to reflect the selected store. 10) The page calls getStorePickupFilterOptions to build the filter UI. 11) The page calls getStorePickupProducts with default or URL-defined filters for the selected store and displays the product list. 12) User clicks a product in the list and is navigated to product.html?id={product_id} to configure and add for pickup at the selected store.

Workflow 2: Returning visitor with a stored store selection adjusts filters and sorting: 1) User navigates to store_pickup.html, possibly with existing URL parameters like zip, pickupCategoryId, price_max, rating_min, same_day_only, sort_by, page. 2) The page calls getStorePickupContext to get the current selected ZIP and store. 3) If a store is selected in the context, that store is shown as the active pickup store in the context bar. 4) The page calls getStorePickupFilterOptions and preselects filter controls based on the URL parameters. 5) The page builds a filters object from URL parameters (category, price_max, rating_min, same_day_only) and calls getStorePickupProducts with filters, sort_by, page, and page_size. 6) The product list is displayed with the current filters and sort applied. 7) User modifies filters or sort (e.g., selects a different category or toggles 'same-day only') in the filter panel. 8) On filter apply, the page updates the URL parameters (including zip from context if needed), resetting page to 1, and reloads or re-renders. 9) The page repeats the getStorePickupProducts call with the updated filters and sort_by. 10) User clicks any product to navigate to product.html?id={product_id}, where they can configure and add the item for pickup at the selected store.

Workflow 3: User changes ZIP and store: 1) While viewing pickup products for a currently selected store, user enters a different ZIP in the ZIP search form. 2) On submit, the URL is updated to store_pickup.html?zip={newZip} (existing filter parameters may be cleared or preserved as per application rules), and the page reloads or re-renders. 3) The page calls searchStoreLocationsByZip with the new ZIP and shows a list of stores for that ZIP. 4) The user selects a new store, triggering selectStorePickupLocation with the chosen storeLocationId and new ZIP. 5) On success, the context is updated via getStorePickupContext, and the page refreshes the product list by calling getStorePickupProducts using the same or reset filters from the current URL. 6) The product list now reflects pickup-eligible products for the newly selected store.

Workflow 4: User paginates through pickup products: 1) User is on store_pickup.html with a selected store and any combination of filters and sort_by, and a product list is visible. 2) The page shows pagination controls based on total_count, page, and page_size returned by getStorePickupProducts. 3) User clicks a pagination control (e.g., next page or a specific page index). 4) The page updates the page URL parameter while preserving zip, pickupCategoryId, price_max, rating_min, same_day_only, and sort_by. 5) The page calls getStorePickupProducts again with the same filters and sort_by, but with the updated page argument and the configured page_size. 6) The product grid updates to show the products for the new page while keeping the current store and filters.

Workflow 5: User focuses on same-day pickup products only: 1) User has a selected store displayed in the context bar and sees the filter panel. 2) User activates the same-day-only toggle in the filter panel. 3) On filter apply, the page updates the URL parameter same_day_only to a truthy value and resets page=1, preserving other filter parameters. 4) The page calls getStorePickupProducts with filters including same_day_only, sort_by from the URL, page=1, and page_size. 5) The product list is refreshed to include only products where is_same_day_pickup_eligible is true. 6) Products that are same-day eligible are additionally highlighted in each product card.
interactions: ZIP search form submission: User enters a ZIP code and submits the form; the page updates the zip URL parameter to the submitted value, navigates (or re-renders) to store_pickup.html with that parameter, and triggers searchStoreLocationsByZip(zip).

Store selection: In the store locations list, user clicks a 'Select for pickup' action on a store; the page calls selectStorePickupLocation(storeLocationId, selectedZip), then refreshes the pickup context via getStorePickupContext, and re-fetches pickup products via getStorePickupProducts if selection succeeds.

Filter changes: User adjusts filters in the filter panel (category selection, maximum price, minimum rating, same-day-only toggle); on applying filters, the page rewrites URL parameters (pickupCategoryId, price_max, rating_min, same_day_only, sort_by if changed, page=1) and calls getStorePickupProducts with filters derived from the new URL state.

Sorting changes: User changes a sort control (e.g., selects a different sort option); the page updates the sort_by parameter in the URL (and resets page=1), then calls getStorePickupProducts with the existing filters and new sort_by.

Pagination controls: User clicks pagination controls (next, previous, or a specific page number); the page updates the page URL parameter while preserving all other parameters and calls getStorePickupProducts with the same filters and sort_by but a different page.
Product selection: User clicks on a product card or a dedicated 'View details' link; the browser navigates to product.html?id={product_id} using the id from the getStorePickupProducts response.

Context bar actions: If the context bar includes a 'Change store' action, user clicks it to focus the ZIP search and store list section; the core behavior is to allow re-running searchStoreLocationsByZip with the current or a new ZIP and then re-running selectStorePickupLocation for a new store.

Initial load behavior: On page load, the application reads URL parameters, calls getStorePickupContext, optionally calls searchStoreLocationsByZip if zip is present, retrieves filter options with getStorePickupFilterOptions, and calls getStorePickupProducts only if a store is selected in the context.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state for this page is managed via URL parameters and the server-side StorePickupContext, not via local storage. URL parameters control which ZIP is being searched and which filters and sort order are applied to the product list. The StorePickupContext determines which store, if any, is considered 'selected' for pickup. On each page load or URL change, the application performs these steps: 1) Parse URL parameters: zip, pickupCategoryId, price_max, rating_min, same_day_only, sort_by, page. 2) Call getStorePickupContext to retrieve selected_zip and selected_store. 3) Determine the active ZIP for the store search area: if the URL contains zip, that value is used for displaying and searching store locations; otherwise, the selected_zip from context is used as the default in the ZIP input field, but no new search is triggered unless user submits. 4) If a zip parameter is present in the URL, searchStoreLocationsByZip(zip) is called to populate the store locations list; this is independent of whether a store is currently selected in the context. 5) The selected store for product listing always comes from getStorePickupContext.selected_store, not directly from URL parameters. Product listings are only requested when a selected_store exists. 6) The filter panel is initialized using getStorePickupFilterOptions; UI selections for category, price_max, rating_min, same_day_only, and sort_by are set based on the corresponding URL parameters. 7) When calling getStorePickupProducts, the page constructs a filters object from the URL and context (including mapping pickupCategoryId to a category_id filter, adding price_max and rating_min numeric thresholds, and interpreting same_day_only as a boolean flag). The active store is implied from the StorePickupContext and does not have to be included in the filters unless defined by the backend. The sort_by argument is taken from the URL or a default configuration if missing. The page argument is taken from the URL (defaulting to the first page if not provided), and page_size is taken from an application configuration constant. 8) When the user submits the ZIP search form, the application updates the zip URL parameter and navigates to store_pickup.html with the new ZIP, which triggers searchStoreLocationsByZip on the new value. It may keep or clear other filter parameters according to product requirements, but the selected store remains the one in the StorePickupContext until the user selects a different store or the backend clears it. 9) When the user selects a store from the store list, selectStorePickupLocation is called, updating the StorePickupContext (both selected_zip and selected_store). After a successful response, the application refreshes the context via getStorePickupContext and requests products via getStorePickupProducts with filters built from the current URL. 10) When filters, sorting, or pagination change, only the corresponding URL parameters are mutated; all other URL parameters and the StorePickupContext remain unchanged. Every such change triggers a new getStorePickupProducts call, keeping the state fully represented by URL + context.

components.functionality: Displays the currently selected store and ZIP from the StorePickupContext, indicates whether pickup and same-day pickup are supported at that store, and offers an entry point to change the store.
event_handlers: onLoad: call getStorePickupContext() to populate selected_zip and selected_store.

onChangeStoreClick: scroll or focus the ZIP search and store location list components to allow the user to choose a different store; no API call is triggered until the user searches or selects a store.

components.functionality: Allows the user to enter a ZIP code and initiate a search for nearby store locations that support pickup.
event_handlers: onInit: prefill ZIP input with the zip URL parameter if present; otherwise, if StorePickupContext.selected_zip exists, use it as the initial value without triggering a search.

onSubmit: prevent default form submission, construct a new URL to store_pickup.html with zip set to the entered ZIP (preserving or clearing other parameters as per rules), and navigate so that searchStoreLocationsByZip is triggered by the new URL state.

components.functionality: Displays the list of store locations that support pickup, returned by searchStoreLocationsByZip for the current ZIP, and lets the user select a store for pickup.
event_handlers: onZipParamChange: when the zip URL parameter changes or the page loads with a zip value, call searchStoreLocationsByZip(zip) and render the returned array of StoreLocation records.

onSelectStoreClick: when the user selects a store, call selectStorePickupLocation(storeLocationId, selectedZip), where selectedZip is taken from the current URL zip or, if absent, from StorePickupContext.selected_zip; on success, call getStorePickupContext() again to refresh context.

onSelectStoreSuccess: after refreshing context, trigger a refresh of the filter panel state and product list (getStorePickupFilterOptions and getStorePickupProducts) and optionally update the URL to ensure zip remains in sync with the new context.

components.functionality: Shows filter controls for category, maximum price, minimum rating, same-day-only toggle, and sort order for pickup-eligible products at the selected store. Applies filters by updating URL parameters and refreshing the product list.
event_handlers: onLoad: call getStorePickupFilterOptions() to load available categories, rating thresholds, and price range suggestions, then initialize filter UI components.
onInitFromUrl: read pickupCategoryId, price_max, rating_min, same_day_only, and sort_by from URL parameters and set the UI controls to match these values.
onFilterChange: capture user changes in category selection, price max input, rating selection, and same-day-only toggle in local component state (not persisted).
onSortChange: capture a new sort option selection and update local component state for sort_by.

onApplyFiltersClick: build a new URL for store_pickup.html containing current zip (from URL or StorePickupContext.selected_zip), pickupCategoryId, price_max, rating_min, same_day_only, and sort_by (if set); set page=1; navigate to the new URL so that getStorePickupProducts executes with the new filters.

onClearFiltersClick: construct a URL that removes pickupCategoryId, price_max, rating_min, same_day_only, and sort_by while preserving zip; set page=1; navigate to the new URL to reset the filters and product list.

components.functionality: Provides sorting options for the pickup product list (e.g., by relevance, price, rating, or other server-supported keys) and updates the sort_by URL parameter.
event_handlers: onInitFromUrl: read sort_by from the URL and select the corresponding option in the UI.

onSortOptionSelect: when user picks a new sort option, update the sort_by parameter in the URL (keeping zip and other filter parameters), reset page=1, and navigate; this triggers a new getStorePickupProducts call with the updated sort_by.

components.functionality: Displays pickup-eligible products for the currently selected store, including name, image, price or price range, category, rating, and an indicator if the product is same-day pickup eligible.
event_handlers: onLoadOrUrlChange: when the page loads or any relevant URL parameter (pickupCategoryId, price_max, rating_min, same_day_only, sort_by, page) changes, check getStorePickupContext for a selected_store; if present, build a filters object using current URL parameters and call getStorePickupProducts(filters, sort_by, page, page_size).

onProductsLoaded: render each product using fields from the getStorePickupProducts response (product_id, name, slug, image_url, base_price, min_price, max_price, category_name, rating_average, rating_count, is_same_day_pickup_eligible).
onSameDayEligibilityRender: for any product where is_same_day_pickup_eligible is true, render a non-promotional badge or text indicator such as 'Same-day pickup eligible' in the product card.
onProductClick: when the user clicks on a product card or its 'View details' control, navigate to product.html?id={product_id} using product_id from the product record.

onNoStoreSelected: if getStorePickupContext returns no selected_store, hide the product grid and show a functional message prompting the user to select a store via the ZIP search and store list components; no getStorePickupProducts call is made in this case.

onEmptyResults: if getStorePickupProducts returns total_count=0 for the current filters, show a message indicating that no pickup-eligible products match the filters, and allow user to adjust filters via the filter panel.

components.functionality: Displays pagination based on getStorePickupProducts response and enables navigation between pages of pickup-eligible products.
event_handlers: onProductsLoaded: read total_count, page, and page_size from the getStorePickupProducts response and compute how many pages are available; render appropriate pagination controls (e.g., previous, next, and page indexes).

onPageSelect: when the user selects a different page, update the page parameter in the URL while preserving zip, pickupCategoryId, price_max, rating_min, same_day_only, and sort_by, then navigate so that a new getStorePickupProducts call is triggered.
onFirstOrLastPageState: enable or disable previous/next controls based on the current page from the response.

components.functionality: Dedicated control (often within or alongside the filter panel) that lets users restrict products to only those eligible for same-day pickup.
event_handlers: onInitFromUrl: read same_day_only from URL and set the toggle to on or off accordingly.

onToggleChange: when the toggle is switched, update the same_day_only parameter in the URL (setting it to a truthy value when on or removing/setting it to falsy when off), reset page=1, preserve other parameters (zip, pickupCategoryId, price_max, rating_min, sort_by), and navigate to trigger a new getStorePickupProducts call.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading and orchestrates calls to the assigned interfaces based on the current URL parameters and context. Ensures the various components have the necessary data on first render and when URL changes.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: 1) Parse URL parameters (zip, pickupCategoryId, price_max, rating_min, same_day_only, sort_by, page). 2) Call getStorePickupContext() to retrieve context. 3) If zip exists in URL, call searchStoreLocationsByZip(zip) to populate store-location-results-list. 4) Call getStorePickupFilterOptions() to initialize the pickup-filter-panel. 5) If getStorePickupContext.selected_store exists, build filters from URL and call getStorePickupProducts(filters, sort_by, page, page_size) to populate pickup-product-grid and pickup-pagination-control.

onUrlChange: when navigation within the same page modifies URL parameters (e.g., filters or pagination), re-run the necessary calls: searchStoreLocationsByZip if zip changed, getStorePickupProducts if any of the product-related parameters changed, while reusing the existing StorePickupContext returned previously or by calling getStorePickupContext if necessary.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with three equal-width content columns (each roughly 4 columns wide) for the product cards. Content is centered within wide horizontal margins.
layout.layout_pattern: All components sit in a single, centered vertical stack beneath the existing full-width navigation bar. The page forms a tall, central content band over a full-bleed dark background: first a slim context bar, then the ZIP search, then store location results, followed by a compact filter/sort strip, and finally a 3-column product grid with pagination at the bottom. There are no sidebars; everything is organized in a linear top-to-bottom flow within a max-width container that mimics the three-card hero band from the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All components sit in a single, centered vertical stack beneath the existing full-width navigation bar. The page forms a tall, central content band over a full-bleed dark background: first a slim context bar, then the ZIP search, then store location results, followed by a compact filter/sort strip, and finally a 3-column product grid with pagination at the bottom. There are no sidebars; everything is organized in a linear top-to-bottom flow within a max-width container that mimics the three-card hero band from the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The central content band occupies roughly 70–80% of the viewport width, leaving generous dark margins left and right. Vertically, the ZIP/search + store selection block occupies a modest area at the top of the content band, while the product grid dominates the page height. Within each product card, approximately 65–70% of the card height is devoted to the product image, with a white text panel occupying the lower 30–35%, overlapping the image. Major vertical separations between sections (context bar → search → filters → grid → pagination) use 48–64px spacing; intra-section elements (within each bar or strip) use 16–24px spacing.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout is moderately spacious: each band (context, search, filters, grid, pagination) has clear breathing room above and below. The grid is limited to a 3-column layout to echo the mockup’s three tall image cards, avoiding dense catalog-like packing. Filter and sort controls are compact but well spaced, with enough padding around each control so nothing feels cramped. Cards have consistent gutters of about 24–32px horizontally and vertically within the grid.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow starts at the top-left of the content band with the Store Pickup context label, moves across the ZIP search area (which is visually emphasized and centered), then dips down to the row of available store locations. From there, the eye travels to the filter/sort strip (left-to-right: filters → same-day toggle → sort), and finally down into the primary content: the 3-column product grid, where attention flows left-to-right across the cards. The high-contrast white product panels over dark, photo-rich cards create a strong focal band similar to the mockup’s hero row.

layout.overall_description: Beneath the full-width dark navigation bar, the page resolves into a single, centered content band that echoes the mockup’s three-card hero section. A nearly-black background runs edge-to-edge, while the main content is constrained in an 70–80%-width container, aligned to the center and built on an implicit 12-column grid. At the top of this band, a slim context bar introduces Store Pickup, followed by a prominent, centered ZIP-code search form that visually anchors the upper portion of the page. Directly below, store location results appear as a neat row or short list of cards within the same central width. As the user scrolls slightly, a compact filter/sort strip spans the width of the content band, with filters on the left, a same-day pickup toggle near the middle-right, and sort controls on the far right, all aligned along a single horizontal baseline. The main visual weight sits in the large 3-column product grid beneath, whose tall cards mirror the mockup: rich, vertically oriented images with a white text panel overlapping the lower third. This grid dominates the middle of the page, framed by generous dark margins on the sides and comfortable vertical spacing between rows. At the bottom, a centered pagination control quietly closes the stack. The result is a vertically flowing, dark and craft-oriented page where the product imagery and overlapping white panels form a strong central band, while search, store selection, and filters sit above it as smaller, clearly separated layers.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Store Pickup context bar appears directly below the site’s full-width navigation, separated from it by about 32–48px of vertical space to keep the nav breathing room. It is a slim, full-width band anchored to the same central max-width container as the rest of the content. Within this container (roughly 70–80% of the viewport width), the context text (e.g., “Store Pickup”) is left-aligned, starting close to the left edge of the content column, echoing the nav’s left anchor. The bar itself is not tall—about 48px in height—with 12–16px of vertical padding, giving it a compact but visible presence. Any secondary text, such as a brief explanation, sits on the same line or just beneath as a smaller, muted line, still aligned flush left and ending well before the right edge to maintain airiness. Dark background and gold accent text or a thin gold underline reinforce its role as a subtle but clear page context indicator.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the normal document flow, spanning the central content width and shifting responsively with the page, but not sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The ZIP search form sits immediately below the context bar, separated by about 24–32px of vertical space. It is horizontally centered within the same max-width content container and spans about 60–70% of that container’s width, creating a strong, hero-like focal element near the top of the page. The ZIP input field is wide and prominent, positioned in the center, with a call-to-action button (e.g., “Find Stores”) directly to its right, aligned on the same baseline. Together they form a single horizontal control, with ample horizontal padding inside each field (16–24px) and 8–16px of space between input and button. Above or just inside the left side of the input, a label or placeholder guides the user subtly without clutter. There is 16–24px of vertical breathing room below the form before the store results list begins, keeping the form visually distinct as the main entry point for this page section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and centered; remains in place during scroll. On very wide screens, it remains anchored to the center of the content band, maintaining generous side margins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The store location results list appears directly under the ZIP search form, with about 24–32px of vertical separation. It stays within the same central content column and typically spans the full width of that column. Each store is represented as a card-like row or tile; when there are three or fewer stores, they can appear as a single 3-column row of compact cards, echoing the mockup’s triple-card layout but at a reduced height. Each card uses a dark background with a subtle border or gold accent and has 16–20px of internal padding. The cards align to the same 12-column grid: for three stores, each card roughly occupies 4 grid columns; for more stores, they wrap into multiple rows while preserving consistent gutters of 24px horizontally and 24–32px vertically. Selection state (e.g., the currently chosen pickup store) is emphasized through a slightly brighter or gold-accented card, but all cards maintain the same footprint. The list ends with about 32–40px of vertical space before the filter/sort strip begins, clearly delineating store selection from product filtering.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static grid/list within the central column; no sticky behavior. Cards reflow responsively while maintaining consistent gutters and alignment with the main content grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pickup filter panel is positioned below the store location results list, separated by roughly 32px of vertical space, and aligned within the same central content width. It forms the left portion of a horizontal control strip that runs above the product grid. The panel’s controls—category dropdown, price range, rating, etc.—are arranged in a single row of pill-like or segmented controls starting from the left edge of the content column, reflecting the compact, organized nav-item style from the mockup. Each filter control has 12–16px horizontal padding and 8–12px vertical padding, with 8–12px gaps between them, keeping the row compact but comfortably spaced. The panel itself consumes approximately the left two-thirds of the control strip, leaving room on the right for the same-day toggle and sort control. A subtle bottom rule or shadow line can visually separate this filter strip from the product grid below. Vertically, there is only about 16–24px between this strip and the top of the product grid, keeping filters tightly associated with the products.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The entire filter/sort strip (including this panel) can be configured as sticky within the content area: once the user scrolls past the ZIP and store selection, the strip pins to the top of the viewport (just below the main nav) until the user scrolls above it, maintaining quick access to filters without leaving the main grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The same-day pickup filter toggle is integrated into the same horizontal strip as the pickup filter panel but positioned toward the right side of the content band, just to the left of the sort control. It appears as a compact toggle pill or switch with a concise label (e.g., “Same-day pickup”), aligned on the same baseline as the other filters. The toggle occupies a small footprint—roughly the width of a short label plus a switch—leaving equal or slightly more space between it and the nearest filter on its left than between individual filters, making it feel like a distinct, important option. When active, it uses the gold accent color to stand out against the dark background, similar to an active nav item, but its size remains modest to avoid overpowering the sort control. Vertically, it shares the same 8–12px vertical padding as neighboring controls, giving the whole strip a tidy, unified appearance.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: It moves and sticks together with the entire filter/sort strip. No independent floating; it simply maintains its relative position near the right side of the control row.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pickup sort control sits at the far right end of the same horizontal control strip as the filters and same-day toggle. It is right-aligned within the central content container, mirroring the way the cart icon or last nav item anchors the top navigation in the mockup. The control itself is a compact dropdown or segmented button labeled “Sort by,” with 12–16px of horizontal padding and 8–12px of vertical padding, giving it enough presence to be easily clickable while remaining visually balanced with the filters. There is a small gap (8–12px) between the sort control and the same-day toggle to visually group them as related, but the sort control aligns flush to the right edge of the usable content width. This right alignment creates a clear left-to-right scan: filters on the left, then same-day, then sort on the right, echoing the nav’s top sweep. The strip sits about 16–24px above the product grid, keeping the sort control closely associated with the products.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Shares the same sticky behavior as the filter panel when configured, pinning to the top of the viewport within the content area. Otherwise, it scrolls normally as part of the control strip.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pickup product grid is the main visual anchor of the page, located immediately below the filter/sort strip with about 16–24px of vertical separation. It spans the full width of the central content container, leaving generous dark margins on both sides. The grid is organized into three equal-width columns on desktop, each column corresponding to roughly 4 columns of an implicit 12-column grid, with 24–32px gutters between cards both horizontally and vertically. Each product card closely mimics the mockup’s hero cards: a tall, vertically oriented card where the top 65–70% is occupied by a product image, and the bottom 30–35% is a white (or very light) text panel that overlaps the lower part of the image. The image fills the width of the card with a slightly taller-than-wide aspect ratio, and the white text panel is inset with 16px padding on all sides, containing a bold, slightly italic product name at the top and a smaller descriptive paragraph or metadata below. The panel’s top edge slightly overlaps the bottom of the image, creating a layered, crafted feel. The dark page background frames these white panels and images, giving the grid a strong, high-contrast central band echoing the original 3-card layout. There is a generous 48–64px of vertical space between the last visible row of products and the pagination control below, keeping the grid visually dominant.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the central content column, expanding vertically as more products load. On narrower viewports, the grid can collapse to 2 or 1 column while maintaining the image-to-text height ratio and consistent card padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination control sits centered below the product grid, separated from the last row of product cards by about 48–64px of vertical space to clearly delineate the end of the grid. It occupies a relatively small vertical footprint (around 40–56px high) and is horizontally centered within the same content container as the grid. The pagination elements (previous/next arrows, page numbers, or a load-more button) are arranged in a single horizontal line, with equal spacing between items to maintain the clean, organized feel. The control does not extend to the edges; it sits comfortably within the central width with ample surrounding whitespace, making it easy to scan without drawing more attention than the product imagery above. A subtle top border or faint shadow can visually cap the product grid just above pagination if needed, but the main emphasis remains on the products.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the normal document flow; it scrolls into view after the product grid and does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pickup page initializer is a non-visual controller responsible for data loading and orchestrating interactions between ZIP search, store list, filters, and the product grid. It has no direct visual presence on the page and does not occupy any explicit space in the layout. Conceptually, it sits behind the scenes at the top of the page structure, ensuring that when the ZIP search runs, store results, filters, and products update in the central content band without altering the established spatial relationships.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-spatial; exists only in the logical/component hierarchy and does not affect layout directly.

name: Custom Projects & Consultation
title: Custom Projects & Consultation
description: This page lets users learn about complex custom project capabilities and request a design consultation by submitting an appointment form that includes project details, preferred format, and contact information.

core_features: Load selectable options for the consultation form (project types, consultation formats, preferred contact methods) using the getConsultationFormOptions interface.
Display a non-marketing informational section describing the types of custom projects handled (e.g., large wall art, signs, maps, fully custom designs), using only dynamic or configurable content.
Provide a prominent 'Schedule Design Consultation' call-to-action that, when activated, scrolls the page to the consultation form and may update the project_type_prefill URL parameter.

Render a consultation request form that lets users choose a project type, appointment date, appointment time, consultation format, project description, estimated budget, contact name, contact email, and preferred contact method.
Prefill the project type field in the form from the project_type_prefill URL parameter when present.
Submit the consultation request via the submitConsultationAppointment interface and handle the response to show success or error feedback.
After a successful request, display confirmation details (including any status and scheduled start data returned by the interface) and provide navigation back to the home page if desired.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Visit page from navigation and submit a new consultation request
1. User clicks the 'Custom Projects' link in the site header or footer, navigating to custom_projects.html without URL parameters.
2. On page load, the consultation-options-loader component calls getConsultationFormOptions to retrieve project types, consultation formats, and preferred contact methods for the form.
3. The custom-projects-info component shows a dynamic explanation of custom capabilities.
4. The user clicks the 'Schedule Design Consultation' call-to-action.
5. The schedule-consultation-cta component triggers a scroll to the consultation-form component and may update the URL (e.g., via history APIs) with a project_type_prefill value matching the currently selected project type (if any context is available).
6. In the consultation-form component, the project type dropdown is populated with the project_types returned by getConsultationFormOptions. If project_type_prefill is present, that option is preselected.
7. The user selects an appointment date and time, a consultation format from the consultation_formats options, and a preferred contact method from the preferred_contact_methods options.
8. The user enters a project description (including dimensions, style needs, and budget constraints) and provides contact name, contact email, and estimated budget amount if desired.
9. The user submits the form, triggering submitConsultationAppointment with the bound form values.
10. If the response indicates success, the appointment-confirmation-panel displays confirmation details (including any appointment_id, status, and scheduled_start_iso) and optional next steps. The user may then click a link or button to navigate back to the home page (index.html) or continue browsing.
11. If the response indicates failure or validation errors are detected on the client, appropriate error messages are shown and the user can correct the form and resubmit.

Workflow 2: Arrive with a preselected project type from another page
1. On another page (e.g., a page describing wall art), the user clicks a 'Schedule Design Consultation' link that navigates to custom_projects.html with a project_type_prefill URL parameter.
2. On load, the page-state-manager reads the project_type_prefill parameter and exposes it to the consultation-form component.
3. The consultation-options-loader calls getConsultationFormOptions and, once options are loaded, the consultation-form attempts to match project_type_prefill to one of the returned project type values.
4. If a match is found, that project type is preselected in the form; otherwise the form leaves the project type unselected and may show a generic placeholder.
5. The user reviews the preselected project type, adjusts it if needed, fills in the remaining fields, and submits the form as in Workflow 1.
6. After successful submission, the appointment-confirmation-panel shows confirmation and navigation options.

Workflow 3: Change project type after initial selection and preserve it in URL state
1. The user lands on custom_projects.html, with or without a project_type_prefill parameter.
2. The consultation-form is rendered with project types from getConsultationFormOptions.
3. The user changes the project type field in the form (e.g., switches from one project type to another).
4. The consultation-form component optionally triggers a URL update (without full page reload) to set project_type_prefill to the newly selected project type value so that the state is shareable via the URL.
5. If the user refreshes the page or shares the link, the project_type_prefill parameter ensures that the same project type is preselected on load.
interactions: Click interaction on the 'Schedule Design Consultation' call-to-action: scrolls to the consultation form and may update the project_type_prefill URL parameter based on context.

Form field interactions in the consultation form: changes in project type, consultation format, preferred contact method, appointment date, appointment time, project description, estimated budget, contact name, and contact email update in-memory form state and may trigger validation feedback.

Form submission interaction: onSubmit on the consultation form sends the current form state to submitConsultationAppointment and manages loading and disabled states for the submit button during the request.

Response handling interaction: if submitConsultationAppointment returns success, the form is optionally hidden or locked and the appointment-confirmation-panel is shown; if it returns an error or does not indicate success, error messages are displayed and the form remains editable.
Navigation interaction after confirmation: a click on a 'Back to Home' or similar navigation element triggers a redirect to index.html without altering consultation data on this page.
page_functionality.state_logic: On initial load, the page-state-manager reads the project_type_prefill URL parameter (if present). No values are hardcoded; the parameter is treated as an opaque string and later compared only to the values received from getConsultationFormOptions.project_types. The consultation-options-loader then calls getConsultationFormOptions to obtain project_types, consultation_formats, and preferred_contact_methods. Once options are available, the consultation-form component uses the project_type_prefill value to attempt to preselect the corresponding project type option. If no matching option is found, the project type field remains unselected. When the user changes the project type, the consultation-form component can update the URL using browser navigation APIs to set or replace the project_type_prefill query parameter so that the current selection is represented in the URL without requiring a page reload. During form submission, the current in-memory form state is used to build the parameters for submitConsultationAppointment; no state is persisted via localStorage or other client storage. The response from submitConsultationAppointment (including success, appointment_id, status, scheduled_start_iso, and message) is stored in memory and used by the appointment-confirmation-panel to render confirmation or error states. Navigation away from the page (such as to index.html) is handled by standard link navigation, and any consultation-specific in-memory state is discarded upon leaving the page.

components.functionality: Coordinates page-level state by reading URL parameters (specifically project_type_prefill), exposing them to child components, and managing updates to the browser URL when the user changes relevant selections such as project type.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Reads the current URL, extracts the project_type_prefill query parameter (if present), and stores it in a shared state accessible to other components.

onProjectTypeChange: Receives the newly selected project type value from the consultation-form component and updates the URL query string with an updated project_type_prefill parameter without reloading the page.
onUrlChange: Optionally listens for browser navigation events to keep the shared project_type_prefill state in sync if the user navigates via the back/forward buttons.

components.functionality: Fetches selectable options for the consultation form (project types, consultation formats, preferred contact methods) using the getConsultationFormOptions interface and provides them to the consultation-form component.
event_handlers: onComponentInit: Calls getConsultationFormOptions with no parameters and stores the returned project_types, consultation_formats, and preferred_contact_methods in component state.
onOptionsLoaded: Notifies dependent components (such as consultation-form) that options are available so they can render dropdowns and apply project_type_prefill logic.
onOptionsLoadError: Handles any error from getConsultationFormOptions by exposing an error state that can be used by the consultation-form to disable inputs or show a retry option.

components.functionality: Displays descriptive information about the shop’s custom project capabilities, such as large wall art, carved maps, large signs, and other bespoke projects. The content is sourced dynamically or from configuration so that it does not conflict with interface data.
event_handlers: onLearnMoreClick: Optionally scrolls the page to the consultation-form component or the schedule-consultation-cta component to encourage starting a consultation request.

components.functionality: Provides a prominent action control labeled generically for scheduling a design consultation. When activated, it scrolls the viewport to the consultation-form component and may communicate a suggested project type to the page-state-manager.
event_handlers: onClickScheduleCTA: Triggers a smooth scroll to the consultation-form component anchor, optionally passes a selected project type value (if known) to the page-state-manager, and may request the page-state-manager to update the URL parameter project_type_prefill accordingly.

components.functionality: Renders and manages the custom project consultation request form, including fields for project type, appointment date, appointment time, consultation format, project description, estimated budget, contact name, contact email, and preferred contact method. It uses getConsultationFormOptions data and the project_type_prefill URL parameter (via page-state-manager) to prefill and validate the form, and submits the request with submitConsultationAppointment.
event_handlers: onInit: Receives project_types, consultation_formats, and preferred_contact_methods from consultation-options-loader and the project_type_prefill value from page-state-manager. If options are loaded and project_type_prefill matches one of the project_types values, sets the project_type form field to that value.
onChangeProjectType: Updates the local form state for project_type and notifies page-state-manager so it can update the project_type_prefill URL parameter.
onChangeAppointmentDate: Updates the local form state for appointment_date, which will later be passed to submitConsultationAppointment.
onChangeAppointmentTime: Updates the local form state for appointment_time, which will later be passed to submitConsultationAppointment.
onChangeConsultationFormat: Updates the local form state for consultation_format using the consultation_formats options from getConsultationFormOptions.
onChangeProjectDescription: Updates the local form state for project_description (including any user-entered dimensions, style requirements, and budget constraints).
onChangeEstimatedBudget: Updates the local form state for estimated_budget_amount as a numeric value if provided.
onChangeContactName: Updates the local form state for contact_name.
onChangeContactEmail: Updates the local form state for contact_email.
onChangePreferredContactMethod: Updates the local form state for preferred_contact_method based on the preferred_contact_methods options from getConsultationFormOptions.

onValidate: Runs client-side validation (e.g., required fields, basic format checks) without using any hardcoded enumerations beyond what is supplied by getConsultationFormOptions, and determines whether the form can be submitted.

onSubmit: If validation passes, calls submitConsultationAppointment with the current form state, mapping the project_type, appointment_date, appointment_time, consultation_format, project_description, estimated_budget_amount, contact_name, contact_email, and preferred_contact_method form fields to the corresponding interface parameters. During the request, sets a loading state and disables the submit action.

onSubmitSuccess: Receives the success, appointment_id, status, scheduled_start_iso, and message from submitConsultationAppointment. If success is true, it updates a shared or local state indicating that the appointment has been requested and passes the response data to the appointment-confirmation-panel. It may also lock the form fields or hide the form to prevent duplicate submissions.
onSubmitError: Handles non-success responses or network errors by setting error messages, re-enabling the submit action, and keeping form data intact so the user can correct and resubmit.

components.functionality: Displays the result of the consultation submission. When submitConsultationAppointment reports a successful request, it shows confirmation information such as the appointment identifier, status, scheduled start time, and any message returned by the interface. It also provides optional next-step guidance and navigation back to the home page.
event_handlers: onShowConfirmation: Listens for a successful submission event from consultation-form and renders the returned success, appointment_id, status, scheduled_start_iso, and message fields. It only becomes visible when a successful submission has occurred.
onShowError: If the submission result indicates failure or partial success, shows the message returned by submitConsultationAppointment in an error state while allowing the form to remain visible.
onClickBackToHome: Navigates the user to index.html (the home page) when the user chooses to leave the confirmation view, without persisting any additional client-side state.

onClickNewRequest: Optionally resets local consultation-related state (for example, clearing the form via consultation-form) to allow another consultation request, while respecting any current project_type_prefill parameter in the URL.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column grid with three equal-width content columns (each roughly 4 columns wide) for the product cards. Content is centered within wide horizontal margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom flow echoing the mockup: a full-width (global) dark navigation bar sits above this page. Directly under it, the Custom Projects & Consultation page begins with a hero-style information band (custom-projects-info) that visually mimics the three-card product row: three tall image-driven info cards in a single centered row. Beneath that row, still within the same central band, a prominent, centered schedule-consultation-cta button invites users to proceed. Scrolling down, the consultation-form occupies a large centered card-like section that spans most of the page width. After submission, the consultation-form is replaced in-place by the appointment-confirmation-panel, preserving the layout footprint. The page-state-manager and consultation-options-loader operate invisibly, orchestrating which primary panels are visible and what options the form shows, without affecting layout spacing.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom flow echoing the mockup: a full-width (global) dark navigation bar sits above this page. Directly under it, the Custom Projects & Consultation page begins with a hero-style information band (custom-projects-info) that visually mimics the three-card product row: three tall image-driven info cards in a single centered row. Beneath that row, still within the same central band, a prominent, centered schedule-consultation-cta button invites users to proceed. Scrolling down, the consultation-form occupies a large centered card-like section that spans most of the page width. After submission, the consultation-form is replaced in-place by the appointment-confirmation-panel, preserving the layout footprint. The page-state-manager and consultation-options-loader operate invisibly, orchestrating which primary panels are visible and what options the form shows, without affecting layout spacing.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Content is constrained to a max-width central column (roughly 70–80% of the viewport width) with generous dark margins left and right. Vertically, the hero info section with the three cards occupies the bulk of the first viewport (around 50–60% of initial view height), with large, tall imagery and overlapping white text panels that mirror the mockup. The CTA button is compact in height but given generous vertical breathing room. The form section below is tall and dominant, designed as a large card that feels substantial but not cramped. The confirmation panel inherits the same height and width behavior as the form. Internal component padding follows a 16–24px rhythm, and vertical gaps between major sections follow the 48–64px scale.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is moderate and mirrors the mockup: content is visually rich but airy. The three hero cards have generous internal padding and consistent gutters between them. The form fields are grouped with clear vertical separation between sections (about 24–32px), and horizontal spacing between columns maintains clarity. No element looks crowded—each primary section (hero, CTA, form/confirmation) reads as a distinct band separated by ample dark background and consistent vertical rhythm.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye follows a strong vertical-then-horizontal flow. Starting at the global nav (logo and nav items), the eye moves across to any highlighted nav item, then drops down into the hero info section. Within that band, attention runs left-to-right across the three tall cards and their overlapping white text panels. From there, the eye is drawn downward to the centered gold schedule-consultation-cta button. Activating or scrolling past the CTA leads directly into the consultation-form, where the focus flows top-to-bottom through headings, project details, format options, and contact fields. On submission, the appointment-confirmation-panel appears in the same central area, keeping the eye anchored in place while communicating next steps.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a nearly-black, full-bleed background with subtle texture, framed by wide dark margins on both sides. At the top, a global nav bar runs edge-to-edge, while the main consultation page content is visually contained within a centered band of about 70–80% of the viewport width. Immediately below the nav, a hero-style section presents three tall, equal-width cards in a single row. Each card shows a vertically oriented photo related to custom laser work, with a clean white text panel overlapping the lower third of the image—featuring a bold italic title and concise description of different custom project types or consultation scenarios. Under this three-card row, a single, gold-accented primary button sits centered, inviting visitors to schedule a consultation. Scrolling down, the visual focus shifts into a large, card-like consultation form, centered and spanning roughly 8 of 12 grid columns, with a subtle elevation or border that echoes the white panels in the hero. The form fields are arranged in a clear, two-column grid where appropriate, with generous spacing between groups. After the user submits, the form area transitions into a confirmation panel occupying the exact same central footprint, ensuring the page feels stable and cohesive. Invisible state and data components manage which of these major panels is visible, but the user’s visual experience remains a simple, vertical journey through hero, CTA, form, and confirmation, all framed by the same dark, craft-oriented aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-state-manager sits logically at the top of the page’s content hierarchy, just inside the main page container but above the visual sections. It does not render any visible UI and therefore does not occupy any perceivable space—no width, height, or padding is reserved for it. Instead, it controls which major content block is visible in the central content band: for example, it toggles between showing the consultation-form and the appointment-confirmation-panel within the same central section beneath the hero and CTA. From a layout perspective, it can be considered an invisible layer spanning the full width of the page but with zero height, positioned above the hero and form sections in the DOM order but not affecting the spatial flow of the design.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, non-interactive, and static. It does not scroll independently, nor is it fixed or sticky. It simply governs which visible components are mounted in the central content areas.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The consultation-options-loader is also non-visual. It is tied to the central content band, typically scoped to the area where the consultation-form will appear. It does not reserve any explicit space on the page. Instead, while it loads options (e.g., project types, preferred formats, available time slots), its state can trigger loading indicators within the form area itself. In the layout, imagine it as an invisible layer occupying the same horizontal span as the form (centered, roughly 70–80% of the viewport width), but with no inherent height. The space actually perceived by the user is defined by the form or placeholder skeletons that the loader enables.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, static, and fully integrated with the form area’s scroll behavior. It does not fix or float; it only influences what content the form section renders.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The custom-projects-info component forms the hero-like section directly beneath the global navigation bar. It spans the full width of the page background but constrains its content to a centered max-width band (approximately 1100–1200px, or about 70–80% of the viewport width), aligned to an implicit 12-column grid. Vertical padding of roughly 48–64px separates this section from the nav above and from the CTA/form below. Inside, three equal-width, tall cards are arranged in a single row. Each card occupies about 4 of the 12 grid columns, with consistent horizontal gutters of about 24–32px between them. The cards are vertically oriented: the upper 65–70% of each card is a full-bleed image, while the lower 30–35% features a crisp white panel that overlaps the bottom of the image, echoing the mockup pattern. These white panels are centered within each card, with 16–24px internal padding, a bold italic heading at the top, and a smaller descriptive paragraph beneath. The cards share the same height so that their top and bottom edges align, creating a unified horizontal band. On the dark background, the trio of cards is horizontally centered, leaving generous dark side margins. On smaller screens (e.g., under ~768px), the three cards stack vertically, each becoming nearly full-width within the central content area, with 24–32px vertical spacing between them while preserving the image-plus-overlapping-panel structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section that scrolls with the page. It is not fixed or sticky; it simply occupies the hero region at the top of the content and yields to the CTA and form sections as the user scrolls down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The schedule-consultation-cta is positioned immediately below the custom-projects-info hero cards, within the same central width alignment. After a vertical gap of about 24–32px beneath the bottom edge of the cards, a single prominent button appears centered horizontally on the page. The button spans approximately 200–280px in width (or auto-width to content, capped around that range) and has generous horizontal padding (16–24px) and vertical padding (12–16px), following the mockup’s comfortable density. It uses the signature gold accent on a dark background to stand out. The button is visually framed by the dark negative space: there is roughly 24–32px of clear space above it (separating it from the card row) and about 40–56px below it before the form section begins, ensuring it reads as a distinct call-to-action band. On mobile, the button still appears centered within the viewport’s safe area, potentially stretching slightly wider but keeping clear margins from the screen edges (at least 16–20px).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and inline with the scroll flow. It is not sticky or fixed; instead, it serves as a transitional focal point between the hero info section and the form below. Clicking it may scroll the page to the consultation-form but does not change its spatial position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The consultation-form occupies a dedicated section directly beneath the CTA, forming a large, centered card-like panel that mirrors the structured cleanliness of the hero’s white text panels. After a vertical gap of roughly 40–56px below the CTA, a wide form card appears within the central content band. This card spans about 8–9 of the 12 grid columns, leaving balanced dark margins on both sides; on wide desktops this feels like 60–70% of the viewport width. The card’s background is either white or a very light neutral, creating strong contrast against the dark page background, with subtle shadow or border to suggest elevation. Internal padding around the form is generous (24–32px on all sides). At the top of the card, a brief heading and one-sentence intro align left, establishing the section. Below, the form fields are arranged in a clear vertical hierarchy, using a two-column grid where appropriate. For example, name and email might share a row (each spanning 6 of 12 internal columns), while large text areas like project description span the full internal width. Field groups (project details, format preferences, contact info) are separated by vertical spacing of about 24–32px to prevent crowding. The primary submit button is aligned either left or centered at the bottom of the card, with 24px padding above it and at least 32–40px of space below before the card’s bottom edge. The entire form section is visually aligned with the hero cards and CTA above, creating a continuous central column. On mobile, the form card becomes nearly full-width within the safe area, with all fields stacking as a single column and consistent 16–24px vertical spacing between inputs.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section that scrolls with the page. It occupies a fixed position in the document flow beneath the hero and CTA. When the page-state-manager switches to the confirmation state, this section is replaced in-place by the appointment-confirmation-panel, preserving the same spatial footprint.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The appointment-confirmation-panel appears in exactly the same spatial slot as the consultation-form, inheriting its central alignment and approximate dimensions so the page does not visually jump. Once the form is successfully submitted, this panel replaces the form card in-place within the central content band. Like the form, it is a card-like panel centered on the page, spanning about 8–9 of the 12 grid columns (roughly 60–70% viewport width) with a light or white background against the dark outer page. Internal padding is similarly generous (24–32px). At the top-left of the panel, a clear success heading (e.g., “Consultation Scheduled” or “Request Received”) is displayed in a larger type size, possibly accompanied by a subtle icon. Below, a concise paragraph confirms receipt and summarizes next steps. Key appointment or request details (such as chosen project type, preferred format, and contact email) are laid out in a simple, structured block, potentially using a two-column arrangement on desktop (labels in the left column, values on the right) and a single stacked column on mobile. A secondary button or link (e.g., “Back to Custom Projects”) may be placed at the bottom-left or centered, with 24px spacing above it and enough breathing room to the card edge. The visual proportions and spacing echo the form card so that the transition feels smooth and cohesive.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and occupies the same position in the scroll flow as the consultation-form. It is conditionally rendered by the page-state-manager, replacing the form without changing the surrounding layout. It does not float or stick; it scrolls naturally with the rest of the page content.

name: About
title: About
description: The About page presents dynamic background information about the engraving shop’s story, values, materials, techniques, and quality standards, and routes users toward contacting the shop or exploring custom projects.

core_features: Display the shop’s story and values as editable content managed outside this page (e.g., via a CMS), without hardcoding copy in the page logic.
Explain materials, techniques, and equipment using dynamic information derived from existing products (e.g., listing currently used materials and style themes by scanning Product records).
Summarize quality and reliability signals by aggregating rating data from Product entities (e.g., overall average rating, rating counts).
Highlight relevance for key occasions (such as weddings and corporate gifts) by using Product.occasions and Product.category_id values to surface which use-cases the shop commonly serves.
Provide clear navigation to contact.html for inquiries and to custom_projects.html for users interested in more complex custom work.
Support direct navigation to specific subsections of the About page (story, materials, occasions) via URL parameters, enabling deep links from other pages or external sources.
user_workflows:
Learn about the shop and then contact the team:
1. User opens about.html (from footer navigation or an external link).
2. The page loads and renders the story and values content in the Story section.
3. User scrolls or uses the internal section navigation to read about materials, techniques, and quality standards.
4. User clicks the 'Contact Us' link or button in a CTA panel.
5. Browser navigates to contact.html, optionally appending a URL parameter such as contact.html?source=about to preserve context.

Evaluate materials, techniques, and product quality:
1. User opens about.html.
2. The Materials & Techniques component fetches Product entities and derives a set of distinct materials and style themes to describe what the shop works with.
3. The Reliability & Quality component aggregates Product.rating_average and Product.rating_count values to compute summary metrics (e.g., overall average rating and total rating count) without hardcoded numbers.
4. User reads dynamic descriptions and metrics to gain confidence in the shop’s craftsmanship and reliability.
5. If interested in a project, user clicks a 'Contact Us' or 'Learn about custom projects' CTA to continue.

Arrive from a specific context (e.g., weddings or corporate gifts) and jump to the relevant section:
1. User follows a link from another page or an external source pointing to about.html with a section URL parameter (e.g., about.html?section=occasions).
2. On load, the About page reads the section parameter.
3. The page scrolls/focuses to the relevant section (for example, the occasions/reliability block) and emphasizes it.
